topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: The amplitude of an oscillating simple pendulum is $10\,cm$ and its time period is $4\,s.$ Its speed after $1 s$, when it passes through its equilibrium position is
explanation:
Given, $A = 10\,cm = 0.1\,m,\,\,T = 4\,s,\,\,t = 1\,\,s.$
Speed of oscillating simple pendulum is given by
$v = \frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = v = A\omega \,\,\cos \,\,\omega t$
$({\text{as}}\,\,y = A\,\,\sin \,\,\omega t)$
$ = A \times \frac{{2\pi }}{T}\cos \left( {\frac{{2\pi }}{T}t} \right)$
$ = \frac{{2\pi \times 0.1}}{4}\,\,\cos \,\,\left( {\frac{{2\pi \times 1}}{4}} \right)$
$ = \frac{{2\pi \times 0.1}}{4}\,\,\cos \frac{\pi }{2} = 0$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Velocity and Acceleration in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A pendulum of length $1 m$ is released from $\theta=60^{\circ}$. The rate of change of speed of the bob at $\theta=30^{\circ}$ is (Take, $g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$ )
explanation:
For a simple pendulum, time period,
$\begin{align*}& T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{2 \pi}{T}=\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{l}} \\& \therefore \quad \omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{l}}, \quad \omega^{2}=\dfrac{g}{l} \tag{1}\end{align*}$
Amplitude when angular displacement is $60^{\circ}$
$=\dfrac{2 \pi l}{360^{\circ}} \times 60^{\circ}=\dfrac{2 \pi l}{6}$
Therefore, displacement when angular displacement is $30^{\circ}$
$\begin{equation*}=\dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{2 \pi l}{6}\right), \quad y=\dfrac{\pi l}{6} \tag{2}\end{equation*}$
Acceleration, $a=-\omega^{2} y$
Using Eqs. (1) and (2), we get
$a = - \frac{g}{l} \times \frac{{\pi l}}{6} = - \frac{{10 \times 3.14}}{6}$
$ = - 5.2\,m{s^{ - 2}} \simeq 5\,m{s^{ - 2}}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: The time period of a simple pendulum in a stationary van is $T$. The time period of a mass attached to a spring is also $T$. The van accelerates at the rate $5\,m{s^{ - 2}}.$ If the new time periods of the pendulum and spring be $T_{p}$ and   $T_{s}$ respectively, then
explanation:
Time period of simple pendulum placed in a van accelerating at the rate of a $m{s^{ - 2}}$ is given by https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956154/preptracker/questions/q_40765_explanation_1764956154095.png $T=2 \pi\left[\dfrac{l}{\sqrt{g^{2}+a^{2}}}\right]^{\frac{1}{2}}$
However, the time period of mass attached to the spring is
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{m}{k}\right)}$
It is independent of $g$ as well as $a$. Hence, when the van accelerates, the time period of the simple pendulum decreases and that of spring remains unchanged.
Hence, $T_{p} < T$ and $T_{s}=T$
i.e. $\quad T_{p} < T_{s}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: Maximum time period of any simple pendulum on the earth is
explanation: The maximum time period of any pendulum on the earth is $1.5 \mathrm{~min}$.
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: As shown in figure, a simple harmonic motion oscillator having identical four springs has time period
explanation:
As equivalent spring constant when joined in series,
$k_{s}=\dfrac{k \times k}{k+k}=\dfrac{k}{2}$
Now, the springs are in parallel this equivalent.
Spring constant, $k_{p}=\dfrac{k}{2}+\dfrac{k}{2}=k$
Thus, overall $k$ does not change and time period will remain same as
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{m / k}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Oscillation of a Body Due to Combination of Springs

question: An iron ball of mass $M$ is hanged from the ceiling by a spring with a spring constant $k$. It executes a $S H M$ with a period $P$. If the mass of the ball is increased by four times, the new period will be
explanation:
The time period of the iron ball would be given by,
$P=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{K}}$
So, if mass of the ball is increased to 4 times of its initial mass, then the new period becomes
$P^{\prime}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{4 M}{K}}=2 P$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: If pendulum bob on a $2 m$ string is displaced $60^{\circ}$ from the vertical and then released. What is the speed of the bob as it passes through the lowest point in its path?
explanation:
Velocity of bob, $v=\sqrt{2 g l(1-\cos \theta)}$
[using ${v^2} = {u^2} + 2gh$]
$v=\sqrt{2 \times 9.8 \times 2 \times\left(1-\cos 60^{\circ}\right)}$
$v = \sqrt {2 \times 9.8} \,\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950087/preptracker/questions/q_40745_explanation_1764950086745.png
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A pendulum is made to hang from the ceiling of an elevator. It has period of $T s$ (for small angles). The elevator is made to accelerate upwards with $10\,m/{s^2}.$ The period of the pendulum now will be (Take, $g = 10\,m/{s^2}$ )
explanation:
When the elevator is at rest, its time period is given by
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{10}}$
When the elevator accelerates upwards, its time period becomes
$T^{\prime}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g+a}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{10+10}}$
$[\because T-m g=m a \Rightarrow T=m(g+a)]$
$=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{20}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{10}} \times \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=\dfrac{T}{\sqrt{2}}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A clock $S$ is based on oscillation of a spring and a clock $P$ is based on pendulum motion. Both clocks run at the same rate on the earth. On a planet having the same density as earth but twice the radius
explanation:
Acceleration due to gravity,
$g=\dfrac{G M}{R^{2}}=\dfrac{G \times \dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^{3} \rho}{R^{2}}=\dfrac{4}{3} \pi G \rho R$
$\Rightarrow g \propto R$
For pendulum clock, $g$ will increase on the planet so time period will decrease. But for spring clock, it will not change. Hence, $P$ will run faster than $S$

options.text: both will run at the same rate as on the earth
both will run at the same rate which will be different from that on the earth

subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: The maximum displacement of the particle executing $S H M$ is $1 \mathrm{~cm}$ and the maximum acceleration is $(1.57)^{2} \mathrm{~cm} \mathrm{~s}^{-2}$. Its time period is
explanation:
Maximum acceleration $=A \omega^{2}$
$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2}\right)^{2}=1\left(\dfrac{2 \pi}{T}\right)^{2}\left(\because 1.57=\dfrac{\pi}{2}\right) \\& \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{\pi^{2}}{4}=\dfrac{4 \pi^{2}}{T^{2}} \\& \Rightarrow \quad T^{2}=\dfrac{4 \times 4 \pi^{2}}{\pi^{2}} \\& \Rightarrow \quad T^{2}=16 \\& \therefore \quad T=4 s\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Velocity and Acceleration in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: The motion of a particle executing $S H M$ in one dimension is described by $x=-0.3 \sin \left(t+\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right)$, where $x$ is in metre and $t$ in second. The frequency of oscillation in $\mathrm{Hz}$ is
explanation:
We have, $x=-0.3 \sin \left(t+\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right)$
Comparing with the general equation
$x=x_{0} \sin (\omega t+\phi)$
where, $x_{0}=$ maximum displacement
So, $\quad x_{0}=0.3, \quad \omega=1, \quad \phi=\dfrac{\pi}{4}$
Hence, $2 \pi f=1 \Rightarrow f=1 / 2 \pi$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A body is executing $S H M$ when its displacement from the mean position are $4\,cm$ and $5\,cm,$  it has velocity $10{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} cm\,{s^{ - 1}}$ and $8{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} cm\,{s^{ - 1}},$ respectively. Its periodic time $t$ is
explanation:
For a body executing $S H M$, velocity,
$v=\sqrt{\omega^{2}\left(A^{2}-y^{2}\right)}$
We have,
$10^{2}=\omega^{2}\left(A^{2}-4^{2}\right)$
and $8^{2}=\omega^{2}\left(A^{2}-5^{2}\right)$
So, $10^{2}-8^{2}=\omega^{2}\left(5^{2}-4^{2}\right)=(3 \omega)^{2}$
$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow \quad 6=3 \omega \\& \Rightarrow \quad \omega=2\end{aligned}$
Since, time period, $t=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\omega}$
$\therefore \quad t=\dfrac{2 \pi}{2}=\pi s$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Velocity and Acceleration in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: $Y=5 \sin \dfrac{\pi}{2}(100 t-2 x)$, what is time period?
explanation:
Given that,
$\begin{equation*}Y=5 \sin (50 \pi t-\pi x) \tag{1}\end{equation*}$
Equation of displacement,
$\begin{equation*}Y=A \sin (\omega t-k x) \tag{2}\end{equation*}$
After comparing with Eqs. (1) and (2),
$\omega=50 \pi$
$\because$ Time period, $T = \frac{{2\pi }}{\omega } = \frac{{2\,\pi }}{{50\,\pi }} = 0.04\,s.$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A body executing $S H M$ has velocity $10\,cm\,{s^{ - 1}}$ and $7cm\,{s^{ - 1}},$ when its displacements from the mean position are $3\,cm$ and $4\,cm$ respectively. The length of path is
explanation:
The velocity ( $v$ ) of a body in SHM changes with displacement $(y)$ as follows
where, $A$ is amplitude and $\omega$ is angular speed. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950088/preptracker/questions/q_40713_explanation_1764950088353.png Given, ${v_1} = 10\,cm\,{s^{ - 1}},\,\,{v_2} = 7\,cm\,{s^{ - 1}}$
${y_1} = 3\,cm$ and ${y_2} = 4\,cm.$
$\therefore \dfrac{v_{2}}{v_{1}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{A^{2}-y_{2}^{2}}{A^{2}-y_{1}^{2}}}\left[\because v=\omega \sqrt{A^{2}-y^{2}}\right]$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{7}{10}=\sqrt{\dfrac{A^{2}-16}{A^{2}-9}}$
$ \Rightarrow A = 4.77\,cm$
Length of path, $2A = 2 \times 4.77 \approx 9.5\,cm$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Velocity and Acceleration in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: If a particle executes $S H M$ with angular velocity  $3.5{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} rad{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {s^{ - 1}}$  and maximum acceleration  $7.5\,m{s^{ - 2}},$  then the amplitude of oscillation will be
explanation:
The maximum acceleration for a particle executing SHM is
$a_{\max }=\omega^{2} x$, where $x=$ maximum displacement from mean path
$\therefore x = \frac{{{a_{\max }}}}{{{\omega ^2}}} = \frac{{7.5}}{{{{(3.5)}^2}}} = 0.61\,\,m$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Velocity and Acceleration in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: Which one of the following statements is true for the speed $v$ and the acceleration $a$ of a particle executing simple harmonic motion?
explanation:
In simple harmonic motion, the displacement equation is,
$y=A \sin \omega t$
where, $A$ is amplitude of the motion.
Velocity, $v=\dfrac{d y}{d t}=A \omega \cos \omega t$
$\begin{equation*}\Rightarrow \quad v=A \omega \sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \omega t} \tag{1}\end{equation*}$
Acceleration,$v = \omega \sqrt {{A^2} - {y^2}}$
$a=\dfrac{d v}{d t}=\dfrac{d}{d t}(A \omega \cos \omega t)$
$\Rightarrow \quad a=-A \omega^{2} \sin \omega t$
$\begin{equation*}a=-\omega^{2} y \tag{2}\end{equation*}$
When $\quad y=0, v=A \omega=v_{\text {max }}$
$\Rightarrow \quad a=0=a_{\text {min }}$
When $\quad y=A, v=0=v_{\text {min }}$$\Rightarrow \quad a=-\omega^{2} A=a_{\max }$
Hence, it is clear that when $v$ is maximum, then $a$ is minimum, i.e. zero or vice-versa.

options.text: Value of $a$ is zero, whatever may be the value of $v$

subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Velocity and Acceleration in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A particle is executing $S H M$. Then, the graph of velocity as a function of displacement is a/an
explanation:
From the relation, $v^{2}=\omega^{2}\left(a^{2}-x^{2}\right)$
$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow \quad v^{2}+\omega^{2} x^{2}=\omega^{2} a^{2} \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{v^{2}}{a^{2} \omega^{2}}+\dfrac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}=1\end{aligned}$
which is the equation of an ellipse.
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Velocity and Acceleration in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A simple performs $S H M$ about $n=0$ with an amplitude a and time period $T$. What is the speed of the pendulum at $x=A / 2$ ?
explanation:
Velocity of oscillating body,
$\begin{aligned}v & =\omega \sqrt{A^{2}-x^{2}} \\\text { At } x=\dfrac{A}{2}, v & =\omega \sqrt{A^{2}-\left(\dfrac{A^{2}}{4}\right)} \\& =\dfrac{\sqrt{3} A \omega}{2}=\dfrac{\sqrt{3} A \pi}{T}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: The length of the second pendulum is decreased by $0.3\,cm$ when it is shifted to Chennai from London. If the acceleration due to gravity at London is $981\,\,cm/{s^2},$ the acceleration due to gravity at Chennai is (assume $\pi^{2}=10$ )
explanation:
As, $L_{1}=\dfrac{g_{1} T^{2}}{4 \pi^{2}}=\dfrac{g_{1}}{\pi^{2}}\left[\because T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}}\right]$
and $\quad L_{2}=\dfrac{g_{2} T^{2}}{4 \pi^{2}}=\dfrac{g_{2}}{\pi^{2}}$
Since, length is decreased, $g_{2}$ is less than $g_{1}$.
$\therefore \quad L_{1}-L_{2}=\dfrac{g_{1}-g_{2}}{\pi^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad\left(L_{1}-L_{2}\right) \pi^{2}=g_{1}-g_{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 0.3 \times 10=g_{1}-g_{2}$
$ \Rightarrow \quad {g_2} = 981 - 3 = 978\,cm/{s^2}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A body is executing simple harmonic motion with an angular frequency of  $2\,rad{s^{ - 1}}.$ The velocity of the body at $20\,mm$ displacement, when the amplitude of the motion is $60\,mm$ is
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Velocity and Acceleration in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A particle doing $S H M$ having amplitude $5\,cm,$ mass $0.5\,kg$ and angular frequency $5\,rad/s$ is at $1\,cm$ from mean position. Find potential energy and kinetic energy
explanation:
Given, $m = 0.5\,kg,\,\,\omega = 5\,rad/s,$
$x = {10^{ - 2}}\,m$
$A = 5 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,m$
$\because$ Potential energy, $P E=\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2} x^{2}$
or $P E=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 0.5 \times(5)^{2} \times\left(10^{-2}\right)^{2}$
$ = \frac{{25}}{4} \times {10^{ - 4}}\,J = 6.25 \times {10^{ - 4}}\,J$
$\because$ Kinetic energy, $K E=\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2}\left(A^{2}-x^{2}\right)$
${\text{ or }}\,\,K = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.5 \times {(5)^2}(25 - 1) \times {10^{ - 4}}$
$ = 150 \times {10^{ - 4}}\,J$
subject: Physics
topic: Force, Energy and their relation in Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Force and Energy in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A particle executes $S H M$, its time period is $16 s$. If it passes through the centre of oscillation, then its velocity is $2\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ at time $2 s$. The amplitude will be
explanation:
Given, $v = 2\,m/s,\,\,T = 16\,s$ and $t = 2\,s$
As the velocity, $\quad v=A \omega \cos \omega t$
$ \Rightarrow \quad 2 = A \cdot \frac{{2\pi }}{{16}} \cdot \cos \frac{{2\pi }}{{16}} \cdot \,\,\,2\left[ {\because \omega = \frac{{2\pi }}{T}} \right]$
$ \Rightarrow \quad A = \frac{{16\sqrt 2 }}{\pi } = 7.2\,\,m$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Velocity and Acceleration in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: The amplitude of a particle executing $S H M$ is $4\,cm.$  At the mean position, the speed of the particle is $16\,cm/s.$ The distance of the particle from the mean position at which the speed of the particle becomes $8\sqrt 3 \,cm/s$ will be
explanation:
At mean position, velocity is
${\text{maximum}}\,\,{v_{\max }} = \omega \,a$
$ \Rightarrow \quad \omega = \frac{{{v_{\max }}}}{a} = \frac{{16}}{4} = 4$
$v = \omega \,\sqrt {{a^2} - {y^2}} $
$ \Rightarrow \quad 8\sqrt 3 = 4\sqrt {{4^2} - {y^2}} $
$192 = 16\left( {16 - {y^2}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow \quad 12 = 16 - {y^2}$
$ \Rightarrow \quad y = 2\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: The displacement of a particle along the $X$ - axis is given by $x=a \sin ^{2} \omega t$. The motion of the particle corresponds to
explanation:
For a $SHM,\frac{{{d^2}x}}{{d{t^2}}} = - {\omega ^2}x\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
According question,
$x=a \sin ^{2} \omega t$
Differentiating w.r.t $t$,
$\begin{aligned}\dfrac{d x}{d t} & =2 a \omega \sin \omega t \cos \omega t \\& =a \omega 2 \sin \omega t \cos \omega t \\& =a \omega \sin 2 \omega t\end{aligned}$
Again differentiating w.r.t $t$,
$\begin{gather*}\dfrac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}=2 a \omega^{2} \cos 2 \omega t \\\text { or } \quad \dfrac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}=2 a \omega^{2}\left(1-2 \sin ^{2} \omega t\right) \\\Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}=2 a \omega^{2}-4 a \omega^{2} \sin ^{2} \omega t \\=2 a \omega^{2}-4 \omega^{2} x \tag{2}\end{gather*}$
From Eqs. (1) and (2), the motion of particle is non- simple harmonic motion.
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Simple Harmonic Motion and Uniform Circular Motion

question: Four massless springs whose force constants are ${2 k, 2 k, k}$, and ${2 k}$, respectively are attached to a mass ${M}$, kept on a frictionless plane (as shown in figure). If the mass ${M}$ is displaced in the horizontal direction, then find the frequency of the system.
explanation:
Spring on the left of the block are in series, hence there equivalent is
$k_{1}=\dfrac{(2 k)(2 k)}{2 k+2 k}=k$
Springs on the right of the block are in parallel, hence there equivalent is
$k_{2}=k+2 k=3 k$
Now again both ${K_{1}}$ and ${K_{2}}$ are in parallel
$\therefore \quad k_{\text {eq. }}=k_{1}+k_{2}=k+3 k=4 k$
Hence, frequency is
$f=\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{k_{e q}}{M}}=\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{4 k}{M}}$
subject: Physics

question: If the mass of the bob in a simple pendulum is increased to thrice its original mass and its length is made half its original length, then the new time period of oscillation is ${\dfrac{x}{2}}$ times its original time period. Then the value of ${x}$ is:
explanation:
Time period of a pendulum is
${T^{\prime}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{\ell^{\prime}}{g}}}$ where ${\ell^{\prime}=\dfrac{\ell}{2}}$
${T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{\ell}{g}}}$
Given that ${T^{\prime}=\dfrac{x}{2} T}$
${2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{\ell}{2 g}}=\dfrac{x}{2} 2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{\ell}{g}}}$
${\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=\dfrac{x}{2} \Rightarrow x=\sqrt{2}}$

question: The maximum potential energy of a block executing simple harmonic motion is $25\,J.A$ is amplitude of oscillation. At $\dfrac{A}{2}$, the kinetic energy of the block is
explanation:
Potential energy, $P E=\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2} x^{2}$
$A$ is amplitude, ${(PE)_{\max }} = \frac{1}{2}m{\omega ^2}{A^2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Kinetic energy, $K E=\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2}\left(A^{2}-x^{2}\right)$
At $x=\dfrac{A}{2}, K E=\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2}\left(A^{2}-\dfrac{A^{2}}{4}\right)$
$K E=\dfrac{3}{4} \times \dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2} A^{2}$
$K E=\dfrac{3}{4} P E_{\max }$
(Given $P E=25 J$)
$K E=\dfrac{3}{4} \times 25=18.75 J$

question: A particle executes $S.H.M.$ of amplitude $A$ along $x$-axis. At $t=0$, the position of the particle is $x=\dfrac{A}{2}$ and it moves along positive $x$-axis. The displacement of particle in time $t$ is $x=A \sin (\omega t+\delta)$, then the value $\delta$ will be
explanation:
Equation of $S.H.M.$ is $x=A \sin (\omega t+\delta)$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950062/preptracker/questions/q_40625_explanation_1764950062069.png Phases diagram
At $t=0, x=A \sin \delta$ and $x=\dfrac{A}{2}$
So, $A / 2=A \sin \delta \Rightarrow \sin \delta=1 / 2$
$\delta=30^{\circ}=\dfrac{\pi}{6}$.

question: For a simple harmonic motion in a mass spring system shown, the surface is frictionless. When the mass of the block is $1\,kg$, the angular frequency is ${\omega _1}$. When the mass is $2\,kg$ the angular frequency is ${\omega _2}$. The ratio ${\omega _2}/{\omega _1}$ is
explanation:
As, for osicillations of a spring - mass system,
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{k}}$ and $T=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\omega}$
$\dfrac{2 \pi}{\omega}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{k}} ; \quad \omega^{2}=\dfrac{k}{m}$
or $\omega _1^2 = \frac{k}{m} = \frac{k}{1}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
and $\omega _2^2 = \frac{k}{{{m_2}}} = \frac{k}{2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Dividing equations (1) and (2)
$\therefore \dfrac{\omega_{2}^{2}}{\omega_{1}^{2}}=\dfrac{k / 2}{k / 1}=\dfrac{1}{2} ; \dfrac{\omega_{2}}{\omega_{1}}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$
$\left[ {{\rm{As}},{m_1} = 1\,kg,{m_2} = 2\,kg} \right]$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A particle executes simple harmonic motion between $x=-A$ and $x=+A$. If time taken by particle to go from $x=0$ to $\dfrac{A}{2}$ is $2 s$; then time taken by particle in going from $x=\dfrac{A}{2}$ to $A$ is
explanation:
The eqn of SHM, $x=A \sin \omega t$
Now, $t=2 s$, from $x=0$ to $A / 2$
So, $A / 2=A \sin \dfrac{2 \pi}{T} \times t$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\pi}{6}=\dfrac{2 \pi \times 2}{T} \quad T=24 s$
Now time taken from 0 to $x=A$ is
$t^{\prime}=\dfrac{T}{4}=\dfrac{24}{4} s=6 s$
So, time from $x=\dfrac{A}{2}$ to $x=A$ is ${t^{\prime \prime }} = 6 - 2 = 4\,s$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion

question: Assume that the earth is a solid sphere of uniform density and a tunnel is dug along its diameter throughout the earth. It is found that when a particle is released in this tunnel, it executes a simple harmonic motion. The mass of the particle is $100\,g$. The time period of the motion of the particle will be (approximately) (Take $g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$, radius of earth $ = 6400\,km$)
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956145/preptracker/questions/q_40573_explanation_1764956145360.png At point $\mathrm{P}$ inside the tunnel $g_{p}=g \dfrac{x}{R}$, where $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity on the surface of earth and $R$ is radius.
$\therefore$ For the particle,
$m \dfrac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}=-m g_{p}=-m g \dfrac{x}{R}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}=-\left(\dfrac{g}{R}\right) x=-\omega^{2} x$, where $\omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}}$
Therefore the motion is simple harmonic.
$\therefore T=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\omega}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}}$
$=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{64 \times 10^{5}}{10}} \sec =\dfrac{2 \pi \times 800}{60 \times 60} h r$
$ \Rightarrow T = 1\,hr \cdot 24\,\min $.
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A particle executes $S H M$ of amplitude $A$. The distance from the mean position when it's kinetic energy becomes equal to its potential energy is
explanation:
Let ' $y$ ' be the distance where kinetic energy equals to potential energy.
Kinetic energy of $SHM$ is
$\Rightarrow$ K.E. $=\dfrac{1}{2} k\left(A^{2}-y^{2}\right)$, where $K$ is a
constant.$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Potential energy of $SHM$ is
$ \Rightarrow P.E.{\rm{ }} = \frac{1}{2}k{y^2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
By equating (1) and (2)
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{1}{2} k\left(A^{2}-y^{2}\right)=\dfrac{1}{2} k y^{2} \\& \dfrac{1}{2} k A^{2}=k y^{2} \Rightarrow y=\dfrac{A}{\sqrt{2}}\end{aligned}$

question: Which graph represents the difference between total energy and potential energy of a particle executing \(S H M\) vs it's distance from mean position?
explanation:
Total energy of a particle executing $S H M$ is given by
$E=\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2} A^{2}$
Potential energy of a particle executing $S H M$ is given by
$U=\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2} x^{2}$
Here, $m=$ mass of the particle, $\omega=$ angular frequency $x=$ displacement from mean position, $A=$ amplitude
The difference
$\Delta E=E-U=\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2}\left(A^{2}-x^{2}\right)$
So, $\Delta E\,VS \cdot X$ represents a parabola with its vertex at point $\left(0, \dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2} A^{2}\right)$,
Thus correct graph is (1).
subject: Physics

question: A particle is executing simple harmonic motion $S H M$. The ratio of potential energy and kinetic energy of the particle when its displacement is half of its amplitude will be
explanation:
$P E=\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2} x^{2} ; K E=\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2}\left(A^{2}-x^{2}\right)$
At $x = \frac{A}{2};PE = \frac{1}{2}m{\omega ^2}\left( {\frac{{{A^2}}}{4}} \right) = \frac{1}{8}m{\omega ^2}{A^2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
$KE = \frac{1}{2}m{\omega ^2}\left( {{A^2} - \frac{{{A^2}}}{4}} \right) = \frac{3}{8}m{\omega ^2}{A^2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From equation (1) and (2), we get
$\dfrac{P \cdot E}{K E}=\dfrac{\dfrac{1}{8} m \omega^{2} A^{2}}{\dfrac{3}{8} m \omega^{2} A^{2}}=\dfrac{1}{3} ; \dfrac{P \cdot E}{K E}=\dfrac{1}{3}=1: 3$
subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: In simple harmonic motion the velocity is maximum when the acceleration is minimum.

Reason: Displacement and velocity of SHM differ in phase by $\dfrac{\pi}{2}$.
explanation:
$x=A \cos (\omega t+\phi)$
$v=(d x / d t)=-A \omega \sin (\omega t+\phi)$
$a=\dfrac{d v}{d t}=-A \omega^{2} \cos (\omega t+\phi)$
$\sin \dfrac{\pi}{2}=1$, and $\cos \dfrac{\pi}{2}=0$
$\Rightarrow$ When $v$ is maximum, $a$ is minimum. In simple harmonic motion, velocity is maximum when acceleration is minimum due to the phase relationship between displacement, velocity, and acceleration in SHM, which differ by a phase angle of $\dfrac{\pi}{2}$.
So correct option is (2).

question:
Assertion: For simple pendulum of vary large $(\rightarrow \infty)$ length, period is $84.6 \mathrm{~min}$.

Reason: The time period of a simple pendulum is proportional to directly the square root of length.
explanation:
When length of pendulum is too high, the direction of $g$ will not be vertically down.(rather, it will be towards centre of earth).
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{g\left(\dfrac{1}{l}+\dfrac{1}{R_{E}}\right)}}$
where $R_{E}=$ radius of earth.
As $l \rightarrow \infty,\left(\dfrac{1}{l}\right) \rightarrow 0 \Rightarrow T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{R_{E}}{g}}=84.6 \mathrm{~min}$
(Using values of $R_{E}, g$ )
Reason is about general formula $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}}$
$\Rightarrow T \propto \sqrt{l}$
So correct option is (2).

question:
Assertion: In simple harmonic motion, the motion is to and fro and periodic.

Reason: Velocity of the particle $(v)=\omega \sqrt{A^{2}-x^{2}}$ (where $x$ is the displacement and $A$ is amplitude)
explanation:
A particle is said to execute simple harmonic motion, if it moves to and fro about a fixed point under the action of a restoring force which is directly proportional to its displacement from the fixed point and is always directed towards the fixed point.
$x=A \cos (\omega t+\phi)$
$v=-A \omega \sin (\omega t+\phi)$
$\Rightarrow v=\omega \sqrt{A^{2}-x^{2}}$
So, correct option is (2).

explanation:
$F=k x$
After cutting, $x^{\prime}=\dfrac{x}{n}$.

$\Rightarrow k^{\prime}=n k$
But Assertion says $k^{\prime}=\dfrac{k}{n}$

Assertion is false
Also, material of spring plays role for value of $k$. So correct option is (4).

Reason: The total energy is directly proportional to the square amplitude of vibration of the harmonic oscillator.
explanation:

$\Rightarrow$ If $A^{\prime}=2 A, E^{\prime}=4 A^{2}$
The total energy is proportional to the square of the amplitude. Assertion is false Reason is true. So correct option is (4).

question:

Reason: If a pendulum clock is taken to mountain top, it will gain time.
explanation: The value of acceleration due to gravity is lower at the mountain top due to the increased distance from the Earth's center, causing the clock to loose time. So, correct option is (3).

question:
Assertion: If a hole were drilled through the centre of earth and ball dropped into the hole at one end, it will oscillate.

Reason: The ball will come out of other end if passed through centre hole in earth.
explanation: The ball undergoes Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM) within the hole, where the restoring force is proportional to its displacement, preventing it from exiting. At the other end of the hole, the ball's velocity becomes zero, and its acceleration is maximized, directed towards the center of the earth. So correct option is (3).

question:
Assertion: The frequency of a second pendulum in an elevator moving up with an acceleration half the acceleration due to gravity is $0.612\,{s^{ - 1}}$.

Reason: The frequency of a second pendulum does not depend upon acceleration due to gravity.
explanation:
$T=2 s, l \approx 1 m$ (without elevator)
$f=\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{l}}$
$f=\dfrac{1}{T}=\dfrac{1}{2}=0.5 \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$
When elevator accelerates up with acceleration $\left(\dfrac{g}{2}\right)$ we have $g^{\prime}=g-\left(-\dfrac{g}{2}\right)=\dfrac{3 g}{2}$
$f' = \frac{1}{{2\pi }}\sqrt {\frac{{g'}}{l}} $
$\Rightarrow f^{\prime}=\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{3}{2}}\right) \times 0.5=0.612 s^{-1}$
The frequency of a second pendulum depends on the acceleration due to gravity. If the elevator is moving with an acceleration, it will affect the apparent gravity experienced by the pendulum, and thus, the frequency. The Reason is false. So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question:
Assertion: When a girl sitting on a swing stands up, the periodic time of the swing will increase.

Reason: In standing position of girl, the length of the swing will increase.
explanation:
When a girl stands up on a swing, the position of the center of gravity of the girl is elevated. This results in a decrease in the effective length ( $l$ ) of the pendulum, leading to a decrease in the time period of the swing, as $T \propto \sqrt{l}$.
(Since $l=$ length from point of suspension to centre of gravity)
So correct option is (4).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: Simple harmonic motion is not a uniformly accelerated motion.

Reason: Velocity is non-uniform in SHM.
explanation:
In simple harmonic motion (SHM), the acceleration (a) varies with position (y), indicating non-uniform acceleration.
$y=A \cos (\omega t+\phi)$
$\Rightarrow v$ and $a$ will be varying
So correct option is (2).

question: A particle under the action of a SHM has a period of $3\,{\rm{sec}}$ and under the effect of another it has a period $4\,{\rm{sec}}$. What will be its period under the combined action of both the SHM's in the same direction?
explanation:
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{K}}$ or $T \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{K}}$
$T$ has increased $\dfrac{4}{3}$ times. $K^{\prime}=\dfrac{9 K}{16}$
When both are combined
$\begin{aligned}K_{n e t} & =K+K_{1} \\& =\dfrac{25}{16} K\end{aligned}$
New time period will become $\dfrac{4}{3}$ times of $3\,{\rm{sec}}$ or $2.4\,{\rm{sec}}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A block is resting on a rough horizontal surface, which is executing SHM with amplitude $A$. The frequency of oscillation of horizontal surface for which the block just starts to slip is
explanation:
Frictional force $\geq$ Restoring force
$\mu m g \geq K A$
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{\mu g}{A} \geq \dfrac{K}{M} \\& f=\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{M}} \\& \quad=\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu g}{A}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A block of mass $100\,g$ attached to a spring of spring constant $100\,N{\rm{/}}m$ is lying on a frictionless floor as shown. The block is moved to compress the spring by $10\,cm$ and then released. If the collisions with the wall in front are elastic, then the time period of the motion is: (Given $\pi^{2}=10$ )
explanation:
Total phase traversed
$=\dfrac{\pi}{2}+\dfrac{\pi}{2}+\dfrac{\pi}{6}+\dfrac{\pi}{6}=\dfrac{4 \pi}{3}$
time $=\dfrac{2}{3} T=0.133 \mathrm{sec}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: The angular amplitude of a simple pendulum is $\theta_{0}$. The maximum tension in its string will be
explanation:
The simple pendulum at angular amplitude $\theta_{0}$ is shown in figure. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951060/preptracker/questions/q_40445_explanation_1764951060176.png ${T_{\max }} = mg + \frac{{m{v^2}}}{l}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
When bob of the pendulum comes form $A$ to $B$, it covers a vertical distance $h$.
$\therefore \cos \theta_{0}=\dfrac{l-h}{l}$
$ \Rightarrow h = l\left( {1 - \cos {\theta _0}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Also, during $B$ to $A$, potential energy of bob converts into kinetic energy, i.e., $m g h=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$.
$\therefore v = \sqrt {2gh} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 3 \right)$
Thus, using Eqs. (1), (2) and (3), we obtain
$T_{\max }=m g+\dfrac{2 m g}{l}$
$\left(1-\cos \theta_{0}\right)$
$=m g+2 m g\left[1-1+\dfrac{\theta_{0}^{2}}{2}\right]=m g\left(1+\theta_{0}^{2}\right)$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: Time period of a simple pendulum of length $L$ as measured in an elevator descending with acceleration $\dfrac{g}{3}$ is
explanation:
Effective acceleration in a lift descending with acceleration $g / 3$ is
$\begin{array}{r}g_{\text {eff }}=g-\dfrac{g}{3} \Rightarrow \dfrac{2 g}{3} \\T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{g_{e f f}}} \Rightarrow 2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{3 L}{2 g}}\end{array}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: The acceleration due to gravity on a planet is $3{\rm{/}}2$ times that on the earth. If length of a seconds pendulum on earth is $1\,m$, length of seconds pendulum on the planet is
explanation:
$\sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}}=$ constant
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{l_{1}}{g_{1}}=\dfrac{l_{2}}{g_{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{g}=\dfrac{l}{3 g / 2}$
$ \Rightarrow l = 1.5\,m$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: Starting from the origin, a body oscillates simple harmonically with a period of $2\,\sec $. After what time will its kinetic energy be $75 \%$ of the total energy?
explanation:
$K . E .=\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2}\left(a^{2}-y^{2}\right)=\dfrac{70}{100} \times \dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2} a^{2}$
$a^{2}-y^{2}=\dfrac{3}{4} a^{2} \Rightarrow y=\dfrac{a}{2}$
$y = a\sin \omega t = \frac{a}{2} \Rightarrow \sin \pi t = \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow t = \frac{1}{6}\,s.$
subject: Physics

question: A linear harmonic oscillator of force constant $2 \times {10^6}\,N{\rm{/}}m$ and amplitude $0.01\,m$ has a total mechanical energy of $160\,J$ its
explanation:
By applying energy conservation
$P E_{\min }+K E_{\max }=P E_{\max }$
$\Rightarrow P E_{\max }=160 \mathrm{~J}$
$K E_{\max }=\dfrac{1}{2} K A^{2}$
$ = \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times {10^6} \times {10^{ - 4}} = 100\,J$
$ \Rightarrow P{E_{\min {\rm{ }}}} = 60\,J$

question: A particle of mass \(1 \mathrm{~kg}\) is undergoing S.H.M., for which graph between force and displacement (from mean position) is shown. Its time period, in seconds, is:
explanation:
$F=-m \omega^{2} x$
so $\omega^{2}=\dfrac{13.5}{1.5}=9$
$\Rightarrow \omega=3$
$\Rightarrow T=\dfrac{2 \pi}{3}$

question: $U$ is the $P E$ of an oscillating particle and $F$ is the force acting on it at a given instant. Which of the following is true?
explanation:
Potential energy, $U=\dfrac{1}{2} K x^{2}$
$\begin{aligned}& 2 U=k x^{2} \therefore F=-k x \\& 2 U=-F x \\& \dfrac{2 U}{F}=-x \\& \dfrac{2 U}{F}+x=0\end{aligned}$

question: When a spring is stretched by $10\,cm$, the potential energy stored is $E$. When the spring is stretched by $10\,cm$ more, the potential energy stored in the spring becomes
explanation:
We get $E=\dfrac{1}{2} K(10)^{2}$
$\begin{aligned}E_{2} & =\dfrac{1}{2} K(20)^{2} \\& =\dfrac{1}{2} K \times 4 \times 10^{2} \\E_{2} & =4 E\end{aligned}$

question: A long spring, when stretched by a distance $x$, has potential energy $U$. On increasing the stretching to $n x$, the potential energy of the spring will be
explanation:
Potential energy of spring
$\begin{aligned}U & =\dfrac{1}{2} k x^{2} \\U^{\prime} & =\dfrac{1}{2} k(n x)^{2}=n^{2}\left[\dfrac{1}{2} k x^{2}\right] \\U^{\prime} & =n^{2} U\end{aligned}$

question: The displacement of a particle of mass $3\,gm$ executing simple harmonic motion is given by $y=3 \sin (0.2 t)$ in SI units. The kinetic energy of the particle at a point which is at a distance equal to $\dfrac{1}{3}$ of its amplitude from its mean position is
explanation:
$y=3 \sin (0.2 t) \Rightarrow \omega=0.2$
$K E=\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2}\left(A^{2}-y^{2}\right)$
$=\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2}\left(A^{2}-\dfrac{A^{2}}{9}\right)$
$=\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2} \dfrac{8 A^{2}}{9}$
$=\dfrac{4}{9} m \omega^{2} A^{2}$
$=\dfrac{4}{9} \times 3 \times 10^{-3} \times 4 \times 10^{-2} \times 9$
$ = 48 \times {10^{ - 5}}\,J$
$= 0.48 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,J$
subject: Physics
topic: Force, Energy and their relation in Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Force and Energy in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: The average kinetic energy of a simple harmonic oscillator is 2 joule and its total energy is 5 joule. Its minimum potential energy is
explanation:
$(K E)_{a v}=2$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{(K E)_{\max }}{2}=2 J$
$ \Rightarrow {(KE)_{\max {\rm{ }}}} = 4\,J$
Applying energy conservation
$P E_{\text {min }}+K E_{\text {max }}=P E_{\text {max }}$
$\Rightarrow P E_{\text {min }}+4=5$
$\Rightarrow P E_{\min }=1 J$
subject: Physics

question: When the potential energy of a particle executing simple harmonic motion is one-fourth of its maximum value during the oscillation, the displacement of the particle from the equilibrium position in terms of its amplitude a is:
explanation:
$\dfrac{U}{U_{\max }}=\dfrac{\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2} y^{2}}{\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2} a^{2}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{4}$
$=\dfrac{y^{2}}{a^{2}} \Rightarrow y=\dfrac{a}{2}$

question: The potential energy of a particle executing S.H.M. is $2.5\,J$, when its displacement is half of amplitude. The total energy of the particle be
explanation:
$\dfrac{\text { Potential energy }(U)}{\text { Total energy }(E)}=\dfrac{\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2} y^{2}}{\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2} a^{2}}=\dfrac{y^{2}}{a^{2}}$
${\rm{ }}So{\rm{ }}\frac{{2.5}}{E} = \frac{{{{\left( {\frac{a}{2}} \right)}^2}}}{{{a^2}}} \Rightarrow E = 10\,J$

question: A particle is vibrating in a simple harmonic motion with an amplitude of $4\,cm$. At what displacement from the equilibrium position, is its energy half potential and half kinetic
explanation:
Let $x$ be the point where K.E.=P.E.
Hence $\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2}\left(a^{2}-x^{2}\right)=\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2} x^{2}$
$ \Rightarrow 2{x^2} = {a^2} \Rightarrow x = \frac{a}{{\sqrt 2 }} = \frac{4}{{\sqrt 2 }} = 2\sqrt 2 \,cm$

question:
Two simple harmonic motions exhibited by two particles $A$ and $B$ are given respectively by the following equations.

${y_2} = a\sin \left[ {\omega t + \frac{{3\pi }}{6}} \right]$
Phase difference between them is
explanation: Phase difference $\Delta \phi=\dfrac{3 \pi}{2}-\dfrac{\pi}{6}=\dfrac{\pi}{3}$.

question:
What is the phase difference between two simple harmonic motions represented by
$X_{1}=A \sin \left[\omega t+\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right] \text { and } X_{2}=A \cos \omega t ?$
explanation:
$x_{1}=A \sin \left[\omega t+\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right]$
$x_{2}=a \cot \omega t \Rightarrow A \sin \left[\omega t+\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right]$
phase $\Delta \phi=\phi_{1}-\phi_{2}$
$=\dfrac{\pi}{2}-\dfrac{\pi}{6}=\dfrac{\pi}{3}$

question: The relation between acceleration and displacement of four particles are given below: Which, one of the particle is executing simple harmonic motion?
explanation:

i.e., $a \propto-(y$ or $x)$
We should be clear that $y$ has to be linear.
Correct option is (4).

question: A particle performing SHM having amplitude ' $a$ ' possesses velocity $\dfrac{(3)^{1 / 2}}{2}$ times the velocity at the mean position. The displacement of the particle shall be
explanation:
$V=\dfrac{3^{1 / 2}}{2} a \omega$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} a \omega=a \omega \sqrt{1-\dfrac{y^{2}}{a^{2}}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{3}{4}=1-\dfrac{y^{2}}{a^{2}} \Rightarrow y=\dfrac{a}{2}$

question: A S.H.M. has amplitude ' $a$ ' and time period $T$. The maximum velocity will be
explanation: $v_{\max }=a \omega=\dfrac{a \cdot 2 \pi}{T}=\dfrac{2 \pi a}{T}$

question: A $1.00 \times {10^{ - 20}}\,kg$ particle is executing with simple harmonic motion with a period of $1.00 \times {10^{ - 5}}\sec $ and a maximum speed of $1.00 \times {10^3}\,m{\rm{/}}s$. The maximum displacement of the particle is:
explanation:
$v_{\max }=A \omega=A \times \dfrac{2 \pi}{T} \Rightarrow A=\dfrac{v_{\max } \times T}{2 \pi}$
$A = \frac{{1.00 \times {{10}^3} \times \left( {1 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}} \right)}}{{2\pi }} = 1.59\,mm$

question: If a particle executes SHM with angular velocity $3.5\,rad{\rm{/}}s$ and maximum acceleration $7.5\,m{\rm{/}}{s^2}$, then the amplitude of oscillation will be
explanation:
Maximum acceleration for particle executing SHM.
$a_{\max }=\omega^{2} A$
$A=\dfrac{a_{\max }}{\omega^{2}}$
$A = \frac{{7.5}}{{{{(3.5)}^2}}} \Rightarrow 0.61\,m$

question: A particle executes $\mathrm{SHM}$, its time period is $16\,\,\sec $. If it passes through the centre of oscillation, then its velocity is $2\,m{\rm{/}}s$ at time $2\,\,\sec $. The amplitude will be:
explanation:

$2 = A\frac{{2\pi }}{{16}}\cos \frac{{2\pi }}{{16}} \times 2$
$A = \frac{{16\sqrt 2 }}{\pi } \Rightarrow 7.2\,m$

question: The amplitude and time period of a particle of mass $0.1\,kg$ executing simple harmonic motion are $1\,m$ and $6.28\,\,s$, respectively. Then its (i) angular frequency, (ii) acceleration at a displacement of $0.5\,m$ are respectively.

$y=5 \sin 2 \pi(t+4 x) m$

explanation: $A=5, T=\dfrac{2 \pi}{2 \pi}=1 s$

question: A body oscillates with SHM according to the equation $x=5 \cos \left(2 \pi t+\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right)$. Its instantaneous displacement at $t=1 \mathrm{sec}$ is
explanation:
For instantaneous displacement

$x=5 \cos \left[2 \pi+\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right]$
$=5 \cos \dfrac{\pi}{4}=\dfrac{5}{\sqrt{2}}$.

question: An object is attached to the bottom of a light vertical spring and set vibrating. The maximum speed of the object is $15\,cm{\rm{/}}\sec $ and the period is $628\,ms$. The amplitude of the motion in centimeters is
explanation:
$A \omega=15$

question: A particle moves according to the equation $x=a \cos \left(\dfrac{\pi t}{2}\right)$. The distance covered by it in the time interval between $t=0$ to $t=3$ is
explanation:
$x=a \cos \dfrac{\pi t}{2} \Rightarrow \omega=\dfrac{\pi}{2}$
$\Rightarrow T=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\omega}=4 \mathrm{sec}$
also at $t=0, x=a \Rightarrow$ particle is at extreme position.
$\Rightarrow$ The distance covered in $1 \mathrm{sec}$ is ' $a$ '
$\Rightarrow$ The required distance is ' $3 a$ '.
subject: Physics

question: A second's pendulum is placed in a space laboratory orbiting around the earth at a height $3 R$. Where $R$ is the radius of the earth. The time period of the pendulum is:
explanation: In the given case effective acceleration $g_{\text {eff }}=0 \Rightarrow T=\infty$.

question: The ratio of frequencies of two oscillating pendulums are $3: 2$. Their lengths are in the ratio
explanation:
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}}$
$\Rightarrow T \propto \sqrt{l} \propto \dfrac{1}{n}$ (where $n$ is frequency)
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{n_{1}}{n_{2}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{l_{2}}{l_{1}}}$
$\Rightarrow l_{1}: l_{2}=n_{2}^{2}: n_{1}^{2}=2^{2}: 3^{2}=4: 9$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A rectangular block of mass ' $M$ ' and cross sectional area ' $A$ ' floats on a liquid of density ' $\rho$ '. It is given a small vertical displacement from equilibrium, it starts oscillating with frequency ' $n$ ' then
explanation:
Time period of SHM is given by
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}}$
(where $l$ is length of rectangular block) According to law of floatation,
Weight of block = weight of the displaced liquid
$\Rightarrow M g=A l \rho g \Rightarrow l=\dfrac{M}{A \rho}$
using value of ' $l$ ' in equation (1), we get
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{A \rho g}}$
Frequency $n=\dfrac{1}{T}$
$\begin{aligned}& n=\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{A \rho g}{M}} \\& \Rightarrow n \propto \sqrt{A} .\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: The equation of motion of a particle performing linear S.H.M is $x=5 \sin \left[4 t-\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right]$, where ' $x$ ' is its displacement in $cm$. The velocity of the particle when its displacement is $3\,\,cm$, is
explanation:
The given equation is, $x=5 \sin \left(4 t-\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right)$
$\Rightarrow$ In proper units:
Amplitude, $A=5 \mathrm{~cm}$
Angular velocity, $\omega=4 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$
$\Rightarrow$ The velocity of the wave obtained by,
$v = \omega \sqrt {{A^2} - {y^2}} = 4\sqrt {{{(5)}^2} - {{(3)}^2}} = 16\,cm{\rm{/}}s$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Velocity and Acceleration in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A particle is executing simple harmonic motion with amplitude $A$. When the ratio of its kinetic energy to the potential energy is $\dfrac{1}{4}$, its displacement from its mean position is
explanation:
Kinetic energy $=\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2}\left(A^{2}-x^{2}\right)$
Potential energy $=\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2} x^{2}$
$\therefore \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2}\left(A^{2}-x^{2}\right)}{\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2} x^{2}}=\dfrac{1}{4} \Rightarrow \dfrac{A^{2}-x^{2}}{x^{2}}=\dfrac{1}{4}$
$\Rightarrow 4 A^{2}-4 x^{2}=x^{2} \Rightarrow x^{2}=\dfrac{4}{5} A^{2} \Rightarrow x=\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{5}} A$.
subject: Physics
topic: Force, Energy and their relation in Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Force and Energy in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: Two simple harmonic motions are represented by the equations $y_{1}=0.1 \sin \left(100 \pi t+\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)$ and $y_{1}=0.1 \cos \pi t$. The phase difference of the velocity of particle 1 with respect to the velocity of particle 2 is
explanation:
$v_{1}=\dfrac{d y_{1}}{d t}=0.1 \times 100 \pi \cos \left(100 \pi t+\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)$
$v_{2}=\dfrac{d y_{2}}{d t}=-0.1 \pi \sin \pi t=0.1 \pi \cos \left(\pi t+\dfrac{\pi}{2}\right)$
$\therefore$ Phase difference is:
$\Delta \phi=\left(\phi_{1}-\phi_{2}\right)=\dfrac{\pi}{3}-\dfrac{\pi}{2}=\dfrac{2 \pi-3 \pi}{6}=-\dfrac{\pi}{6}$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Phase and Phase Difference in SHM

question:
Assertion: The percentage change in time period is $1.5 \%$. If the length of simple pendulum increases by $3 \%$.


Reason: The period is directly proportional to length of pendulum.
explanation:
The percentage change in the time period of a simple pendulum is $1.5 \%$ if the length of the pendulum increases by $3 \%$. This is a valid assertion and can be explained using the formula for the period of a simple pendulum, which is $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}}$. Here, "l" is the length of the pendulum, and " $\mathrm{g}$ " is the acceleration due to gravity.
The period of a simple pendulum is not directly proportional to the length of the pendulum; it is inversely proportional to the square root of the length of the pendulum.
So, option (3) is the correct choice. Assertion is correct, but Reason is incorrect.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Both Assertion and Reason are incorrect.

subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: The mass and diameter of a planet are twice those of the earth. The period of oscillation of pendulum on this planet will be (if it is a second's pendulum on earth)
explanation:
Gravity, $g = \frac{{GM}}{{{R^2}}}$
( $G$ is constant)
$\therefore \dfrac{g_{\text {earth }}}{g_{\text {planet }}}=\dfrac{M_{e}}{M_{p}} \times \dfrac{R_{p}^{2}}{R_{e}^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{g_{e}}{g_{p}}=\dfrac{2}{1}$
Also, $T \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{g}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{T_{e}}{T_{p}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{g_{p}}{g_{e}}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{2}{T_{p}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{2}} \Rightarrow T_{p}=2 \sqrt{2} s$
So, correct option is (2)
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: Two simple pendulums first of bob mass $M_{1}$ ${\rm{(bob }}A{\rm{)}}$ and length $L_{1}$, second of bob mass $M_{2}$(bob $B$ ) and length $L_{2} \cdot M_{2}=M_{1}$ and $L_{1}=2 L_{2}$. If the vibrational energies of both are same. Then, which is correct?
explanation:
Frequency, $n=\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{l}}$
$\Rightarrow n \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{l}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{n_{1}}{n_{2}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{l_{2}}{l_{1}}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{L_{2}}{2 L_{1}}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{n_{1}}{n_{2}}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$
$\Rightarrow n_{2}=\sqrt{2} n_{1}$
$\Rightarrow n_{2}>n_{1}$
Energy, $E=\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2} A^{2}=2 \pi^{2} m n^{2} A^{2}$ and $A^{2} \propto \dfrac{1}{m n^{2}}(\because E$ is same $)$
$\therefore \dfrac{A_{1}^{2}}{A_{2}^{2}}=\dfrac{m_{2} n_{2}^{2}}{m_{1} n_{1}^{2}}$
Given, $n_{2}>n_{1}$ and $m_{1}=m_{2}$
$\Rightarrow A_{1}>A_{2}$
So, amplitude of bob $B$ is smaller than bob $A$.
So, correct option is (2)
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A simple harmonic oscillator consists of a particle of mass $m$ and an ideal spring with spring constant $k$. The particle oscillates with a time period $T$. The spring is cut into two equal parts. If one part oscillates with the same particle, the time period will be
explanation:
Mass of the particle $=m$
Spring constant $=k$
Time period of oscillator, $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{k}}$
As $k \propto \dfrac{1}{l}$
(where, $l$ is the length of spring)
$\because k^{\prime}=2 k$
$\therefore T^{\prime}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{2 k}}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} T$
So, correct option is (3)
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A block of rectangular size of mass $m$ and area of cross - section $A$, floats in a liquid of density $\rho$. If we give a small vertical displacement from equilibrium, it undergoes SHM with time period $T$, then
explanation:
According to the law of floatation, weight of the rectangular block $\downarrow=$ buoyant force $\uparrow$
$\Rightarrow m g=v \times \rho \times g$
Now, volume of rectangular block
$=$ length $\times$ breadth $\times$ height
$=l \times b \times h=l \times A$
(because $A=b \times h$ )
At equilibrium, $m g=A l \rho g$
$\Rightarrow m=A \rho l$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951578/preptracker/questions/q_40276_explanation_1764951578329.png where, $l=$ length of part immersed in liquid.
When it is given in downward displacement $y$, restoring force (upward direction) on block is
$\begin{aligned}& F=-[A(l+y) \rho g-m g] \\& =-[A(l+y) \rho g-A l \rho g] \\& =-A \rho g y\end{aligned}$
$i.e.{\rm{ }}F \propto - y\,{\rm{or }}a \propto - y,$ so it executes SHM
Mass of block $=m$
Spring factor $=A \rho g$
Time period $=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{\text { Inertia factor }}{\text { Spring factor }}}$
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{A \rho g}} \Rightarrow T^{2} \propto \dfrac{m}{A \rho}$
So, correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: The function $\sin ^{2} \omega t$ represents
explanation:
Here, $y=\sin ^{2} \omega t$
$\Rightarrow v=\dfrac{d y}{d t}=2 \omega \sin \omega t \cos \omega t=\omega \sin 2 \omega t$$\Rightarrow \dfrac{d^{2} y}{d t^{2}}=2 \omega^{2} \cos 2 \omega t$
Which does not imply the condition for SHM
$\operatorname{viz}\left(\dfrac{d^{2} y}{d t^{2}}\right) \propto(-y)$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951575/preptracker/questions/q_40272_explanation_1764951575361.png Hence, function is not SHM, but periodic.
From the expression for $v$, time period is $t=\dfrac{\pi}{\omega}$
So, correct option is (2).

options.text: A periodic but not simple harmonic motion with a period $2 \pi / \omega$
A periodic but not simple harmonic motion with a period $\pi / \omega$
A simple harmonic motion with a period $2 \pi / \omega$
A simple harmonic motion with a period $\pi / \omega$

subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A block of mass $m$ is connected to a light spring of force constant $k$. The system is placed inside a damping medium of damping constant $b$. The instantaneous values of displacement, acceleration and energy of the block are $x, a$ and $E$ respectively. The initial amplitude of oscillation is $A$ and $\omega^{\prime}$ is the angular frequency of oscillations. The incorrect expression related to the damped oscillations is
explanation:
Standard expression for displacment of damped oscillator is
$X = A{e^{ - \frac{{bt}}{{2m}}}}\cos \left( {{\omega ^\prime } + \phi } \right)$
Correct option is (3).
subject: Physics
topic: Damped Simple Harmonic Motion

question: The \(x-t\) graph of a particle performing simple harmonic motion is shown in the figure. The acceleration of the particle at \(t=2 s\) is:
explanation:
$x=A \sin (\omega t)$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{d x}{d t}=v=A \omega \cos (\omega t)$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{d v}{d t}=a=-\omega^{2} A \sin (\omega t)$
$\Rightarrow a=-\left(\dfrac{2 \pi}{8}\right)^{2} \times 1 \sin \left(\dfrac{2 \pi}{8} \times 2\right)$
$\Rightarrow a=\dfrac{-\pi^{2}}{16} \sin \left(\dfrac{\pi}{2}\right)$
$ \Rightarrow a = \frac{{ - {\pi ^2}}}{{16}}\,m/{s^2}$.
Correct option is (3).
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Velocity and Acceleration in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: Two massless springs with spring constant $2\,K$ and $2\,K$, carry $20\,g$ and $90\,g$ masses at their free ends. These two masses oscillate vertically such that their maximum velocities are equal. Then, the ratio of their respective amplitudes will be:
explanation:
$v_{\max }=\omega A$,
$\omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{m}{K}}$
Given $v_{\text {max }}$ is same for both springs
$\Rightarrow \omega_{1} A_{1}=\omega_{2} A_{2}$
$\dfrac{A_{1}}{A_{2}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{m_{2} K_{1}}{m_{1} K_{2}}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{90 \times K}{20 \times 2 K}}=3: 2 \text {. }$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: In a damped oscillation, damping constant is $20\,gm/\sec $ and mass of an object is $500\,gm$. Find out time when amplitude of oscillation becomes half
explanation:
Amplitude of a damped oscillation:
$A=A_{0} e^{-\dfrac{b t}{2 m}}$
Given $A=\dfrac{1}{2} A_{0}$,
$\frac{{bt}}{{2\,m}} = \ln 2 = 0.693$
$t = \frac{{2\,m}}{{\,b}} \times 0.693$
$t = 2 \times \frac{{500}}{{20}} \times 0.693$
$t = 50 \times 0.693 = 34.6\,\sec .$
subject: Physics
topic: Damped Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A simple pendulum attached to ceiling of lift has time period $T$ when lift is at rest. Find its time period of lift if it starts accelerating upwards with acceleration $g/2$ ?
explanation:
$T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{\ell }{g}} $
When the lift is accelerating upwards, effective value of $g$ is $(g+g / 2)$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {T^\prime } = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{\ell }{{g + \frac{g}{2}}}} \therefore \frac{{{T^\prime }}}{T} = \sqrt {\frac{g}{{\frac{{3\,g}}{2}}}} $
${T^\prime } = T\sqrt {\frac{2}{3}} $
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A simple harmonic motion is represented by $y = 5(\sin 3\pi t + \sqrt 3 \cos 3\pi t)cm$. The amplitude and time period of the motion are
explanation:
The given equation is
$y = 5(\sin 3\pi t + \sqrt 3 \cos 3\pi t)$
$ \Rightarrow y = 2 \times 5\left( {\frac{1}{2}\sin 3\pi t + \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}\cos 3\pi t} \right)$
$ = 10\left( {\cos \frac{\pi }{3}\sin 3\pi t + \sin \frac{\pi }{3}\cos 3\pi t} \right)$

$y=10 \sin \left(3 \pi t+\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)$
Comparing it with standard equation
$y = A\sin (\omega t + \phi )$
$ \Rightarrow \omega = 3\pi {\rm{ }}and{\rm{ }}A = 10\,cm$
$\therefore$ Time period $T = \frac{{2\pi }}{\omega } = \frac{2}{3}\,s$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A particle executes simple harmonic motion and is located at $x=a, b$ and $\mathrm{c}$ at times $t_{0}, 2 t_{0}$ and $3 t_{0}$ respectively. The frequency of the oscillation is:
explanation:
$a = A\sin \omega {t_0}$
$b = A\sin 2\omega {t_0}$
$c = A\sin 3\omega {t_0}$
$a + c = A\left[ {\sin \omega {t_0} + \sin 3\omega {t_0}} \right]$
$ = 2\,A\sin 2\omega {t_0}\cos \omega {t_0}$
$\frac{{a + c}}{b} = 2\cos \omega {t_0}$
$\omega = \frac{1}{{{t_0}}}{\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{a + c}}{{2b}}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow f = \frac{1}{{2\pi {t_0}}}{\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{a + c}}{{2b}}} \right)$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion

question:
An oscillator of mass $M$ is at rest in the equillibrium position in a potential
$V=\dfrac{1}{2} k(x-X)^{2}$. A particle of mass $m$ comes from right with speed $u$ and collides completely inelastically with $M$ and sticks to it. This process repeats every time the oscillator crosses its equillibrium position. The amplitude of oscillations after 13 collisions is:$(M=10, m=5, u=1, k=1)$
explanation:
In first collision mu momentum will be imparted to system. In second collision when momentum of $(M+m)$ is in opposite direction $mu$ momentum of particle will make its momentum zero.
After $13^{\text {th }}$ collision https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956130/preptracker/questions/q_40188_explanation_1764956129976.png The amplitude is related with maximum velocity as
$\begin{aligned}v & =\omega A \\\Rightarrow \dfrac{u}{15} & =\sqrt{\dfrac{K}{M+13 m}} \times A \Rightarrow A=\dfrac{1}{15} \sqrt{\dfrac{75}{1}}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Force, Energy and their relation in Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Force and Energy in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A block of mass $0.1\,kg$ is connected to an elastic spring of spring constant $640\,N{m^{ - 1}}$ and oscillates in a damping medium of damping constant ${10^{ - 2}}\,kg\,{s^{ - 1}}$. The system dissipates its energy gradually. The time taken for its mechanical energy of vibration to drop to half of its initial value, is closest to -
explanation:
The amplitude of oscillation is
$A=A_{0} e^{\frac{-b t}{m}}$
Energy of vibration drops to half of its initial value
$\begin{gathered}E \alpha A^{2} \Rightarrow A \alpha \sqrt{E} \\\dfrac{A}{A_{0}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{E_{0}}{E_{0}}}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\A=\dfrac{A_{0}}{\sqrt{2}}, m=0.1 \mathrm{~kg}, b=10^{-2} \mathrm{kgs}^{-1} \\\dfrac{A_{0}}{\sqrt{2}}=A_{0} e^{\frac{-t}{10}} \\\ln \sqrt{2}=\dfrac{t}{10} \Rightarrow t=3.5 \mathrm{~s}\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics
topic: Damped Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A $1\,kg$ block attached to a spring vibrates with a frequency of $1\,Hz$ on a frictionless horizontal table. Two springs identical to the original spring are attached in parallel to an $8\,kg$ block placed on the same table. So, the frequency of vibration of the $8\,kg$ block is -
explanation:
In first case $f=\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}=1$
$\begin{aligned}& 4 \pi^{2}=\dfrac{k}{m} \\& k=4 \pi^{2} \quad \mathrm{~m}=1\end{aligned}$
In second case
In parallel $k_{e q}=2 k$
$f=\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{k \times 2}{8}}=\dfrac{1}{2} H z$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Oscillation of a Body Due to Combination of Springs

question: The ratio of maximum acceleration to maximum velocity in a simple harmonic motion is $10\,{s^{ - 1}}$. At, $t = 0$ the displacement is $5\,m$. What is the maximum acceleration? The initial phase is $\dfrac{\pi}{4}$.
explanation:
Let $a_{o}$ and $v_{o}$ are maximum acceleration and velocity respectively.
Given that $\dfrac{a_{o}}{v_{o}}=10=\dfrac{\omega^{2} A}{\omega A}=\omega$
$\omega=10 s^{-1}, \phi=\dfrac{\pi}{4} \& x=5 m$
Let $\quad x=A \sin \left(\omega t+\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right)$
$\begin{aligned}& 5=A \sin \left(0+\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right) \Rightarrow A=5 \\& a_{o}=\omega^{2} A=500 \sqrt{2}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Velocity and Acceleration in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: Two particles are performing simple harmonic motion in a straight line about the same equilibrium point. The amplitude and time period for both particles are same and equal to $A$ and $T$, respectively. At time $t = 0$ one particle has displacement A while the other one has displacement $\frac{{ - A}}{2}$ and they are moving towards each other. If they cross each at time $t$, then $t$ is :
explanation:
Projection of uniform circular motion on along a line represents SHM. The two particles are shown in the circular motion https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2023_09_27_1a60125c58390c5c7f6bg-33.jpg?height=518&width=420&top_left_y=302&top_left_x=1357 The two particles are moving in opposite direction along the circle. The time of meeting is
$\begin{gathered}\omega_{r}=\dfrac{\theta}{t}=\dfrac{2 \pi / 3}{t} \\\omega_{r}=2 \omega=2\left(\dfrac{2 \pi}{T}\right)=\dfrac{2 \pi}{T} \\\Rightarrow t=\dfrac{T}{6}\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Phase and Phase Difference in SHM

question: A pendulum with time period of $1\,s$ is losing energy due to damping. At certain time its energy is $45\,J$. If after completing 15 oscillations, its energy has become $15\,J$, its damping constant (in ${s^{ - 1}}$) is:
explanation:
As we know, $E=E_{0} e^{-\dfrac{b t}{m}}$
$15 = 45{e^{ - \frac{{b15}}{{\,m}}}}$
(As no. of oscillations $=15$ so $t = 15\,\sec $)
$\frac{1}{3} = {e^{ - \frac{{b15}}{m}}}$
Taking $\log$ on both sides
$\frac{b}{m} = \frac{1}{{15}}\ln 3$
subject: Physics
topic: Damped Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A cylindrical block of wood (density $ = 650\,kg\,{m^{ - 3}}$), of base area $30\,c{m^2}$ and height $54\,cm$, floats in a liquid of density $900\,kg\,{m^{ - 3}}$. The block is depressed slightly and then released. The time period of the resulting oscillations of the block would be equal to that of a simple pendulum of length (nearly):
explanation:
$h = $ Length of block immersed in water $mg = {F_B}$
$\rho A\lg = {\rho _l}Ahg$
$650 \times A \times 54 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,g = 900 \times A \times hg$
$ \Rightarrow h = 0.39\,m = 39\,cm$
For a floating cylinder, $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{h}{g}}$
so for pendulum $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}} \Rightarrow l=h$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A body is in simple harmonic motion with time period half second $(T = 0.5\,s)$ and amplitude one $cm(A = 1\,cm)$. Find the average velocity in the interval in which it moves from equilibrium position to half of its amplitude
explanation:
Assume that the body starts its motion from the mean position. The displacement of the body is
$\begin{gathered}x=A \sin (\omega t) \\x=\sin \left(\dfrac{2 \pi}{0.5} t\right)=\sin (4 \pi t) \\v=\dfrac{d x}{d t}=4 \pi \cos (4 \pi t)\end{gathered}$
When its displacement is half
$\begin{aligned}& x=\dfrac{1}{2}=\sin \left(\dfrac{2 \pi}{0.5} t\right) \\& 4 \pi t=\dfrac{\pi}{6} \Rightarrow t=\dfrac{1}{24} s\end{aligned}$
The average velocity is
$ < v > = \frac{{\int_0^t v dt}}{{t - 0}} = \frac{{4\pi \int_0^{\frac{1}{{24}}} {\cos } (4\pi t)dt}}{{\frac{1}{{24}}}}$
$ < v > = \frac{{96\pi }}{{4\pi }}\left[ {\sin \left( {4\pi r\left( {\frac{1}{{24}}} \right)} \right) - \sin (0)} \right]$
$ < v > = 12\,cm/s$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Velocity and Acceleration in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: Which of the following expressions for force acting on a particle corresponds to simple harmonic motion along a straight line, where $x$ is the displacement and $a,b,c$ are positive constants?
explanation: $F=m \dfrac{d^{2} x}{d t}=-b x$

question: Two bodies of masses $1\,kg$ and $4\,kg$ are connected to a vertical spring, as shown in the figure. The smaller mass executes simple harmonic motion of angular frequency $25\,rad/s$, and amplitude $1.6\,cm$ while the bigger mass remains stationary on the ground. The maximum force exerted by the system on the floor is (take $g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$).
explanation:
Let $T$ is the maximum tension in the spring The force on the upper block is $T - mg = m{\omega ^2}\,A$
$ = 1 \times 625 \times \frac{{1.6}}{{100}} = 10\,N$
$T = 20\,N$
The force on the floor is $N = T + 40 = 60\,N$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A uniform cylinder of length $L$ and mass $M$ having cross-sectional area $A$ is suspended, with its length vertical, from a fixed point by a massless spring, such that it is half submerged in a liquid of density $\sigma$ at equilibrium position. When the cylinder is given a downward push and released, it starts oscillating vertically with a small amplitude. The time period $T$ of the oscillations of the cylinder will be
explanation:
Let $x_{0}=$ Submerged length in equilibrium
$x=$ Additional submerged length at time $t$ At equilibrium
$\therefore \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} Mg = A{x_0}\sigma g + k{x_0}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
$ - M\frac{{{d^2}x}}{{d{t^2}}} = A\left( {{x_0} + x} \right)\sigma g - Mg + k\left( {x + {x_0}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From (1) and (2) $\therefore \quad M \dfrac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}=-(A \sigma g+k) x$
$\begin{aligned}& \therefore \quad \omega^{2}=\dfrac{A \sigma g+k}{M} \\& \therefore \quad T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{(A \sigma g+k)}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A particle is executing simple harmonic motion with a time period \[T\]. At time \[t = 0\], it is at its position of equilibrium. The kinetic energy-time graph of the particle will look like
explanation:
Time taken to reach the extreme position from equilibrium position is $\dfrac{T}{4}$. Velocity is maximum at equilibrium position and zero at extreme position. $v=A \omega \cos \omega t, K . E=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ ( $m$ is the mass of particle and $v$ is the velocity of particle)
$K.E = \frac{1}{2}\,m{A^2}{\omega ^2}{\cos ^2}\omega t$
Hence graph of $K.E.$ and time is (2) https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956970/preptracker/questions/q_40131_explanation_1764956970395.jpg
subject: Physics
topic: Force, Energy and their relation in Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Force and Energy in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: The variation of velocity of a particle executing SHM with time is shown in fig. The velocity of the particle when a phase change of \(\pi / 6\) takes place from the instant it is at one of the extreme positions will be
explanation:
From the graph $T = 4\,s$
and ${v_{\max {\rm{ }}}} = 5\,m/s;\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} \omega A = 5\,m/s$
$\left(\dfrac{2 \pi}{T}\right) A=5 \Rightarrow A=\dfrac{5 T}{2 \pi}=\dfrac{5 \times 4}{2 \pi}=\dfrac{10}{\pi} m$
Also, $\omega = \frac{{2\pi }}{4} = \frac{\pi }{2}rad/s$
The equation of velocity can be written as
$v=5 \sin \left(\dfrac{\pi}{2} t\right) m / s$
The phase $\phi=\dfrac{\pi}{2} t$
Phase change is
$\Delta \phi = \frac{\pi }{2}\Delta t = \frac{\pi }{6} \Rightarrow \Delta t = {t_f} - {t_i} = t = \frac{1}{3}s$
$v = 5\sin \left( {\frac{\pi }{2}\left( {\frac{1}{3}} \right)} \right) = 2.5\,m/s$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Velocity and Acceleration in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: Assume that you are examining the characteristics of the suspension system of a $2000\,kg$ automobile. The suspension "sags" $25\,cm$ when the weight of the entire automobile is placed on it. In addition, the amplitude of oscillation decreases by $50 \%$ in $100\,\sec $. Estimate the value of $b$ for the shock absorber system of one wheel, assuming each wheel supports $500\,kg$.
explanation:
Now, $A=A_{0} e^{-b t / 2 m}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{A_0}}}{2} = {A_0}{e^{ - 100b/2 \times 500}} = {A_0}{e^{ - b/10}}$
$\therefore \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} b = 10\ln 2 = 6.93\,kg/\sec .$
