question: A wire is bent at an angle $\theta$. A rod of mass $m$ can slide along the bent wire without friction as shown in Fig. Soap film is maintained in the frame kept in a vertical position and the rod is in equilibrium as shown in the figure. If rod is displaced slightly in vertical direction, then the time period of small oscillation of the rod is
explanation:
Let $S$ be the surface tension of the soap film. For equilibrium of rod https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951058/preptracker/questions/q_40073_explanation_1764951058122.jpg $2(l+y) \tan \dfrac{\theta}{2}=$ Length of the boundary of soap film in contact with the rod.
$\begin{gathered}m g=\left(F_{\text {surface }}\right)_{1} \\m g=\left(2 l \tan \dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) S \times 2 ; m g=4 S l \tan \dfrac{\theta}{2}\end{gathered}$
If the rod is displaced from its mean position by small displacement $y$, then restoring force on the rod is
$\begin{gathered}F=-\left[4 S(l+y) \tan \dfrac{\theta}{2}-m g\right]=-\left[4 S \tan \dfrac{\theta}{2}\right] y \\a=-\dfrac{4 S \tan \dfrac{\theta}{2}}{m} y=\dfrac{-4 S \tan \left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) y}{\left(\dfrac{4 S l \tan \dfrac{\theta}{2}}{g}\right)} \\\dfrac{d^{2} y}{d t^{2}}=-\left(\dfrac{g}{l}\right) y \quad \therefore T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}}\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: An air chamber of volume $V$, has a long neck of cross-sectional area A. A ball of mass $m$ is fitted smoothly in the neck. The bulk modulus of air is $B$. If the ball is pressed down slightly and released, the time period of its oscillation is
explanation:
Let the ball is pressed down by a small amount $\mathrm{y}$, then the volume of air decreases by Ay.
Then, excess pressure is
$d p=-B\left(\dfrac{d V}{V}\right)=-B\left(\dfrac{y A}{V}\right)$
A restoring force $F(A d P)$ acts in the upward direction.
$\therefore \quad F=A\left[-B\left(\dfrac{y A}{V}\right)\right]=-\dfrac{B A^{2}}{V} \cdot y$
$\therefore \quad a=-\dfrac{B A^{2}}{m V} y$ comparing this with $a=-\omega^{2} y$
$\begin{aligned}\omega & =\sqrt{\dfrac{B A^{2}}{m V}} \\\Rightarrow \quad T & =\dfrac{2 \pi}{\omega}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m V}{B A^{2}}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A block $A$ of mass $m$ is connected to spring and performs simple harmonic motion with a time period of $2 s$. Another block $B$ of mass $m$ rests on $A$. The coefficient of static friction betwwen $A$ and $B$ is $\mu_{s}=0.6$. The maximum amplitude of oscillation which the system can have so that there is no relative motion between $A$ and $B$ is (take $\pi^{2}=g=10$ )
explanation:
Let $A$ be the maximum amplitude of oscillation for the required situation.
Maximum force on block $B$ is
$F=M a_{\text {max }}=m \omega^{2} A$
For no slipping $F_{\max } < f_{L}$
The maximum frictional force between the blocks is
$f_{L}=\mu_{s} m g$
i.e., $\quad m \omega^{2} A < \mu_{s} m g$
$ = A < \frac{{0.6 \times g}}{{\frac{{4{\pi ^2}}}{4}}} = 0.6 \Rightarrow \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {A_{\max }} = 0.6\,m$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A block $P$ of mass $m$ is placed on a smooth horizontal surface. A block $Q$ of same mass is placed over the block $P$ and the coefficient of static friction between them is $\mu_{s}$. A spring of spring constant $K$ is attached to block $Q$. The blocks are displaced together to a distance A and released. The upper block oscillates without slipping over the lower block. The maximum frictional force between the block is
explanation:
Angular frequency of system,
$\omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{K}{m+m}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{K}{2 m}}$
Maximum acceleration, ${a_{\max }} = {\omega ^2}\,A$
Frictional force between $P$ and $Q$
$=$ force exerted on lower block
$=m \omega^{2} A=m\left(\dfrac{K}{2 m}\right) A=\dfrac{K A}{2}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A horizontal rod of mass $m$ and length $L$ is pivoted at one end. The rod's other end is supported by a spring of force constant $k$. The rod is displaced by a small angle $\theta$ from its horizontal equilibrium position and released. The angular frequency of the subsequent simple harmonic motion is
explanation:
Restoring torque
$\tau = KyL = \frac{{m{L^2}}}{3}\alpha {\rm{ (Since }}y \cong L\theta {\rm{ from figure)}}$
$ \Rightarrow K{L^2}\theta = \frac{{m{L^2}}}{3}\alpha \Rightarrow \frac{{{d^2}\theta }}{{d{t^2}}} = - \frac{{3k}}{m}\theta $
$ \Rightarrow T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{\theta }{\alpha }} = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{{m\theta }}{{3k\theta }}} = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{m}{{3k}}} $ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951054/preptracker/questions/q_40057_explanation_1764951054072.jpg $\omega = \sqrt {\frac{{3k}}{m}} $
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A uniform disc of mass $m$ and radius $R$ is connected with two light springs 1 and 2 . The springs are connected at the highest point $M$ and the $CM$ ' $N$ ' of the disc. The other ends of the springs are rigidly attached with vertical walls. If we shift the CM in horizontal by a small distance, the disc oscillates simple harmonically. Assuming a perfect rolling of the disc on the horizontal surface, find the angular frequency of oscillation.
explanation:
Let $x$ be the displacement of CM from mean position. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956969/preptracker/questions/q_40053_explanation_1764956968817.jpg The torque about $\mathrm{O}$ is
$\begin{aligned}& \tau_{o}=K_{2} x R+K_{1}(2 x) 2 R=I_{o} \alpha \\& \tan \theta=\theta=\dfrac{x}{R}=\dfrac{2 x}{2 R} \\& K_{2} R^{2} \theta+4 K_{1} R^{2} \theta=I_{o} \dfrac{d^{2} \theta}{d t^{2}} \\& \dfrac{d^{2} \theta}{d t^{2}}=-\dfrac{R^{2} \theta\left[K_{2}+4 K_{1}\right]}{\left(\dfrac{m R^{2}}{2}+m R^{2}\right)} \\& \omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{2\left(4 K_{1}+K_{2}\right)}{3 m} .}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Oscillation of a Body Due to Combination of Springs

question: A simple pendulum has a length $l$. The inertia and gravitational masses of the bob are $m_{i}$ and $m_{g}$ respectively. Then the time period $T$ is given by
explanation:
Torque acting on the bob
$=l \alpha=-(m g) l \sin \theta$
or $\quad\left(m_{i} l^{2}\right) \alpha=-\left(m_{g} g\right) l \theta$
or $\quad \alpha=-\left(\dfrac{m_{g} g}{m_{i} l}\right) \theta=-\omega^{2} \theta$
where, $\omega^{2}=\dfrac{m_{g} g}{m_{i} l} \quad \therefore T=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\omega}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m_{i} l}{m_{g} g}}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: $A$ and $B$ are fixed points and the mass $M$ is tied by strings at $A$ and $B$. If the mass $M$ is displaced slightly out of this plane and released, it will execute oscillations with period. (Given, $AM = BM = L,AB = 2\,d$ )
explanation:
The motion of $M$ is SHM, with length
$CM = \sqrt {{L^2} - {d^2}} $
$T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{{{{\left( {{L^2} - {d^2}} \right)}^{1/2}}}}{g}} $ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956961/preptracker/questions/q_40045_explanation_1764956961341.jpg
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A pendulum has time period $T$ for small oscillations. An obstacle is placed directly beneath the pivot, so that only the lowest one quarter of the string can follow the pendulum bob when it swings in the left of its resting position as shown in figure. The pendulum is released from rest at a certain point $A$. The time taken by it to return to the point $A$ is
explanation:
The total time for oscillation is $\dfrac{T_{1}}{2}+\dfrac{T_{2}}{2}$
$T_{1}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{g}}, T_{2}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{4 g}}$
Given that $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{g}}$
$\begin{aligned}& T_{1}=\dfrac{T}{2}, T_{2}=\dfrac{T}{4} \\& T_{N e t}=\dfrac{3 T}{4}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: The potential energy of a particle $\left(U_{x}\right)$ executing SHM is given by
explanation:
Potential energy of body in SHM at an instant,
$U=\dfrac{1}{2} k y^{2}$
where the displacement, $y=(x-a)$
subject: Physics
topic: Force, Energy and their relation in Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Force and Energy in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A body is executing simple harmonic motion. At a displacement $x$ its potential energy is $E_{1}$ and at a displacement $y$ its potential energy is $E_{2}$. The potential energy $E$ at displacement $(x+y)$ is
explanation:
$E_{1}=\dfrac{1}{2} K x^{2} \Rightarrow x=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 E_{1}}{K}}$,
$E_{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} K y^{2} \Rightarrow y=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 E_{2}}{K}}$
And $E=\dfrac{1}{2} K(x+y)^{2} \Rightarrow x+y=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 E}{K}}$
$\Rightarrow \sqrt{\dfrac{2 E_{1}}{K}}+\sqrt{\dfrac{2 E_{2}}{K}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 E}{K}} \Rightarrow \sqrt{E_{1}}+\sqrt{E_{2}}=\sqrt{E}$
subject: Physics
topic: Force, Energy and their relation in Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Force and Energy in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: Two pendulums start oscillating in the same direction at the same time from the same mean position. Their time periods are respectively $2\,s$ and $1.5\,s$. The phase difference betwen them, when the smaller pendulum has completed one vibration, will be
explanation: Smaller pendulum will be that for which time period is less, here $1.5 \mathrm{~s}$. So when this completes one vibration, the bigger pendulum will complete its $3 / 4$ oscillation (because its time period is 2 s) i.e., it will be at its negative extreme position. Hence the smaller pendulum will be ahead by a phase of $\pi / 2$ than bigger pendulum at this time.
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Phase and Phase Difference in SHM

question: Two particles $P$ and $Q$ describe S.H.M. of same amplitude $a$, same frequency $f$ along the same straight line. The maximum distance between the two particles is $a \sqrt{2}$. The phase difference between the particles is:
explanation:
$x_{1}=a \sin \left(\omega t+\phi_{1}\right) ; \quad x_{2}=a \sin \left(\omega t+\phi_{2}\right)$
$\Rightarrow\left|x_{1}-x_{2}\right|=2 a \sin \left(\omega t+\dfrac{\phi_{1}+\phi_{2}}{2}\right) \cos \left(\dfrac{\phi_{1}-\phi_{2}}{2}\right)$
To maximise $\left|x_{1}-x_{2}\right|: \sin \left(\omega t+\dfrac{\phi_{1}+\phi_{2}}{2}\right)=1$
Given that $\left|x_{1}-x_{2}\right|_{\max }=a \sqrt{2}$
$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow a \sqrt{2}=2 a \times 1 \times \cos \left(\dfrac{\phi_{1}-\phi_{2}}{2}\right) \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=\cos \left(\dfrac{\phi_{1}-\phi_{2}}{2}\right) \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{\pi}{4}=\dfrac{\phi_{1}-\phi_{2}}{2} \Rightarrow \phi_{1}-\phi_{2}=\dfrac{\pi}{2}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Phase and Phase Difference in SHM

question: Time period $(T)$ and amplitude (1) are same for two particles which undergo SHM along the same line. At one particular instant, one particle is at phase $\dfrac{3 \pi}{2}$ and other is at phase zero. While moving in the same direction. Find the time at which they will cross each other.
explanation:
At phase $3 \pi / 2$,
$\begin{aligned}& x_{1}=A \sin \dfrac{3 \pi}{2}=-A \\& x_{2}=0\end{aligned}$
So, at $t=0, x_{1}=-A \cos \omega t$ and $x_{2}=A \sin \omega t$
$\begin{aligned}& x_{1}=x_{2} \\& -\cos \omega t=\sin \omega t \\& \tan \omega t=-1 \\& \omega t=\dfrac{3 \pi}{4}\end{aligned}$

$\left(\dfrac{2 \pi}{T}\right) t=\dfrac{3 \pi}{4} \Rightarrow t=3 T / 8$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Phase and Phase Difference in SHM

question:
How long does it take for the amplitude of the damped oscillations to drop to half its initial value. For the damped oscillator of shown in the figure. $m = 250\,g,k = 85\,N/m$, and
$b = 70\,g/s$.
explanation:
$A=A_{0} e^{-b t / 2 m}$
$\therefore \quad t=\dfrac{2 m}{b} \ln \dfrac{A_{0}}{A}=\dfrac{2 \times 0.25}{0.07} \ln \dfrac{A_{0}}{A_{0} / 2}=4.95 \mathrm{sec}$
subject: Physics
topic: Damped Simple Harmonic Motion

question: An ideal gas enclosed in a vertical cylinderical container supports a freely moving piston of mass $M$. The piston and the cylinder have equal cross sectional area A. When the piston is in equilibrium, the volume of the gas is $V_{0}$ and its pressure is $P_{0}$. The piston is slightly displaced from the equilibrium position and released. Assuming that the system is completely isolated from its surrounding, the piston executes a simple harmonic motion with frequency
explanation:
Initially at equilibrium $\dfrac{M g}{A}=P_{0}$
$\begin{aligned}& P_{0} V_{0}^{\gamma}=P V^{\gamma} \\& M g=P_{0} A\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned}& P_{0} A^{\gamma} x_{0}^{\gamma}=P A^{\gamma}\left(x_{0}-x\right)^{\gamma} \\& P=\dfrac{P_{0} x_{0}^{\gamma}}{\left(x_{0}-x\right)^{\gamma}}\end{aligned}$
Piston https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951015/preptracker/questions/q_40009_explanation_1764951015417.jpg Let piston is displaced by distance $x$
$\begin{aligned}& M g-\left(\dfrac{P_{0} x_{0}^{\gamma}}{\left(x_{0}-x\right)^{\gamma}}\right) A=F_{\text {restoring }} \\& P_{0} A\left(1-\dfrac{x_{0}^{\gamma}}{\left(x_{0}-x\right)^{\gamma}}\right)=F_{\text {restoring }}\end{aligned}$
$F=P_{o} A\left[1-\dfrac{1}{\left(1-\dfrac{x}{x_{o}}\right)^{\gamma}}\right]=P_{o} A\left[1-\left(1-\dfrac{x}{x_{o}}\right)^{-\gamma}\right]$$=P_{o} A\left[1-\left(1+\dfrac{\gamma x}{x_{o}}\right)\right]$
$F=-\dfrac{\gamma P_{0} A x}{x_{0}}$
$\therefore$ Frequency with which piston executes SHM.
$f=\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma P_{0} A}{x_{0} M}}=\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma P_{0} A^{2}}{M V_{0}}}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question:
Two uniform ropes having linear mass densities $m$ and $4\,m$ are joined to form a closed loop. The loop is hanging over a fixed frictionless small pulley with the lighter rope above as shown in the figure. (in the figure equilibrium position is shown). Now if point A (joint) is slightly displaced in downward direction and released. It is found that the loop performs SHM with the period of oscillation equal to T. Find the value of
$T\left( {} \right.$ take $\left. {l = \frac{{150m}}{{4{\pi ^2}}},g = 10\,m/{s^2}} \right)$.
explanation:
Let $x$ be the instantaneous downward displacement of joint $A$.
The initial and final position are https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956975/preptracker/questions/q_40005_explanation_1764956974606.jpg $\therefore \quad(2 l \times 4 m+2 l \times m) \ddot{x}=-(4 m \times 2 x-m \times 2 x) g$
$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow \quad \ddot{x}=-\dfrac{3 g}{5 l} x=-\dfrac{3 \times 10}{5 \times \dfrac{150}{4 \pi^{2}}} \times x=-\left(\dfrac{2 \pi}{5}\right)^{2} x \\& \therefore \quad T=\dfrac{2 \pi}{2 \pi / 5} \sec =5 \mathrm{sec}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A bead of mass $m$ can slide on a frictionless wire as shown in figure. Because of the given shape of the wire near $P$, the bottom point, it can be approximated as a parabola. Near $P$, the potential energy of the bead is given $U=c x^{2}$ where $c$ is a constant and $x$ is measured from $P$. The bead, if displaced slightly from point $P$ will oscillate about $P$. The period of oscillation is
explanation:
$U=C x^{2}$
$F=-\dfrac{d U}{d x} \Rightarrow m a=-2 c x \Rightarrow a=-\dfrac{2 c}{m} x$

$\Rightarrow \omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 c}{m}} \Rightarrow T=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\omega}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{2 c}}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A sphere of mass $m$ and radius $r$ rolls without slipping on a rough concave surface of large radius $\mathrm{R}$. It makes small oscillations about the lowest point. Find the time period.
explanation:
Let at any instant, the body is at angular position $\theta$ with respect to the vertical line drawn from the centre $Q$. If $\phi$ is the angular displacement of the ball about its centre, then
$(R-r) \theta=r \phi \quad \therefore \theta=\left(\dfrac{r}{R-r}\right) \phi$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956112/preptracker/questions/q_39997_explanation_1764956112455.jpg (b)Restoring torque acting on the ball
$\tau=-m g \sin \theta \times r$
For small $\theta, \quad \sin \theta=\theta$
$\begin{gathered}\therefore \tau=-m g \theta \times r \\\text { or } \tau=-m g\left[\dfrac{r}{R-r}\right] \phi r=m g\left(\dfrac{r^{2}}{R-r}\right)(-\phi)\end{gathered}$
Angular acceleration $\alpha=\dfrac{m g}{I}\left(\dfrac{r^{2}}{R-r}\right)(-\phi)$
Now comparing above equation with standard equation of SHM,
$\alpha=-\omega^{2} \phi, \text { we get } \omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{m g}{I}\left(\dfrac{r^{2}}{R-r}\right)}$
Here $I$ is the moment of inertia of the rolling ball about the point of contact which is $I=(7 / 5) m r^{2}$
$\omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{m g}{\dfrac{7}{5} m r^{2}}\left(\dfrac{r^{2}}{R-r}\right)}=\sqrt{\dfrac{5}{7} \dfrac{g}{(R-r)}}$
and $\quad T=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\omega}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{7(R-r)}{5 g}}$
Alternative method:
We can imagine that when the $CM$ of the body is shifted slightly by an angle $\theta$ relative to the centre of curvature of the surface, the magnitude of acceleration of the $CM$ of the body can be given as
$a=\dfrac{g \sin \theta}{1+\dfrac{k^{2}}{r^{2}}}$
(because the body is accelerating down the inclined plane of instantaneous angle $\theta$ )
Substituting $\sin \theta=\theta=\dfrac{x}{R-r}$, we have
$a = \frac{{ - gx}}{{(R - r)\left( {1 + \frac{{{k^2}}}{{{r^2}}}} \right)}}$https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956113/preptracker/questions/q_39997_explanation_1764956113265.jpg (negative sign is given because $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{x}$ are oppositely directed)
Comparing the above equation with $a=-\omega^{2} x$, we have
$\omega^{2}=\dfrac{g}{(R-r)\left(1+\dfrac{k^{2}}{r^{2}}\right)}$
For solid sphere $k^{2}=\dfrac{2 r^{2}}{5}$.
Hence $\omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{5}{7} \dfrac{g}{(R-r)}}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A body of mass $m$ hangs from a smooth light fixed pulley $P_{1}$ by the inextensible string fitted with the springs of stiffness $k_{1}$ and $k_{2}$. the string passes over the smooth light pulley $P_{2}$ which is connected with another ideal spring of stiffness $k_{2}$. Find the period of oscillation of the body.
explanation:
Suppose static deflection of the body of mass $m$ be $x$, that of spring (1) be $x_{1}$ and spring (2) be $x_{2}$.
$\therefore \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {x_1}{K_1} = {x_3}{K_3} = mg\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
${K_2}{x_2} = 2{K_1}{x_1}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
The given arrangement can be obtained by superimposing the following arrangements. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956976/preptracker/questions/q_39993_explanation_1764956976269.png $K_{3} x_{3}=m g \quad \dfrac{K_{2} x_{2}}{2}=m g \quad k_{1} x_{1}=m g$
Now, $x=x_{3}+2 x_{2}+x_{1}=\dfrac{m g}{K_{3}}+\dfrac{4 m g}{K_{2}}+\dfrac{m g}{K_{1}}$
The given arrangement can be replaced by an equivalent spring-mass system shown in fig -IV in the which static deflection of the mass is $x$.
$\begin{aligned}\therefore \quad m g & =K_{e} x \\\Rightarrow m g & =K_{e}\left(\dfrac{m g}{K_{3}}+\dfrac{4 m g}{K_{2}}+\dfrac{m g}{K_{1}}\right) \\\Rightarrow & \dfrac{1}{K_{e}}=\left(\dfrac{1}{K_{3}}+\dfrac{4}{K_{2}}+\dfrac{1}{K_{1}}\right) \\\therefore \quad T & =2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{K_{e}}}=2 \pi \sqrt{m\left(\dfrac{1}{K_{3}}+\dfrac{4}{K_{2}}+\dfrac{1}{K_{1}}\right)}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Oscillation of a Body Due to Combination of Springs

question: A mass $m$ is hung on an ideal massless spring. Another equal mass is connected to the other end of the spring. the whole system is at rest. At $t = 0,\,m$ is released and the system falls freely under gravity. Assume that natural length of the spring is $L_{0}$, its initial stretched length is $L$ and the acceleration due to gravity is $g$. What is distance between masses as function of time?
explanation:
In $CM$ frame both the masses execute SHM with
$\omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{\mu}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 k}{m}}$
Initially particles are at extreme Initial extension in the spring is $\left(L-L_{o}\right)$
Distance between them $=L_{0}+\left(L-L_{0}\right) \cos \sqrt{\dfrac{2 k}{m}} t$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: The coefficient of friction between block of mass $m$ and $2\,m$ is $\mu=2 \tan \theta$. There is no friction between block of mass $2\,m$ and inclined plane. The maximum amplitude of the two block system for which there is no relative motion between both the blocks is
explanation:
Here $\omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{3 m}}$. The maximum static frictional force is
$f_{\max }=\mu m g \cos \theta=2 \tan \theta m g \cos \theta=2 m g \sin \theta$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951008/preptracker/questions/q_39985_explanation_1764951007835.jpg Applying Newton's second law on the upper block at lower extreme position,
$f-m g \sin \theta=m \omega^{2} A \Rightarrow f=A m \omega^{2}+m g \sin \theta$
As $f \leq f_{\max }, \quad \omega^{2} A \leq g \sin \theta$ or $A \leq \dfrac{3 m g \sin \theta}{k}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: The pulley shown in the figure. has a radius $r$, moment of inertia $I$ about its axis and mass $m$. Find the time period of vertical oscillations of its centre of mass. the spring constant of spring is $K$ and the spring does not slip over the pulley.
explanation:
Let $x_{0}$ be the static deflection of the pulley. It is slightly pulled downward and released at $t=0$. At time $=t$, its displacement from equilibrium position is $x$ and linear acceleration is ' $a$ '. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956964/preptracker/questions/q_39981_explanation_1764956964094.jpg $\therefore \,\,2K{x_0} = mg\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Let $T_{1} \& T_{2}$ are tensions in the strings
$\left( {{T_1} + {T_2}} \right) - mg = ma\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
For rotation, $\left(T_{1}-T_{2}\right) R=I\left(\dfrac{a}{R}\right)$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} \left( {{T_1} - {T_1}} \right) = \frac{{Ia}}{{{R^2}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(3)$
From (2) and (3)
$\begin{gathered}2 T_{1}=m a+\dfrac{I a}{R^{2}}+m g \\2\left(k x_{o}+k x\right)=m a+\dfrac{I a}{R^{2}}+m g \\\Rightarrow a=\dfrac{2 K}{\left(m+\dfrac{I}{R^{2}}\right)} \times x\end{gathered}$

$\therefore \quad T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m+\dfrac{I}{R^{2}}}{2 K}}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Oscillation of a Body Due to Combination of Springs

question: A uniform stick of mass $M$ and length $L$ is pivoted at its centre. Its ends are tied to two springs each of force constant $K$. In the position shown figure, the strings are in their natural length. When the string is displaced through a small angle $\theta$ and released. The stick:
explanation:
If stick is rotated through a small angle $\theta$, the spring is stretched by a distance $\dfrac{L}{2} \theta$. Therefore restoring force is spring $=K\left(\dfrac{L}{2} \theta\right)$. Each spring causes a torque $\tau(=F d)=\left(\dfrac{K L \theta}{2}\right) \dfrac{L}{2}$ in the same direction.
Therefore equation of rotational motion is
$\begin{aligned}& \tau=I \alpha \\& -2\left(\dfrac{K L \theta}{2}\right) \dfrac{L}{2}=I \alpha \text { with } I=\dfrac{M L^{2}}{12}\end{aligned}$
$\alpha=-\left(\dfrac{6 K}{M}\right) \theta$
Standard equation of angular S.H.M. is
$\begin{aligned}& \alpha=-\omega^{2} \theta \\& \omega^{2}=\dfrac{6 K}{M}\end{aligned}$
frequency $f=\dfrac{\omega}{2 \pi}=\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{6 K}{M}}$

options.text: Executes periodic motion which is not simple harmonic

subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Oscillation of a Body Due to Combination of Springs

question: A smooth ring of mass $m_{1}$ is lying on a rigid horizontal string. A bob of mass $m_{2}$ hangs from the ring by an inextensible light string of length $l$. Find the angular frequency of oscillation of the system.
explanation:
Both will oscillate about their centre of mass. the oscillation of $\mathrm{M}$ is same as a simple pendulum of mass $\mathrm{M}$ and length $l_{1}$. The time period of both particles are same. Let it be $T$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951002/preptracker/questions/q_39974_explanation_1764951001716.jpg $T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{{{l_1}}}{g}} $
where $l_{1}$ can be given as $M l_{1}=m l_{2}$ and $l_{1}+l_{2}=l$
$l_{1}=\dfrac{m l}{M+m}$
Then, $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m l}{(M+m) g}}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: Two light strings, each of length $l$, are fixed at points $A$ and $B$ on a fixed horizontal rod $xy$. A small bob is tied by both strings the strings are making angle $45^{\circ}$ with the rod. If the bob is slightly displaced normal to the plane of the strings and released then period of the resulting small oscillation will be:
explanation:
The effective length of the bob is $\dfrac{l}{\sqrt{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{\sqrt{2} g}{l}} \Rightarrow T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{\sqrt{2} g}}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A simple pendulum of length $L$ is suspended from the top of a flat beam of thickness $L / 2$. The bob is pulled away from the beam so that it makes an angle $\theta=30^{\circ}$ with the vertical, as shown in the figure. It is then released from rest If $\phi$ is the maximum angular deflection to the right, then
explanation:
Take the lowest point of the pendulum as the reference line to measure $P.E$
$P . E_{i}=P . E_{f}$ (when the bob is at extreme positions)
$\begin{gathered}m g(L-L \cos \theta)=m g\left(\dfrac{L}{2}-\dfrac{L}{2} \cos \phi\right) \\1-\cos \theta=\dfrac{1}{2}-\dfrac{\cos \phi}{2}\end{gathered}$
$\dfrac{\cos \phi}{2}=\cos \theta-\dfrac{1}{2}$
As $\theta=30^{\circ} \quad \cos \theta=\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$
$\begin{aligned}& \cos \phi=\sqrt{3}-1 \\& \phi=\cos ^{-1}(0.732) \approx 42^{0} \\& \theta < \phi < 2 \theta\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question:
A particle is moving on $x$-axis has potential energy $U=2-20 x+5 x^{2}$ Joules along $x$-axis. The particle is released at $x=-3$. The maximum value of ' $x$ ' will be:
( $x$ is in metres and $U$ is in joules)
explanation:
$U=2-20 x+5 x^{2}$
$F=-\dfrac{d U}{d x}=20-10 x$
At equilibrium position: $F=0$
$20-10 x=0 \Rightarrow x=2$
Since particle is released at $x=-3$, therefore amplitude of particle is 5. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950999/preptracker/questions/q_39961_explanation_1764950998991.jpg It will oscillate about $x=2$ with an amplitude of 5.
$\therefore$ maximum value of $x$ will be 7 .
subject: Physics
topic: Force, Energy and their relation in Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Force and Energy in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: Two particles are executing simple harmonic motion of the same amplitude $A$ and frequency $\omega$ along the $x$-axis. Their mean position is separated by distance $X_{0}\left(X_{0}>A\right)$. If the maximum separation between them is $A$, the phase difference between thier motion is:
explanation:
Let, $x_{1}=A \sin \omega t$ and $x_{2}=A \sin (\omega t+\phi)$
$x_{2}-x_{1}=2 A \cos \left(\omega t+\dfrac{\phi}{2}\right) \sin \dfrac{\phi}{2}$
As the maximum separation between the particle is $\mathrm{A}, \quad \therefore 2 A \sin \dfrac{\phi}{2}=A \Rightarrow \phi=\dfrac{\pi}{3}$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Phase and Phase Difference in SHM

question: Two simple pendulum of length $1\,m$ and $4\,m$ respectively are given small linear displacement in one direction at the same time. They will again be in the phase when the pendulum of shorter length has completed oscillations $\left[n T_{1}=(n-1) T_{2}\right.$, where $T_{1}$ is time period of shorter length ${T_2}$ be time period of longer wavelength and $n$ are no. of oscillations completed]
explanation:
Let $T_{1}, T_{2}$ be the time period of shorter length and longer length pendulum respectively. As per question, $n T_{1}=(n-1) T_{2}$;
So $\begin{aligned} n 2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{g}}=(n-1) 2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{4}{g}} \\ n \sqrt{1}=(n-1) \sqrt{4} \Rightarrow n=2\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Phase and Phase Difference in SHM

question: Two particles are executing simple harmonic motion of the same amplitude $A$ and frequency $\omega$ along the $x$-axis. If the maximum separation between them is A the phase difference between their motion is
explanation:
$x_{1}=A \sin \left(\omega t+\phi_{1}\right), x_{2}=A \sin \left(\omega t+\phi_{2}\right)$ $\left|x_{1}-x_{2}\right|=A\left[2 \cos \left(\omega t+\dfrac{\phi_{1}+\phi_{2}}{2}\right) \sin \left(\dfrac{\phi_{1}-\phi_{2}}{2}\right)\right]$
When $\left|x_{1}-x_{2}\right|$ is maximum the variable function $\cos \left(\omega t+\dfrac{\phi_{1}+\phi_{2}}{2}\right)$ should be equal to 1 .
$\begin{aligned}& A=2 A \sin \left(\dfrac{\phi_{1}-\phi_{2}}{2}\right) \\& \dfrac{\phi_{1}-\phi_{2}}{2}=\dfrac{\pi}{6} \Rightarrow \phi_{1}-\phi_{2}=\dfrac{\pi}{3} .\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Phase and Phase Difference in SHM

question: The phase difference between two particles executing SHM of the same amplitudes and frequency along same straight line while passing one another when going in opposite directions with equal displacement from their respective starting point is $2\,\pi /3$. If the phase of one particle is $\pi / 6$, find the displacement at this instant, if amplitude is $A$.
explanation:
Phase difference $\delta=\dfrac{2 \pi}{3}=\delta_{2}-\delta_{1}$
Let $\delta_{1}=\dfrac{\pi}{6}$,
$\begin{aligned}& \therefore \quad \delta_{2}=\left(\dfrac{2 \pi}{3}+\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right)=\dfrac{5 \pi}{6} \\& \therefore \quad \sin \delta_{1}=\sin \delta_{2}=\dfrac{1}{2}\end{aligned}$
Displacement is measured form starting point $(t=0)$
So, $\quad x=A \sin \left(\omega t+\delta_{1}\right)=A \sin \left(0+\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right)=\dfrac{A}{2}$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Phase and Phase Difference in SHM

question: The damping force on an oscillator is directly proportional to the velocity. The units of the constant of proportionality are
explanation:
Given, damping force $\propto$ velocity
$F=k v \Rightarrow k=\dfrac{F}{v}$
Uint of $k=\dfrac{\text { unit of } F}{\text { unit of } v}=\dfrac{k g-m s^{-2}}{m s^{-1}}=k g s^{-1}$
subject: Physics
topic: Damped Simple Harmonic Motion

question: In damped SHM, the SI unit of damping constant is
explanation:
In damped SHM, damping force is proportional to the velocity of the oscillator
i.e., $F_{d} \propto v \Rightarrow F_{d}=b v$
Where $\mathrm{b}$ is a damping constant
$\therefore$ Damping constant, $b=\dfrac{F_{d}}{v}$
SI unit of damping constant
$b = \frac{{{\rm{SI}}\,\,{\rm{unit}}\,\,{\rm{of}}\,\,{\rm{force}}}}{{{\rm{SI}}\,\,{\rm{unit}}\,\,{\rm{of}}\,\,{\rm{velocity}}}} = \frac{{kgm{s^{ - 2}}}}{{\,m{s^{ - 1}}}}$
$=k g \cdot s^{-1}=\dfrac{k g}{s}$
Hence, the SI unit of damping constant is $kg/s$
subject: Physics
topic: Damped Simple Harmonic Motion

question: Which of the following quantity does not change due to damping of oscillations?
explanation: The initial phase $\phi$ of a pendulum during damped oscillation doesn't change
subject: Physics
topic: Damped Simple Harmonic Motion

question: Two particles are executing simple harmonic motion. At an instant of time $t$ their displacements are ${y_1} = a\cos (\omega t)$ and ${y_2} = a\sin (\omega t)$. Then the phase difference between ${y_1}$ and ${y_2}$ is
explanation:
${y_1} = a\cos \omega t$
$ = a\sin \left( {\omega t + \frac{\pi }{2}} \right)$
${y_2} = a\sin \omega t$
$\therefore \Delta \phi = \omega t + \frac{\pi }{2} - \omega t$
$\frac{\pi }{2} = 90^\circ $
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Phase and Phase Difference in SHM

question: Two identical springs of spring constant $k$ each are connected in series and parallel as shown in figure. A mass $M$ is suspended from them. The ratio of their frequencies of vertical oscillation will be
explanation:
${k_s} = \frac{{k \times k}}{{k + k}} = \frac{k}{2}$
${k_p} = k + k = 2k$
$\therefore \frac{{{n_s}}}{{{n_p}}} = \frac{{\frac{1}{{2\pi }}\sqrt {\frac{{{k_s}}}{M}} }}{{\frac{1}{{2\pi }}\sqrt {\frac{{{k_p}}}{M}} }}$
$ = \sqrt {\frac{{{k_s}}}{{{k_p}}}} $
$ = \sqrt {\frac{{k/2}}{{2k}}} = \frac{1}{2}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Oscillation of a Body Due to Combination of Springs

question: Two masses $m_{1}$ and $m_{2}$ are suspended together by a massless spring of spring constant $k$. When the masses are in equilibrium ${m_1}$ is removed without disturbing the system, then the angular frequency of oscillation will be
explanation: $\omega  = \sqrt {\frac{k}{m}} $, here $m = {m_2}$ $\therefore w = \sqrt {\frac{k}{{{m_2}}}} $
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: Three masses $0.1\,kg,0.3\,kg$ and $0.4\,kg$ are suspended at end of a spring. When the $0.4\,kg$ mass is removed, the system oscillates with a period $2\,s$. When the $0.3\,kg$ mass is also removed, the system will oscillate with a period
explanation:
$T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{m}{k}} {\rm{ }}or{\rm{ }}T \propto \sqrt m $
Mass has reduced to $\dfrac{1}{4}$ th the initial mass.
Hence time period will remain half i.e., $\frac{2}{2} = 1$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: Force constant of a weight less spring is $16\,N/m$. A body of mass $1.0\,kg$ suspended from it is pulled down through $5\,cm$ from its mean position and then released. The maximum kinetic energy of the body will be
explanation:
$\omega = \sqrt {\frac{K}{m}} = \sqrt {\frac{{16}}{1}} = 4rad/s$
Now
${K_{\max }} = \frac{1}{2}\,{m^2}{A^2}$
$ = \frac{1}{2} \times 1 \times {(4)^2} \times {\left( {5 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}} \right)^2}$
$ = 2 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,J$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: The period of oscillation of a mass $M$ suspended from a spring of negligiable mass is $T$. If along with it another mass $M$ is also suspended, the period of oscillation will now be
explanation:
Time period of spring pendulum, $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{k}}$.
If now mass in doubled $T^{\prime}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{2 M}{k}}=\sqrt{2} T$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A mass is suspended from a spring vibrating spring constant $k$ is displaced vertically and released, it oscillates with period $T$. The weight of the mass suspended is ( $g = $ gravitational acceleration)
explanation:
Time period of oscillation of the block
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{k}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{T^{2}}{4 \pi^{2}}=\dfrac{m}{k}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950968/preptracker/questions/q_39905_explanation_1764950967932.jpg or $m=\dfrac{T^{2} k}{4 \pi^{2}}$
weight of the mass, $w = mg$
$ = \left( {\frac{{K{T^2}}}{{4{\pi ^2}}}} \right)\left( g \right)$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A tray of mass $12\,kg$ is supported by two identical springs as shown in figure. When the tray is pressed down slightly and then released, it executes SHM with a time period of $1.5\,s$. The spring constant of each spring is
explanation:
Given $m = 12\,kg,\,T = 1.5\,s$
Since, both the springs are connected in parallel then
Spring constant, $K = {k_1} + {k_2}$
Time period, $T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{m}{K}} = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{{12}}{{{k_1} + {k_2}}}} $
$ = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{{12}}{{2k}}} \left( {\because {k_1} = {k_2} = k} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow 1.5 = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{6}{k}} {\rm{ }}or{\rm{ }}k = 105.16\,N{m^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Oscillation of a Body Due to Combination of Springs

question: A $10\,kg$ metal block is attached to a spring of spring constant $1000\,N\,{m^{ - 1}}$. A block is displaced from equilibrium position by $10\,cm$ and released. The maximum acceleration of the block is
explanation:
Given that
$A = 10\,cm = 0.1\,m$
$k = 1000N{m^{ - 1}}$
$m = 10\,kg$
Maximum acceleration of the block is
${a_{\max }} = {\omega ^2}\,A = \frac{{kA}}{m}\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} \left( {\because \omega = \sqrt {\frac{k}{m}} } \right)$
$ = \frac{{1000N{m^{ - 1}} \times 0.1\,m}}{{10\,kg}} = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A mass of $0.2\,kg$ is attached to the lower end of a massless spring of force constant $200\,N/m$, the upper end of which is fixed to a rigid support Which of the following statement is true?
explanation:
${\rm{At}}\,\,{\rm{equilibrium}}\,\,x = \frac{{mg}}{k}$
$ = \frac{{0.2 \times 10}}{{200}} = 0.01\,m = 1\,cm$
$f = \frac{1}{{2\pi }}\sqrt {\frac{K}{m}} = \frac{1}{{2\pi }}\sqrt {\frac{{200}}{{0.2}}} \approx 5.0\,Hz$

options.text: In equilibrium, the spring will be stretched by $1\,cm$
If the mass is raised till the spring becomes unstretched and then released, it will go down by $2\,cm$ before moving upwards
The frequency of oscillation will be nearly $5\,Hz$

subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A man measures the period of a simple pendulum inside a stationary lift and finds it to be $T$ second. If the accelerates upwards with an accelerating $\frac{g}{4}$, then the period of the pendulum will be
explanation:
In stationary lift $T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{l}{{\,g}}} $
In upward moving lift
${T^\prime } = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{l}{{(\,g + a)}}} $
$({\rm{a}}\,\,{\rm{ = }}\,\,{\rm{acceleration of lift}})$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{T^\prime }}}{T}\sqrt {\frac{g}{{(g + a)}}} $
$\sqrt {\frac{g}{{\left( {g + \frac{g}{4}} \right)}}} = \sqrt {\frac{4}{5}} $
$ \Rightarrow T' = \frac{{2T}}{{\sqrt 5 }}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A person measures a time period of a simple pendulum inside a stationary lift and finds it to be $T$. If the lift starts accelerating upwards with an acceleration $\left(\dfrac{g}{3}\right)$, the time period of the pendulum will be
explanation:
Time period of simple pendulum inside a stationary lift,
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}}$
where $l=$ length of string and $g = $ gravitational acceleration Acceleration of lift in upward direction,
$a=\dfrac{g}{3}$
Therefore, time period of pendulum
$\begin{aligned}& T^{\prime}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g+a}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g+\dfrac{g}{3}}} \\& =2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{3 l}{4 g}}=2 \pi \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}} \\& =\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} 2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}}=\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} T \quad \text { [from eq.(1)] }\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: The path length of oscillation of simple pendulum of length $1\,m$ is $16\,cm$. Its maximum velocity is ( take, $g = {\pi ^2}m/{s^2}$)
explanation:
Given,
Length of the pendulum $1\,m$
amplitude $(a) = \frac{{{\rm{path}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{length}}}}{2} = \frac{{16}}{2} = 8\,cm$
Acceleration due to gravity $(g) = {\pi ^2}m/{s^2}$
we know that, time period
$(T) = $ $2\pi \sqrt {\frac{l}{g}} = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{1}{{{\pi ^2}}}} $
$T = \frac{{2\pi }}{\pi }$
$T = 2\,s$
Therefore, Maximum velocity $\left(v_{\max }\right)=a \omega$
$ = a \times \frac{{2\pi }}{T}\left( {\because \omega = \frac{{2\pi }}{T}} \right)$
$ = \frac{{8 \times 2 \times \pi }}{2} = 8\pi cm/s$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: The bob of a simple pendulum performs SHM with period $T$ in air and with period $T_{1}$ in water. Relation between $T$ and $T_{1}$ is ( neglect friction due to water, density of the material of the bob is $ = \frac{9}{8} \times {10^3}kg{m^3}$, density of water $ = 1\,gc{c^{ - 1}}$)
explanation:
Time period of simple pendulum in water is given by
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g_{\text {eff }}}}$
[ symbols have their usual meaningd and $g_{\text {eff }}=$ acceleration due to gravity in water]
As we know, $g_{\text {eff }}=g\left(\dfrac{\sigma-\rho}{\rho}\right)$
[ where, $\sigma=$ density of bob, $\rho=$ density of water] $=9.8\left(\dfrac{\dfrac{9}{8} \times 10^{3}-10^{3}}{\dfrac{9}{8} \times 10^{3}}\right)$
$=9.8\left(\dfrac{\dfrac{9}{8}-1}{\dfrac{9}{8}}\right)=9.8\left(\dfrac{1}{8} \times \dfrac{8}{9}\right)$
$=\dfrac{9.8}{9}$
So, $T_{1}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l \times 9}{9.8}} \Rightarrow T_{1}=3 T$
[ therefore, time period of simple pendulum in air, $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{9.8}}$ ]
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A simple pendulum is oscillating with amplitude A and angular frequency $\omega$. At displacement $x$ from mean position, the ratio of kinetic energy to potential energy is
explanation:
Kinetic energy of pendulum oscillating with amplitude $A$ and angular frequency $\omega$ at displacement $x$ from mean position is
$K E=\dfrac{1}{2} k\left(A^{2}-x^{2}\right)$
Potential energy of pendulum at displacement $x$, from mean position is
$\begin{aligned}P E & =\dfrac{1}{2} k x^{2} \\\therefore \dfrac{K E}{P E} & =\dfrac{\dfrac{1}{2} k\left(A^{2}-x^{2}\right)}{\dfrac{1}{2} k x^{2}}=\dfrac{A^{2}-x^{2}}{x^{2}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question:
A pendulum oscillates simple harmonically and only if
i. The size of the bob of pendulum is negligible in comparison with the length of the pendulum
ii. The angular amplitude is less than $10^{\circ}$
explanation: For a simple pendulum the bob is considered to be a particle and the angle $\theta$ is very small.

options.text: Both i and ii are correct
Both i and ii are incorrect

subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: Two simple pendulums $A$ and $B$ are made to oscillate simultaneously and it is found that $A$ completes 10 oscillations in $20\,s$ and $B$ completes 8 oscillations in $10\,s$. The ratio of the lengths of $A$ and $B$ is
explanation:
Frequency of oscillations made by a simple pendulum of length $l$ is
$f=\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{l}} \Rightarrow f \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{l}}$
Since, $A$ completes 10 oscillations in $20\,s$
$ \Rightarrow {f_A} = \frac{{10}}{{20}} = 0.5\,Hz$
Similarly, ${f_B} = \frac{8}{{10}} = 0.8\,Hz$
$\therefore \dfrac{f_{A}}{f_{B}}=\dfrac{5}{8}=\sqrt{\dfrac{l_{B}}{l_{A}}}$ or $\quad \dfrac{l_{A}}{l_{B}}=\dfrac{64}{25}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A particle undergoing SHM has the equation $x = A\sin (\omega t + \phi )$, where $x$ represents the displacement of the particle. The kinetic energy oscillates with time period
explanation:
Frequency of oscillation of kinetic energy is doubled, i.e., $2 \omega$
$\therefore \mathrm{T}=\dfrac{2 \pi}{2 \omega}=\dfrac{\pi}{\omega}$
subject: Physics
topic: Force, Energy and their relation in Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Force and Energy in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: The quantity which does not vary periodically for a particle performing SHM is
explanation:
For a particle executing simple harmonic motion (SHM)
Total energy $=$ kinetic energy + potential energy
$\begin{aligned}& =E_{K}+E_{P}=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}+\dfrac{1}{2} k x^{2} \\& =\dfrac{1}{m}\left(A^{2}-x^{2}\right)+\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2} A^{2}\end{aligned}$
$\left(\because v=\sqrt{A^{2}-x^{2}}\right.$ where, $A$ is the amplitude of the particle)
$=\dfrac{1}{2} K A^{2}$
subject: Physics
topic: Force, Energy and their relation in Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Force and Energy in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: The ratio of kinetic energy to the potential energy of a particle executing SHM at a distance equal to half its amplitude, the distance being measured from its equilibrium position is
explanation:
The kinetic energy of the particle executing SHM at a distance $x$ from its equilibrium position is
$K = \frac{1}{2} = m{\omega ^2}\left( {\,{A^2} - {x^2}} \right)$
and the potential energy is
$U = \frac{1}{2}\,m{\omega ^2}{x^2}$
At $x = \frac{A}{2} \Rightarrow K = \frac{1}{2}m{\omega ^2}\left( {{A^2} - {{\left( {\frac{A}{2}} \right)}^2}} \right)$
and $U = \frac{1}{2}m{\omega ^2}{\left( {\frac{A}{2}} \right)^2}$
Their corresponding ratio is
$\dfrac{K}{U}=\dfrac{\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2}\left(A^{2}-\left(\dfrac{A}{2}\right)^{2}\right)}{\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2}\left(\dfrac{A}{2}\right)^{2}}=\dfrac{\left(A^{2}-\dfrac{A^{2}}{4}\right)}{\dfrac{A^{2}}{4}}=3$
subject: Physics
topic: Force, Energy and their relation in Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Force and Energy in Simple Harmonic Motion

question:
Which of the following quantities is always negative in SHM?
Here $\mathrm{s}$ is displacement from mean position
explanation: $a$ and $s$ are always in opposite directions
subject: Physics
topic: Force, Energy and their relation in Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Force and Energy in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: The length of second's pendulum on the earth is about $1\,m$. What should be the length of second's pendulum on the moon?
explanation:
$T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{1}{{\,g}}} $. For $T$ to be constant value of $\frac{1}{{\,g}}$ should remain constant.
So if $g_{\text {moon }}=\dfrac{g_{\text {earth }}}{6}$, then $I_{\text {moon }}=\dfrac{I_{\text {earth }}}{6}$,
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: The phase difference between the instantaneous velocity and acceleration of a particle executing simple harmonic motion
explanation:
The displacement equation of a particle executing SHM is
$x=a \cos \omega t$
(i) velocity, $v=\dfrac{d x}{d t}=-a \omega \sin \omega t=a \omega \cos \left(\omega t+\dfrac{\pi}{2}\right)$
(ii) and its acceleration,
$a=\dfrac{d v}{d t}=-a \omega^{2} \cos \omega t=+a \omega^{2} \cos (\omega t+\pi)$
(iii) The phase difference is $\dfrac{\pi}{2}$$ = 0.5{\mkern 1mu} \pi $
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Phase and Phase Difference in SHM

question: A particle executing simple harmonic motion has an amplitude of $6\,cm$. Its acceleration at distance of $2\,cm$ from the mean position is $8\,cm/{s^2}$. The maximum speed of the particle is
explanation:
$a = {\omega ^2}y \Rightarrow \omega = \sqrt {\frac{a}{y}} = \sqrt {\frac{8}{2}} = 2\,rad/s$
Now ${v_{\max {\rm{ }}}} = A\omega = 6 \times 2 = 12\,cm/s$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Velocity and Acceleration in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: Two simple harmonic motions of angular frequency ${\rm{100}}$ and $1000\,rad\,{s^{ - 1}}$ have the same displacement amplitude. The ratio of their maximum acceleration is
explanation:
Acceleration of simple harmonic motion is $a_{\max }=-\omega^{2} A$
or $\dfrac{\left(a_{\max }\right)_{1}}{\left(a_{\max }\right)_{2}}=\dfrac{\omega_{1}^{2}}{\omega_{2}^{2}} \quad$ (as $A$ remains the same) or $\dfrac{\left(a_{\max }\right)_{1}}{\left(a_{\max }\right)_{2}}=\dfrac{(100)^{2}}{(1000)^{2}}=\left(\dfrac{1}{10}\right)^{2}=1: 10^{2}$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Velocity and Acceleration in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A particle is performing a linear simple harmonic motion of amplitude ' $A$ '. When it is midway between its mean and extreme position, the magnitude of its velocity and acceleration are equal. What is the periodic time of the motion?
explanation:
In linear simple harmonic motion, the velocity of particle is given by
$v = \omega \sqrt {{A^2} - {x^2}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
where, $\omega=$ angular frequency
$A = $ maximum displacement of amplitude and
$x = $ displacement from mean position
The acceleration of a particle in simple harmonic motion(SHM), is given by
$a = {\omega ^2}x\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Here, $x=\dfrac{A}{2}$
Alos, $v = a$ (given)
$\omega \sqrt{\left(A^{2}-x^{2}\right)}=\omega^{2} x \quad$ from eq.(1) and (2), we get
$\Rightarrow \sqrt{\left(A^{2}-\dfrac{A^{2}}{4}\right)}=\omega \dfrac{A}{2} \Rightarrow \dfrac{\sqrt{3} A}{2}=\omega \times \dfrac{A}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{2 \pi}{T}=\sqrt{3}\left[\because \omega=\dfrac{2 \pi}{T}\right]$
$\Rightarrow T=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\sqrt{3}} s$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Velocity and Acceleration in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: If ' $x$ ', ' $v$ 's, and a denote the displacement velocity and acceleration of a particle respectively executing SHM of preiodic time $t$, then which one of the following does not change with time?
explanation:
The dimensions of given variables of SHM are as
Displacement $[x]=\left[M^{0} L T^{0}\right]$
Velocity $[v]=\left[M^{0} L T^{-1}\right]$
Acceleration $[a]=\left[M^{0} L T^{-2}\right]$
and time period, $[T]=\left[M^{0} L^{0} T\right]$
Now, checking each option for these values
For option(1)
$\dfrac{[a][T]}{[x]}=\dfrac{\left[M^{0} L T^{-2}\right]\left[M^{0} L^{0} T\right]}{\left[M^{0} L T^{0}\right]}=\left[M^{0} L^{0} T^{-1}\right]$
As it depends on time, so change with it.
For option(2)
$[a][T]+2 \pi[v]=$
$\left[M^{0} L T^{-2}\right]\left[M^{0} L^{0} T\right]+\left[M^{0} L^{0} T^{-1}\right]$
$=\left[M^{0} L T^{-1}\right]$
it is also dependent on time and hence changes with it
For option (3)
$\begin{aligned}\dfrac{[a][T]}{[v]} & =\dfrac{\left[M^{0} L T^{-2}\right]\left[M^{0} L^{0} T\right]}{\left[M^{0} L T^{-1}\right]} \\& =\left[M^{0} L^{0} T^{0}\right]\end{aligned}$
As it is a constant having no dimension, so it does not change in time
For option (4),
$\begin{aligned}& {[a][t]+4 \pi^{2}[d]^{2}=} \\& {\left[M^{0} L T^{-2}\right]\left[M^{0} L^{0} T\right]+\left[M^{0} L T^{-1}\right]^{2}} \\& =\left[L T^{-1}\right]+\left[L^{2} T^{-2}\right]\end{aligned}$
As, the term is dependent on time, so changes with it
Also, it is dimensionally incorrect
Hence option (3) is correct
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Velocity and Acceleration in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A particle executing SHM has maximum speed of $0.5\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ and maximum acceleration of $1.0\,m{s^{ - 2}}$. The angular frequency of oscillation is
explanation:
In SHM, ${a_{\max }} = A{\omega ^2}$ and ${v_{\max }} = A\omega $
$\omega=\dfrac{a_{\max }}{v_{\max }}$
Here, ${a_{\max {\text{ }}}} = 1\,m\,{s^{ - 2}},{v_{\max }} = 0.5\,m\,{s^{ - 1}}$
$\therefore \omega = \frac{1}{{0.5}} = 2{\mkern 1mu} rad\,{s^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Velocity and Acceleration in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A simple pendulum performs simple harmonic motion about $x=0$ with an amplitude $a$ and time period $T$. The speed of the pendulum at $x=a / 2$ will be
explanation:
$v=\omega \sqrt{A^{2}-y^{2}}$
Here $y=\dfrac{a}{2}$
$\therefore v=\omega \sqrt{a^{2}-\dfrac{a^{2}}{4}}=\omega \sqrt{\dfrac{3 a^{2}}{4}}=\dfrac{2 \pi}{T} \dfrac{a \sqrt{3}}{2}=\dfrac{\pi a \sqrt{3}}{T}$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Velocity and Acceleration in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: The equation of a simple harmonic wave is give by $y=3 \sin \dfrac{\pi}{2}(50 t-x)$, where $x$ and $y$ are in meters and $t$ is in seconds. The ratio of maximum particle velocity to the wave velocity is
explanation:
We now that $v_{\max }=a \omega$ and $v=\lambda f$
$\begin{aligned}& \therefore \dfrac{v_{\max }}{v}=\dfrac{a \omega}{f \lambda}=\dfrac{a(2 \pi f)}{f \lambda}=\dfrac{2 \pi a}{\lambda} \\& \dfrac{2 \pi a}{2 \pi / k}=k a=\dfrac{\pi}{2} \times 3=\dfrac{3 \pi}{2}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Velocity and Acceleration in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: For a particle performing linear SHM, its average speed over one oscillation is ( where $a$ = amplitude of SHM, $n = $ frequency of oscillation)
explanation:
Given, amplitude of $SHM = a$
frequency of oscillation $=n$
Distance travelled in one oscillation $ = 4x$ amplitude of $SHM = 4a$
We know that,
velocity $=\dfrac{\text { distance travelled in one oscillation }}{\text { TimePeriod }}$
$\begin{aligned}v & =\dfrac{4 a}{T} \\\therefore v & =4 a n \quad\left[\because \text { frequency, } n=\dfrac{1}{T}\right]\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Velocity and Acceleration in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A particle executes the simple harmonic motion with an amplitude 'A'. The distance travelled by it in one periodic time is
explanation:
In simple harmonic motion total distance travelled is $4\,A$.
This is because the particle covers $A$ in $\dfrac{T}{4}$ time period so in complete $T$ time it covers 4 times the amplitude distance i.e., $4A$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A particle performing SHM starts equilibrium position and its time period is 16 seconds. After 2 seconds its velocity is $\pi m / s$. Amplitude of oscillation is $\left(\cos 45^{\circ}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$
explanation:
Given, $v = \pi m/s,\,T = 16\,s$
Displacement of the particle $=\mathrm{x}=A \sin \omega t$
Velocity of particle $ = {\rm{v}} = \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = A\omega \cos \omega t\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Angular velocity, $\omega = \frac{{2\pi }}{T} = \frac{{2\pi }}{{16}} = \frac{\pi }{8}rad/s$
substituting the values in eq.(1), we get
$\pi = A \times \frac{\pi }{8} \times \cos \frac{\pi }{8} \times 2$
$ \Rightarrow 1 = \frac{A}{8} \times \cos \frac{\pi }{4}$
$ = \frac{A}{8}\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }} \Rightarrow A = 8\sqrt 2 \,m$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion

question: The circular motion of a particle with constant speed is
explanation: The circular motion of a particle with constant speed is periodic but not SHM.
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Simple Harmonic Motion and Uniform Circular Motion

question:
Two particles are executing SHMs. The equations of their motions are
${y_1} = 10\sin \left( {\omega t + \frac{\pi }{4}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,{y_2} = 5\sin \left( {\omega t + \frac{{\sqrt 3 \pi }}{4}} \right)$
What is the ratio of their amplitudes.
explanation:
There is no effect of phase angle on amplitude. hence
$\dfrac{\mathrm{A}_{1}}{\mathrm{~A}_{2}}=\dfrac{10}{5}=\dfrac{2}{1}$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A particle executes SHM with amplitude $0.2\,m$ and time period $24\,s$. The time required for it to move from the mean position to a point $0.1\,m$ from the mean position is
explanation:
Given that
$A = 0.2\,m,\,T = 24\,s$
Displacement $x$ of a particle from its mean position is given by
$x = {\rm{ }}A\sin {\rm{ }}\omega t$
${\rm{Here}},{\rm{ }}x = 0.1\,m$
$\therefore 0.1 = 0.2\sin \omega t$
$\frac{1}{2} = \sin \omega t$
$ \Rightarrow \omega t = \frac{\pi }{6}t = \frac{\pi }{{6\omega }} = \frac{\pi }{6}\left( {\frac{T}{{2\pi }}} \right)\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} \left( {\because \omega = \frac{{2\pi }}{T}} \right)$
$ = \frac{{\pi \times 24}}{{6 \times 2\pi }} = 2\,s$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion

question: Five identical springs are used in the three configuration as shown in figure. The time period of vertical oscillations in configuration (a), (b) and (c) are in the ratio
explanation:
${T_a} = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{m}{k}} $
${T_b} = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{m}{{k/2}}} $
${T_c} = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{m}{{2k}}} $

question: Two bodies $A$ and $B$ of equal mass are suspended from two separate massles springs of force constant $k_{1}$ and $k_{2}$ respectively. The bodies oscillate vertically such that their maximum velocities are equal. The ratio of the amplitudes of body $A$ to that of body $B$ is
explanation:
The maximum velocity of body in SHM
$v=A \omega$
Where, A is amplitude of body and
$\omega=$ angular frequency
It is given that, the maximum velocities of bodies are equal
$\begin{aligned}v_{A} & =v_{B} \\A_{A} \omega_{A} & =A_{B} \omega_{B} \\A_{A} \sqrt{\dfrac{k_{1}}{m}} & =A_{B} \sqrt{\dfrac{k_{2}}{m}}\left(\because \omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}\right) \\\dfrac{A_{A}}{A_{B}} & =\sqrt{\dfrac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A block of mass $m$ attached to one end of the vertical spring produces extension $x$. If the block is pulled and released, the periodic time of oscillation is
explanation:
For vertical spring, the periodic time of oscillation is
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{k}}$
As, restoring force, $|F|=k x$
$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow k=\dfrac{F}{x}=\dfrac{m g}{x} \\& \therefore T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{\dfrac{m}{m g}}{x}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{x}{g}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: In SHM phase difference between displacement and velocity is $\phi_{1}$ and that between displacement and acceleration is $\phi_{2}$, then
explanation:
$\phi_{1}=\dfrac{\pi}{2}$ and $\phi_{2}=\pi$
$=\dfrac{\pi}{2}=90^{\circ}$
$\therefore {\phi _2} = 2{\phi _1}$
subject: Physics

subtopic: Phase and Phase Difference in SHM

question: A body of mass $64\,g$ is made to oscillate turn by turn on two different springs $A$ and $B$. Spring $A$ and $B$ has force constant $4 \dfrac{N}{m}$ and $16 \dfrac{N}{m}$ respectively. If $T_{1}$ and $T_{2}$ are period of oscillations of spring $A$ and $B$ respectively, then $\dfrac{T_{1}+T_{2}}{T_{1}-T_{2}}$ will be
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A mass $m_{1}$ connected to a horizontal spring perform SHM with amplitude $A$. While mass $m_{1}$ is passing through mean position, another mass $m_{2}$ is placed on it so that both the masses move together with amplitude $A_{1}$. The ratio of $\dfrac{A_{1}}{A}$ is $\left(m_{2} < m_{1}\right)$
explanation:
Potential energy of oscillating mass is given by
$P E=\dfrac{1}{2} k x^{2}$
When only one block is oscillating at $x = A$
$E_{\max }=\dfrac{1}{2} k A^{2}$ and at mean position $x = 0$. So,
$E = 0$
$\therefore A \propto \frac{1}{{\sqrt m }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
When mass $m_{2}$ is placed on top of the mass $m$. Then, total mass is $\left(m_{1}+m_{2}\right)$ and $E = 0$ at this point, as $x = 0$
When they reaches at $x = A$,
then ${A_1} \propto \frac{1}{{\sqrt {{m_1} + {m_2}} }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Dividing eq.(2) by (1)
$\dfrac{A_{1}}{A}=\left(\dfrac{m_{1}}{m_{1}+m_{2}}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A piston is performing S.H.M. in the vertical direction with a frequency of $0.5\,Hz$. A block of $10\,kg$ is placed on the piston. The maximum amplitude of the system such that the block remains in contact with the piston is
explanation:
For maximum amplitude, ${\omega ^2}\,A = g$
${(2\pi \times 0.5)^2} \times A = 10$
$\therefore A = \frac{{10}}{{{{(2\pi \times 0.5)}^2}}} = 1\,m$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A clock pendulum having coefficient of linear expansion, $\alpha=9 \times 10^{7} /{ }^{\circ} C^{-1}$ has a period of 0.5 $s$ at ${20^{\rm{o}}}C$. If the clock is used in a climate, where the temperature is ${30^{\rm{o}}}C$, how much time does the clock lose in each oscillation ? $g = $ constant)
explanation:
Given, the coefficient of linear expansion
$\alpha=9 \times 10^{-7 o} C^{-1}$
Initial time period ${T_0} = 0.5\,s$,
initial temperature ${T_i} = {\rm{ }}{20^{\rm{o}}}C$
final temperature ${T_i} = {\rm{ }}{30^{\rm{o}}}C$
Expansion in length $\Delta l=l \alpha\left(T_{f}-T_{i}\right)$
$\Delta l=l \times 9 \times 10^{-7}(30-20)$
Now, the time period of pendulum
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}}$
Error in time period
$\dfrac{\Delta t}{T}=\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{\Delta l}{l}+\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{\Delta g}{g}$
Since $\Delta g=0$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\Delta T}{T}=\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{\Delta l}{l}$
Now, substituting values in the above equation, we get,
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{\Delta T}}{{0.5}} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {\frac{{l \times 9 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}}{l}(30 - 20)} \right]$
$ \Rightarrow \Delta T = \frac{{0.5 \times 9 \times {{10}^{ - 7}} \times 10}}{2}$
$ \Rightarrow \Delta T = 2.25 \times {10^{ - 6}}\,s$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: Two pendulum begin to swing simultaneously. The first pendulum makes nine full oscillations when the other makes seven. The ratio of the lengths of the two pendulums is
explanation:
As two pendulums being to swing simultaneously, then
${n_1}{T_1} = {n_2}{T_2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Where $n_{1}$ and $n_{2}$ are the number of oscillations of first and second pendulums respectively and $T_{1}$ and $T_{2}$ be their respective time periods
The time period of simple pendulum is given by
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}}$
where $l = $ length of pendulum
and $g = $ acceleration due to gravity
$ \Rightarrow {T^2} \propto l\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
So, from eq.(1) and (2), we get
$\dfrac{l_{1}}{l_{2}}=\dfrac{T_{1}^{2}}{T_{2}^{2}}=\dfrac{n_{2}^{2}}{n_{1}^{2}}$
Here $n_{1}=9, n_{2}=7$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{l_{1}}{l_{2}}=\dfrac{(7)^{2}}{(9)^{2}}=\dfrac{49}{81}$
Hence the ratio of pendulums length
$l_{1}: l_{2}=49: 81$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A bob of a simple pendulum has mass $m$ and is oscillating with an amplitude $a$. If the length of the pendulum is $L$, then the maximum tension in the string is [$\cos \,{0^{\rm{o}}} = 1,\,g$ acceleration due to gravity]
explanation:
Consider the figure shown below https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956100/preptracker/questions/q_39750_explanation_1764956099891.jpg The string posses maximum tension when bob is at mean position of oscillation i.e., at position $R$
From geometry, $O P=\sqrt{L^{2}-a^{2}}$
Also, $RP = OR - OP$
$=L-\sqrt{L^{2}-a^{2}}$
The whole kinetic energy of bob at position $R$ is converted into its potential energy at position $B$
$\begin{gathered}\therefore \dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=m g\left(L-\sqrt{L^{2}-a^{2}}\right) \\v^{2}=2 g\left(L-\sqrt{L^{2}-a^{2}}\right)\end{gathered}$
Balancing forces at $R$,
$\begin{aligned}& T-m g=\dfrac{m v^{2}}{l}=\dfrac{2 m g\left(L-\sqrt{L^{2}-a^{2}}\right)}{L} \\& \therefore T=m g+2 m g\left(1-\sqrt{1-\dfrac{a^{2}}{L^{2}}}\right)\end{aligned}$
Using approximation,
$\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} = 1 - \frac{{{x^2}}}{2}{\rm{ for }}x < < 1,$
we get
$\begin{aligned}T & =m g+2 m g\left[1-\left(1-\dfrac{a^{2}}{2 L^{2}}\right)\right] \\& =m g+m g\left(\dfrac{a}{L}\right)^{2}=m g\left[1+\left(\dfrac{a}{L}\right)^{2}\right]\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: The particle executing simple harmonic motion has a kinetic energy $K_{0} \cos ^{2} \omega t$. The maximum values of the potential energy and the total energy are respectively:
explanation:
In simple harmonic motion, the total energy of the particle is constant at all instants which is totally kinetic when particle is passing through the mean position and is totally potential when particle is passing through the extreme position.
Now, $E_{k}=K_{0} \cos ^{2} \omega t$
$\therefore {\left( {{E_K}} \right)_{Max}} = {K_0}$
So, $\left(E_{p}\right)_{\text {Max }}=K_{0}$ and $(E)_{\text {total }}=K_{0}$
subject: Physics
topic: Force, Energy and their relation in Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Force and Energy in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: The total energy of a simple harmonic oscillator is proportional to
explanation:
Total energy of simple harmonic oscillator is given by
$E=\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2} A^{2}$
where, $m = $ mass of body performing SHM
$\omega=$ angular velocity and $A = $ amplitude
$\therefore E \propto A^{2}$
subject: Physics
topic: Force, Energy and their relation in Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Force and Energy in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: For a particle performing SHM when displacement is $x$, the potential energy and restoring force acting on it is denoted by $E$ and $F$ respectively. The relation between $x,E$ and $F$ is
explanation:
The restoring force acting on the particle in SHM,
$F = - kx\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
The potential energy in SHM, $E = \frac{1}{2}k{x^2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From eq.(1) and (2), we get
$\begin{aligned}& E=\dfrac{1}{2} \times\left(\dfrac{-F}{x}\right) \times x^{2}=-\dfrac{F x}{2} \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{2 E}{F}+x=0\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Force, Energy and their relation in Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Force and Energy in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: The amplitude and the time period in a SHM is $0.5\,cm$ and $0.4\,s$ respectively. If the initial phase is $\frac{\pi }{2}rad$, then the equation of SHM will be
explanation:
$y = a\sin (\omega t + \phi ) = a\sin \left( {\frac{{2\pi }}{T}t + \phi } \right)$
$ \Rightarrow y = 0.5\sin \left( {\frac{{2\pi }}{{0.4}}t + \frac{\pi }{2}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow y = 0.5\sin \left( {5\pi t + \frac{\pi }{2}} \right)$
$ = 0.5\cos 5\pi t$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion

question: Two particles are oscillating along two close parallel straight lines side by side, with the same frequency and amplitudes. They pass each other, moving in opposite directions when their displacement is half of the amplitude. The mean positions of the two particles lie on a straight line perpendicular to the paths of the two particles. The phase difference is
explanation:
Equation of SHM is given by $x=A \sin (\omega t+\delta)$
when $x=\dfrac{A}{2}$, then
$\sin (\omega t+\delta)=\dfrac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \omega t+\delta=\dfrac{\pi}{6}$ or $\phi_{1}=\dfrac{\pi}{6}$
For second particle, $\phi_{2}=\pi-\dfrac{\pi}{6}=\dfrac{5 \pi}{6}$
$\therefore \quad \phi=\phi_{2}-\phi_{1}=\dfrac{4 \pi}{6}==\dfrac{2 \pi}{3}$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Phase and Phase Difference in SHM

question:
What is the maximum acceleration of the particle doing the SHM?
$y = 2\sin \left[ {\frac{{\pi t}}{2} + \phi } \right]{\rm{where}}\,\,y\,\,{\rm{is}}\,\,{\rm{in}}\,\,cm$
explanation:
$\omega = \frac{\pi }{2}rad/s$ and $A = 2\,cm$
$\therefore$ Maximum acceleration $ = {\omega ^2}\,A = \frac{{{\pi ^2}}}{2}\,cm/{s^2}$

question:
The oscillation of a body on a smooth horizontal surface is reprsented by the equation.
$x=A \cos (\omega t)$
where $x=$ displacement at time $t$
$\omega=\text { frequency of oscillation }$
Which one of the following graphs shows correctly the variation $a$ with $t$ ?
Here $a=$ acceleration at time $t$
$T = {\rm{ time period}}$
explanation:
Displacement, $x=A \cos (\omega t)($ given $)$
Velocity, $v=\dfrac{d x}{d t}=-A \omega \sin (\omega t)$
Acceleration, $a=\dfrac{d x}{d t}=-A \omega^{2} \cos (\omega t)$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950677/preptracker/questions/q_39718_explanation_1764950677102.png Hence graph (3) correctly depicts the variation of $a$ with $t$.
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Velocity and Acceleration in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A particle performs linear SHM at a particular instant, velocity of the particle is ' $u$ ' and acceleration is ' $\alpha$ ' while at another instant velocity is ' $v$ ' and acceleration is $\beta(0 < \alpha < \beta)$. The distance between the two positions is
explanation:
Let the distance be $p$ when velocity is $u$ and acceleration $\alpha$
Let the distance $q$ when velocity is $v$ and acceleration $\beta$
If $\omega$ is the angular frequency, then
$\alpha = {\omega ^2}p{\rm{ and }}\beta = {\omega ^2}p$
$\therefore \alpha + \beta = {\omega ^2}(p + q)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Also, $u^{2}=\omega^{2} A^{2}-\omega^{2} p^{2}$
and $v^{2}=\omega^{2} A^{2}-\omega^{2} q^{2}$
$ \Rightarrow {v^2} - {u^2} = {\omega ^2}\left( {{p^2} - {q^2}} \right)$
${v^2} - {u^2} = {\omega ^2}(p - q)(p + q)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
By eqs.(1) and (2), we get
$\begin{aligned}v^{2}-u^{2} & =(p-q)(\alpha+\beta) \\\therefore p-q & =\dfrac{v^{2}-u^{2}}{\alpha+\beta} \text { or } q-p=\dfrac{u^{2}-v^{2}}{\alpha+\beta}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Velocity and Acceleration in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A point performs simple harmonic oscillation of period $T$ and the equation of motion is given by $x=a \sin (\omega t+\pi / 6)$. After the elapse of what fraction of the time period the velocity of the point will be equal to half of its maximum velocity?
explanation:
Velocity is the time dervative of displacement.
Writing the given equation of a point performing SHM
$x = a\sin \left( {\omega t + \frac{\pi }{6}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Differentiating Eq. (1), w.r.t. time, we obtain
$v=\dfrac{d x}{d t}=a \omega \cos \left(\omega t+\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right)$
It is given that $v=\dfrac{a \omega}{2}$, so that
$\dfrac{a \omega}{2}=a \omega \cos \left(\omega t+\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right)$
$\begin{aligned}\cos \dfrac{\pi}{3} & =\cos \left(\omega t+\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right) \\\Rightarrow \quad t & =\dfrac{\pi}{6 \omega}=\dfrac{\pi \times T}{6 \times 2 \pi}=\dfrac{T}{12}\end{aligned}$
Thus, at $T/12$ velocity of the point will be equal to half of its maximum velocity.
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Velocity and Acceleration in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: Which one of the following equations of motion represents simple harmonic motion?
explanation:
Acceleration $\propto-$ (displacement).
$a \propto - y \Rightarrow a = - {\omega ^2}y$
$a = - \frac{k}{m}y{\text{ Here, }}y = x + a{\text{ }}and{\text{ }}m = 1$
$\therefore \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {\text{ Acceleration }} = - k(x + a)$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A particle performs SHM with amplitude $25\,cm$ and period $3\,s$. The minimum time required for it to move between two points $12.5\,cm$ on either side of the mean position is
explanation:
Here, amplitude of particle, $A = 25\,cm$ and time period, $T = 3\,s$
If the particle at $t = 0$ is at mean position, its displacement equation will be
$x=A \sin \omega t$
i.e., $x=25 \sin \dfrac{2 \pi t}{3}\left[\because \omega=2 \pi v=\dfrac{2 \pi}{T}=\dfrac{2 \pi}{3}\right]$
If it takes time $t_{1}$ to move a distance $x = 12.5\,cm$ to one side of its mean position, then
$12.5=25 \sin \dfrac{2 \pi t_{1}}{3}$
or $\dfrac{1}{2}=\sin \dfrac{2 \pi t_{1}}{3}$ or $\sin \dfrac{\pi}{6}=\sin \dfrac{2 \pi t_{1}}{3}$
$\therefore \dfrac{\pi}{6}=\dfrac{2 \pi t_{1}}{3} \Rightarrow t_{1}=\dfrac{1}{4} s$
The same will be the time to move $12.5\,cm$ to the other side of its mean position, therefore, total time
$t = {t_1} + {t_2} = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{4} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5\,s$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion

question: Molten - wax of mass m drops on a block of mass $M$, which is oscillating on a frictionless table as shown. Select the incorrect option
explanation:
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{K}}$ or $T \propto \sqrt{m}$
Mass is increasing therefore time period will increase.

options.text: If the collision takes place at extreme position, amplitude does not change
If the collision takes place at mean position, amplitude decreases
If the collision takes place at mean position, time period decreases
If the collision takes place at extreme position, time period increases

subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A particle is executing SHM of periodic time $T$. The time taken by a particle in moving from mean position to half the maximum displacement is $\left( {\sin {{30}^o} = 0.5} \right)$
explanation:
If the particle at $t = 0\,s$ is at mean position, the maximum displacement is given by
$x=A \sin \dfrac{2 \pi t}{T}$
If it takes time $t_{1}$ to move from mean position to half the displacemnt, then
$\frac{A}{2} = A\sin \frac{{2\pi t}}{T}{\rm{ or }}\frac{1}{2} = \sin \frac{{2\pi t}}{T}{\rm{ }}$
${\rm{or }}\sin {30^{\rm{o}}} = \sin \frac{{2\pi t}}{T}\,\,\,1pt\sin \,{30^{\rm{o}}} = \frac{1}{2}$
$\sin \frac{\pi }{6} = \sin \frac{{2\pi t}}{T}$
$\frac{\pi }{6} = \frac{{2\pi t}}{T}$
$ \Rightarrow t = \frac{T}{{12}}s$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A particle excuting a simple harmonic motion has a period of $6{\rm{ }}\sec $. The time taken by the particle to move from the mean position to half the amplitude, starting from the mean position is
explanation:
Since the motion is started from the mean position, of the particle is given by $x = A\sin \omega t$
$At{\rm{ }}x = \frac{A}{2}$
$\therefore \frac{A}{2} = A\sin \omega t \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} = \sin \omega t \Rightarrow \frac{\pi }{6} = \omega t$
$t = \frac{\pi }{{6\omega }} = \frac{\pi }{{6\left( {\frac{{2\pi }}{T}} \right)}}\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} \left( {\because \omega = \frac{{2\pi }}{T}} \right)$
$ = \frac{T}{{12}} = \frac{6}{{12}} = \frac{1}{2}s$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion

question: Two simple harmonic motions are represented by ${y_1} = 5\left[ {\sin 2\pi \,t + \sqrt 3 \cos 2\pi \,t} \right]$ and ${y_2} = 5\sin \left( {2\pi \,t + \frac{\pi }{4}} \right).$ The ratio of their amplitudes is
explanation:
$y_{1}=5[\sin 2 \pi t+\sqrt{3} \cos 2 \pi t]$
$\begin{aligned}& =10\left[\dfrac{1}{2} \sin 2 \pi t+\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cos 2 \pi t\right] \\& =10\left[\cos \dfrac{\pi}{3} \sin 2 \pi t+\cos \dfrac{\pi}{3} \cos 2 \pi t\right] \\& =10\left[\sin \left(2 \pi t+\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)\right]\end{aligned}$
$\therefore {A_1} = 10$
${y_2} = 5\sin \left( {2\pi t + \frac{\pi }{4}} \right)$
$\therefore {A_2} = 5$, Hence $\dfrac{A_{1}}{A_{2}}=\dfrac{2}{1}$

question: The phase difference between displacement and acceleraton of a particle in a simple harmonic motion is :
explanation:
Displacement of SHM is
$x = A\sin (\omega t + \phi )\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
$\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = A\omega \cos (\omega t + \phi )$
$a = - {\omega ^2}A\sin (\omega t + \phi )\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
$a = {\omega ^2}A\sin (\omega t + \phi + \pi )$
From (1) & (2), phase difference between displacement and accelertion is $\pi$.

question: Average velocity of a particle executing SHM in one complete vibration is :
explanation:

$ = \frac{{{\rm{ Displacement }}}}{{{\rm{ Time}}\,\,{\rm{interval }}}} = 0$

question:
The displacement of a particle executing simple harmonic motion is given by
$y=A_{0}+A \sin \omega t+B \cos \omega t$
Then the amplitude of its oscillation is given by :
explanation:
$Y=A_{0}+A \sin \omega t+B \sin \omega t$
$=A_{0}+R \sin (\omega t+\phi)$
Resultant amplitude
$\begin{aligned}R & =\sqrt{A^{2}+B^{2}+2 A B \cos 90^{\circ}} \\& =\sqrt{A^{2}+B^{2}}\end{aligned}$

question: A pendulum is hung from the roof of a sufficiently high building and is moving freely to and fro like a simple harmonic oscillator. The acceleration of the bob of the pendulum is $20\,m/{s^2}$ at a distance of $5\,m$ from the mean position. The time period of oscillation is
explanation:

$\begin{aligned}& 20=\omega^{2} \times 5 \Rightarrow \omega=2 \\& T=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\omega}=\pi\end{aligned}$

question:
Assertion: The bob of simple pendulum is a ball full of water. If a fine hole is made at the bottom of the ball, then the time period will no more remain constant.


Reason: The time period of simple pendulum does not depend upon mass.
explanation: When water flows out of the ball the centre of mass comes down from the centre of the ball. So, the effective length of the ball increase, hence the time period of pendulum also increase. After the water is completely drained, centre of mass of ball is at its geometric centre. So, effective length of pendulum decreases and the time period of pendulum also decreases hence, time period does not remain constant. Time period of a simple pendulum does not depend upon the mass of bob as $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}}$. So option (2) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: The graph between velocity and displacement for a harmonic oscillator is a parabola.


Reason: Velocity does not change uniformly with displacement in simple harmonic motion.
explanation:
While executing SHM, from relation,
$\begin{aligned}& v=\omega \sqrt{A^{2}-x^{2}} \text { or } v^{2}=\omega^{2}\left(A^{2}-x^{2}\right) \\& v^{2}=\omega^{2} A^{2}-\omega^{2} x^{2}\end{aligned}$
Dividing both side by $\omega^{2} A^{2}$
$\frac{{{v^2}}}{{{\omega ^2}{A^2}}} + \frac{{{x^2}}}{{{A^2}}} = 1\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Eq. (1) represent the equation of an ellipse. InSHM, the velocity does not change uniformly. So option (4) is correct.

Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

question:
Assertion: For an oscillating simple pendulum, the tension in the string is maximum at the mean position and minimum at the extreme position.


Reason: The velocity of oscillating bob in simple harmonic motion is maximum at the mean position.
explanation:
In simple pendulum, when bob is in deflection position, the tension in the string is
$T=m g \cos \theta+\dfrac{m v^{2}}{l}$. Since the value of $\theta$ is
different at different positions, hence tension in the string is not constant throughout the oscillation. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956094/preptracker/questions/q_39624_explanation_1764956094547.jpg At end points $\theta$ is maximum; the value of $\cos \theta$ is least, hence the value of tension in the string is least. At the mean position, the value of $\theta=0^{\circ}$ and $\cos 0^{\circ}=1$, so the value of tension is largest.
Also velocity is given by $v=\omega \sqrt{a^{2}-x^{2}}$ which is maximum when $x=0$, at mean position.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.

question:
$x=A \cos \omega t$ and $x=A \sin \omega t$ can represent same motion with different initial positions.
If the argument of $x=A \sin \omega t$, i.e., $\omega t$ is increased by $2 \pi$ radian the value of $x$ remains same.
explanation:
$x=A \cos \omega t$ and $x=A \sin \omega t$ both represent the displacement of particle undergoing periodic motion,
At $t=0$,
If $x=A$, we can represents its displacement by
$x=A \cos \omega t$
and if $x=0$, we can represent its displacement by
$x=A \sin \omega t$
This implies, both $x=A \cos \omega t$ and $x=A \sin \omega t$ represents the same motion with different initial positions.
Since, $x=A \sin \omega t$ represents a periodic function with time period $2{\mkern 1mu} \pi \,rad$. So, if the argument of this function $\omega t$ is increased by an integral multiple of $2{\mkern 1mu} \pi \,rad$, the value of the function remains the same.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

question: Figure shows the circular motion of a particle. The radius of the circle, the period, sense of revolution and the initial position are indicated on the figure. The simple harmonic motion of the $x$-projection of the radius vetor of the rotating particle $P$ is
explanation:

Let the particle go from $P$ to $Q$ in time $t$.
Then $\angle P O Q=\omega t=\angle O Q P$. The projection of radius $OQ$ on $x$-axis will be $OR = x(t)$ say. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956967/preptracker/questions/q_39573_explanation_1764956966835.jpg $\sin \omega t=\dfrac{x(t)}{B}$
$x(t)=B \sin \omega t=B \sin \dfrac{2 \pi}{T} t=B \sin \dfrac{2 \pi}{30} t$

question: The piston in the cylinder head of a locomotive has a stroke (twice the amplitude) of $1.0\,m$. If the piston moves with simple harmonic motion with an angular frequency of $200\,rad/\min $, what is its maximum speed?
