$\omega = 200\,rad/\min $
Stroke length $ = 1\,m$
$\therefore$ Amplitude of SHM,
$A = \frac{{{\rm{ }}Stroke{\rm{ }}length{\rm{ }}}}{2} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5\,m$
Now, ${v_{\max }} = \omega A = 200 \times 0.5 = 100\,m/\min $

explanation:

question: The displacement of a particle is represented by the equation $y=3 \cos \left(\dfrac{\pi}{4}-2 \omega t\right)$. The motion of the particle is
explanation:
Given,
$y = 3\cos \left( {\frac{\pi }{4} - 2\omega t} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Velocity, $v=\dfrac{d y}{d t}=3 \times 2 \omega \sin \left(\dfrac{\pi}{4}-2 \omega t\right)$
Acceleration,
$a=\dfrac{d v}{d t}=-4 \omega^{2} \times 3 \cos \left(\dfrac{\pi}{4}-2 \omega t\right)=-4 \omega^{2} y$
As $A \propto-y$ hence particle will execute SHM comparing Eq. (i) with equation
$y=A \cos \left(\phi-\omega^{\prime} t\right)$
we have, $\omega^{\prime}=2 \omega$ or $\dfrac{2 \pi}{T^{\prime}}=2 \omega$ or $T^{\prime}=\dfrac{\pi}{\omega}$

question: A graph of the square of the velocity against the square of the acceleration of a given simple harmonic motion is
explanation:
Let the equation of SHM is $x=A \sin \omega t$
$\begin{aligned}& v=\dfrac{d x}{d t}=A \omega \cos \omega t=\omega \sqrt{A^{2}-x^{2}} \\& a=\dfrac{d v}{d t}=-A \omega^{2} \sin \omega t=-\omega^{2} x\end{aligned}$
But $\quad x=-\dfrac{a}{\omega^{2}}$
$\therefore v=\omega \sqrt{A^{2}-\dfrac{a^{2}}{\omega^{4}}} \Rightarrow v^{2}=\omega^{2}\left(A^{2}-\dfrac{a^{2}}{\omega^{4}}\right)$
The graph between $v^{2}$ and $a^{2}$ is a straight line with (-)ve slope.

question: If a simple pendulum has significant amplitude (up to a factor of 1/e of original) only in the period between $t=0 s$ to $t=\tau s$, then $\tau$ may be called the average life of the pendulum. When the spherical bob of the pendulum suffers a retardation (due to viscous drag) proportional to its velocity with $\mathrm{b}$ as the constant of proportionality, the average life time of the pendulum in second is (assuming damping is small) mass of bob is $m$.
explanation:
For damped oscillations
$\begin{aligned}& x=a_{0} e^{-(b t / 2 m)} \cos (\omega t+\phi) \\& \therefore \quad a=a_{0} e^{-(b t / 2 m)}\end{aligned}$
Given that $a=\dfrac{a_{o}}{e}$ when $t=\tau$ $\therefore \dfrac{a_{0}}{e}=a_{0} e^{-(b \tau / 2 m)} \Rightarrow \tau=\dfrac{2 m}{b}$.

question: The amplitude of damped oscillator reduced to one-third of its initial value $A_{0}$ in $2\,s$. Its amplitude after $6\,s$ is $1/n$ times the original. Then $n$ is equal to
explanation:
For damped motion $A=A_{0} e^{-\left(\dfrac{b t}{2 m}\right)}=A_{0} e^{-P t}$
where $P=\dfrac{b}{2 m}$
For first case, $\dfrac{A_{0}}{3}=A_{0} e^{-2 P}$
or $\quad \dfrac{1}{3}=e^{-2 P}$
For second case, $\dfrac{A_{0}}{n}=A_{0} e^{-6 P}$
or $\dfrac{1}{n}=e^{-6 P}=\left(e^{-2 P}\right)^{3}=\left(\dfrac{1}{3}\right)^{3} \Rightarrow n=3^{3}$
subject: Physics
topic: Damped Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A round metal hoop is suspended on the edge by a hook. The hoop can oscillate side to side in the plane of the hoop, or it can oscillate back and fourth in a direction perpendicular to the plane of the hoop. For which mode will the frequency of oscillation be larger?
explanation:
$T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{I}{{mgR}}} $
For side by side oscillations
$I_{1}=2 m R^{2}$ (Passing the centre and plane)
$I_{2}=\dfrac{m R^{2}}{2}($ Passing through edge $)$
As $I_{1}>I_{2} \Rightarrow T_{1}>T_{2} \Rightarrow f_{1} < f_{2}$

options.text: The frequency of oscillation will be the same in either mode
Oscillations in the plane of the hoop
Oscillations perpendicular to the plane of the hoop

question: A tunnel is made across the earth of radius $R$, passing through its centre. A ball is dropped from a height h in the tunnel. the motion will be periodic with time period.
explanation:
When the ball $m$ falls from a height $h$, it reaches the surface of earth in time, $t=\sqrt{2 h / g}$. Its velocity is $v=\sqrt{2 g h}$. It then moves into the tunnel and reaches on the other side of earth and goes again upto a height $h$ on the other side of earth. The ball again returns back and thus executes periodic motion. Outside the earth ball crosses distance $h$ four times. When the ball is in the tunnel at distance $x$ from the centre of the earth, then gravitational force acting on ball is
$\begin{aligned}& \therefore \quad F=\dfrac{G M m x}{R^{3}} \\& \text { i.e., } F \propto x\end{aligned}$
As, this force, $F$ is directed towards the centre of earth i.e., the mean position. So, the ball will execute periodic motion about the centre of earth.
Here, inertia factor $=$ mass of ball $=m$
$\text { Spring factor }=\dfrac{G M m}{R^{3}}=\dfrac{g m}{R}$
$\therefore$ Time period of oscillation of ball in the tunnel is
$\begin{aligned}T^{\prime} & =2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{\text { inertia factor }}{\text { spring factor }}} \\& =2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{g m / R}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{R}{g}}\end{aligned}$
Time spent by ball outside the tunnel on both the sides will be $=4 \sqrt{2 h / g}$
Therefore, total time period of oscillation of ball is
$=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{R}{g}}+4 \sqrt{\dfrac{2 h}{g}}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A wooden cube (density of wood '$d$') of side ' $l$ ' floats in a liquid of density '$\rho$' with its upper and lower surface horizontal. If the cube is pushed slightly down and relased, it performs simple harmonic motion of period '$T$' to
explanation:
At equilibrium $F_{b}=m g$
$\rho A l_{0} g=d l g$
Restoring force,
$\begin{aligned}& F_{r}=m g-F_{b}^{\prime} \\& F=m g-\rho A\left(l_{0}+x\right) g \\& d A l a=d A l g-\rho A l_{0} g-\rho g A x \\& a=-\dfrac{\rho g}{d l} x\end{aligned}$
Therefore, wooden cube performs S.H.M.
$\therefore \omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho g}{d l}} \Rightarrow T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l d}{\rho g}}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: Figure shows a system consisting of massless pulley, a spring of force constant $k$ and a block of mass $m$. If the block is slightly displaced vertically down from its equilibrium position and then released, the period of its vertical oscillation is
explanation:
Let us assume that in equilibrium condition spring is $x_{0}$ elongate from its natural length https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949681/preptracker/questions/q_39479_explanation_1764949680914.jpg In equilibrium $T_{0}=m g$
and $k x_{0}=2 T_{0}$
$ \Rightarrow k{x_0} = 2mg\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
If the mass $m$ moves down a distance $\mathrm{x}$ from its equilibrium position then pulley will move down by $\dfrac{x}{2}$. So the extra force in spring will be $\dfrac{k x}{2}$.
From figure
$\begin{aligned}& F_{\text {net }}=m g-T=m g-\dfrac{k}{2}\left(x_{0}+\dfrac{x}{2}\right) \\& \left(T=\dfrac{k\left(x_{o}+\dfrac{x}{2}\right)}{2}\right) \\& F_{\text {net }}=m g-\dfrac{k x_{0}}{2}-\dfrac{k x}{4}\end{aligned}$
from eq. (1), we get
${F_{{\rm{net }}}} = \frac{{ - kx}}{4}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Comparing eq. (2) with $F=-K_{\text {Sнм }} x$
then $\quad K_{\text {SHM }}=\dfrac{K}{4}$
$\Rightarrow T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{K_{\mathrm{SHM}}}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{4 m}{K}}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: Two identical particles each of mass $m$ are interconnected by a light spring of stiffness $k$, the time period for small oscillation is equal to:
explanation:
The reduced mass
$\mu=\dfrac{(m)(m)}{m+m}=\dfrac{m}{2}$
The given system is equivalent to a system of a particle of mass $m/2$ connected to a spring of stiffness $k$ ragidly.
The required period of oscillation.
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu}{k}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m / 2}{k}}=\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{2 m}{k}}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: The masses $m_{1}$ and $m_{2}$ are suspended together by a massless spring of constant $k$. When the masses are in equilibrium, $m_{1}$ is removed without disturbing the system; the amplitude of vibration is -
explanation:
In equilibrium $F=\left(m_{1}+m_{2}\right) g$
By removing mass $m_{1}$
$\begin{aligned}& F^{\prime}=m_{2} g \\& F_{r}=F^{\prime}-F=m_{2} g-\left(m_{1}+m_{2}\right) g \\& k A=m_{1} g \Rightarrow A=\dfrac{m_{1} g}{k}\end{aligned}$
since the lower spring exerts force on the block only in the downward motion.
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question:
A mass $M$, attached to a horizontal spring, executes S.H.M. with amplitude $A_{1}$. When the mass $M$ passes through its mean position then a smaller mass $m$ is placed over it and both of them move together with amplitude $A_{2}$. The ratio
of $\left(\dfrac{A_{1}}{A_{2}}\right)$ is:
explanation:
The net force exerted by the spring is zero at the mean point. Therefore, linear momentum must be conserved. $\therefore M v_{1}=(M+m) v_{2}$
$\begin{aligned}\text { As } \quad & =A \omega=A \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}} \\\Rightarrow & M A_{1} \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{M}}=(M+m) A_{2} \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m+M}} \\& \therefore\left(V=A \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{M}}\right) \\& \because A_{1} \sqrt{M}=A_{2} \sqrt{M+m} \\& \therefore \dfrac{A_{1}}{A_{2}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{m+M}{M}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A block of mass $m$ rests on a platform. The platform is given up and down SHM with an amplitude $\mathrm{d}$. What can be the maximum frequency so that the block never leaves the platform?
explanation:
For the gievn amplitude $d$ the block looses contact with the platform when maximum acceleration is equal to $g$ at the highest position.
$\begin{aligned}& k d=m g \Rightarrow m \omega^{2} d=m g \\& \Rightarrow \omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{d}} \Rightarrow n=\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{d}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: Two springs, of force constants $k_{1}$ and $k_{2}$ are connected to a mass $m$ as shown. The frequency of oscillation of the mass is $f$. If both $k_{1}$ and $k_{2}$ are made four times their original values, the frequency of oscillation becomes.
explanation:
The two springs are in parallel.
$\therefore$ Effective spring constant,
$k=k_{1}+k_{2}$
Now, frequency of oscillation is given by
$f = \frac{1}{{2\pi }}\sqrt {\frac{{{k_1} + {k_2}}}{m}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
When both $k_{1}$ and $k_{2}$ are made four times their original values, the new frequency is given by
$\begin{aligned}& f^{\prime}=\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{4 k_{1}+4 k_{2}}{m}} \\& =\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{4\left(k_{1}+k_{2}\right)}{m}}=2\left(\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{k_{1}+k_{2}}{m}}\right)=2 f\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Oscillation of a Body Due to Combination of Springs

question: A simple pendulum of length $l$ and having a bob of mass $M$ is suspended in a car. The car is moving on a circular track of radius $R$ with a uniform speed $v$. If the pendulum makes small oscillations about its equilibrium position, what will be its time period?
explanation:
The bob is subjected to two simultaneous, accelerations perpendicular to each other viz acceleration due to gravity $g$ and radial acceleration $a_{R}=\dfrac{v^{2}}{R}$ towards the centre of the circular path. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956084/preptracker/questions/q_39455_explanation_1764956083776.jpg $\therefore$ Effective acceleration $a_{e f f}=\sqrt{g^{2}+\left(\dfrac{v^{2}}{R}\right)^{2}}$
$\therefore$ Time period of the simple pendulum
$ = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{l}{{{a_{{\text{eff }}}}}}} $
$ = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{l}{{\sqrt {{g^2} + {{\left( {\frac{{{v^2}}}{R}} \right)}^2}} }}} = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{l}{{\sqrt {{g^2} + \frac{{{v^4}}}{{{R^2}}}} }}} $
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: If $T_{1}$ and $T_{2}$ are the time-periods of oscillation of a simple pendulum on the surface of earth (of radius $R$) and at a depth $d$, then $d$ is equal to
explanation: $\begin{aligned}& T_{1}=\dfrac{k}{\sqrt{g}} \text { and } T_{2}=\dfrac{k}{\sqrt{g\left(1-\dfrac{d}{R}\right)}} \\& \dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}=\sqrt{1-\dfrac{d}{R}} \Rightarrow\left(\dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}\right)^{2}=1-\dfrac{d}{R} \\& d=\left[1-\left(\dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}\right)^{2}\right] R\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: The period of a simple pendulum, whose bob is a hollow metallic sphere, is $T_{1}$. The period is $T_{2}$ when the bob is filled with sand, $T_{3}$ when it is filled with mercury and $T_{4}$ when it is half filled with mercury. Which of the following is true?
explanation: Time period of pendulum doesn't depends upon mass but it depends upon length (distance between point of suspension and centre of mass). In first three cases length are same so $T_{1}=T_{2}=T_{3}$ but in last case centre of mass lowers which in turn increases the length. So option (3) is correct
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A particle of mass $m$ moves in a one-dimensional potential energy $U(x)=a x^{2}-b x^{4}$, where $a$ and $b$ are positive constants. The angular frequency of small oscillations about the minima of the potential energy is equal to
explanation:
$U(x)=-a x^{2}+b x^{4}$
$\therefore F=-\dfrac{d U}{d x}=-2 a x+4 b x^{3}$
At mean position $F=0$
$\begin{gathered}\therefore 2 a x=4 b x^{3} \\\text { or } x^{2}=\dfrac{2 a}{4 b}=\dfrac{a}{2 b} \Rightarrow x= \pm \sqrt{a / 2 b} \\\text { ' } \mathrm{U} \text { ' is minimum at } x=-\sqrt{\dfrac{a}{2 b}}\end{gathered}$
Now, $F=m \dfrac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}$
$\begin{aligned}& \therefore \quad m \dfrac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}=-2 a x+4 b x^{3} \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}=-\dfrac{2 a x}{m}\left(1-\dfrac{2 b}{a} x^{2}\right)\end{aligned}$
For small ' $x$ ',
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}=-\left(\dfrac{2 a}{m}\right) x \\& \therefore \quad \omega=\sqrt{2 a / m}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Force, Energy and their relation in Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Force and Energy in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A particle of mass $m$ is executing oscillations about the origin on the $x$ - axis. Its potential energy is $U(x)=k[x]^{3}$, where $k$ is a positive constant. If the amplitude of oscillation is $a$, then its time period $T$ is
explanation:
$U(x)=k|x|^{3} \Rightarrow F=-\dfrac{d U}{d x}$
$F = - 3k|x{|^2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Also, for SHM, $x=a \sin \omega t$
and $\dfrac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}+\omega^{2} x=0$
$\Rightarrow$ Acceleration, $a=\dfrac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}=-\omega^{2} x$
$ \Rightarrow F = ma = m\frac{{{d^2}x}}{{d{t^2}}} = - m{\omega ^2}x\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From Eqs. (1) and (2), we get $\omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{3 k x}{m}}$
$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow T=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\omega}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{3 k x}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{3 k(a \sin \omega t)}} \\& \Rightarrow T \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{a}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Force, Energy and their relation in Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Force and Energy in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A particle of mass $m$ moving along the $x$-axis has a potential energy $U(x)=a+b x^{2}$ where $a$ and $b$ are positive constants. It will execute simple harmonic motion with a frequency determined by the value of
explanation:
$U=a+b x^{2}$
$\begin{aligned}& F=-\dfrac{d U}{d x}=-[0+2 b x] \\& F=m a=-2 b x \Rightarrow a=-\left(\dfrac{2 b}{m}\right) x \\& \omega=2 \pi f=\left(\dfrac{2 b}{m}\right)^{1 / 2}\end{aligned}$
Hence frequency depends upon $b$ and $m$.
subject: Physics
topic: Force, Energy and their relation in Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Force and Energy in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: The equation describing the motion of a simple harmonic oscillator along the $x$-axis is given as: $x=A \cos (\omega t+\phi)$. If at time $t = 0$, the oscillator is at $x = 0$, and moving in the negative $t$ direction, then the phase angle $\phi$ is
explanation:
$x=A \cos (\omega t+\phi)$
$\begin{aligned}& \text { At } t=0, x=0 \\& \therefore 0=\cos \phi \\& \cos \dfrac{\pi}{2}=\cos \phi \quad \therefore \phi=\dfrac{\pi}{2}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Phase and Phase Difference in SHM

question:
Two simple harmonic motions are given by $x_{1}=a \sin \omega t+a \cos \omega t$ and $x_{2}=a \sin \omega t+\dfrac{a}{\sqrt{3}} \cos \omega t$.
The ratio of the amplitudes of first and second motion and the phase difference between them are respectively
explanation: https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956082/preptracker/questions/q_39433_explanation_1764956081729.jpg
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Phase and Phase Difference in SHM

question: If $x, v$ and a denote the displacement, the velocity and the acceleration of a particle executing simple harmonic motion of time period $T$, then, which of the following does not change with time?
explanation:
For an SHM, the acceleration $a=-\omega^{2} x$
where $\omega^{2}$ is a constant. Therefore, $\dfrac{a}{x}$ is a constant. The time period $\mathrm{T}$ is also constant. Therefore, $\dfrac{a T}{x}$ is a constant.
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Velocity and Acceleration in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: For a particle executing simple harmonic motion, determine the ratio of average acceleration of particle from extreme position to equilibrium position to the maximum acceleration.
explanation:
Let the equation of simple harmonic motion be,
$x=A \sin \omega t$
Acceleration $a=-\omega^{2} x$
$|a|=\omega^{2} A \sin \omega t$
Then, the reqiured ratio is $\dfrac{\int_{0}^{T / 4} \omega^{2} A \sin \omega t d t}{\dfrac{T}{4} \times \omega^{2} A}=\dfrac{2}{\pi}$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Velocity and Acceleration in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A particle executes SHM with $x=A \sin \omega t$. Find the average acceleration of the particle from $t=0$ to $t=\dfrac{T}{4}$.
explanation:
$x=A \sin \omega t$
$a = - {\omega ^2}A\sin \omega t$
$ < a > = \frac{{\int_0^{T/4} - {\omega ^2}A\sin (\omega t)dt}}{{\frac{T}{4} - 0}} = \frac{{ - 4{\omega ^2}{A^{T/4}}}}{T}\int_0^T {\sin } (\omega t)dt$
$ < a > = \frac{{4{\omega ^2}A}}{{\omega T}}[\cos (\omega t)]_0^{t/4}$
$ < a > = \frac{{4\omega A}}{T}\left[ {\cos \frac{{2\pi }}{T}\frac{T}{4} - \cos 0} \right]_0^{t/4}$
$ = \frac{{ - 4A\omega }}{T} = \frac{{ - 8\pi A}}{{{T^2}}}$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Velocity and Acceleration in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A particle moves with simple harmonic motion in a straight line. In first $\tau \,s$, after starting from rest it travels a distance $a$, and in next $\tau \,s$ it travels $2 a$, in same direction, then :
explanation:
The displacement of the particle is
$x=A \cos \omega t$
Given that $A(1 - \cos \omega \tau ) = a$
$\begin{aligned}& \text { A }(1-\cos 2 \omega \tau)=3 a \\& \cos \omega \tau=\left(1-\dfrac{a}{A}\right) \\& \cos 2 \omega \tau=\left(1-\dfrac{3 a}{A}\right) \\& 2\left(1-\dfrac{a}{A}\right)^{2}-1=1-\dfrac{3 a}{A} \\& \dfrac{a}{A}=\dfrac{1}{2} \Rightarrow A=2 a \\& \cos \omega \tau=\dfrac{1}{2} \\& T=6 \tau\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion

question: Which of the following functions represents a simple harmonic oscillation?
explanation:
One of the condition for SHM is that restoring force $(F)$ and hence acceleration $(a)$ should be proportional to displacement $(y)$.
Let, $\quad y=\sin \omega t-\cos \omega t$
$\begin{gathered}\dfrac{d y}{d t}=\omega \cos \omega t+\omega \sin \omega t \\\dfrac{d^{2} y}{d t^{2}}=-\omega^{2} \cos \omega t+\omega^{2} \cos \omega t \\\text { or } \quad a=-\omega^{2}(\sin \omega t-\cos \omega t) \\a=-\omega^{2} y \Rightarrow a \propto-y\end{gathered}$
This satisfies the condition of SHM, other equations do not satisfy this condition.
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion

question: The displacement-time equation of particle executing SHM is: $x=A \sin (\omega t+\phi)$. At time $t = 0$, position of the particle is $x = A/2$ and it is moving along negative $x$-direction. Then the angle $\phi$ can be:
explanation:
At $t=0, x=\dfrac{A}{2}$
$\dfrac{A}{2}=A \sin \phi \text { or } \phi=\dfrac{\pi}{6} \text { or } \phi=\dfrac{5 \pi}{6}$

$v=\dfrac{d x}{d t}=A \omega \cos (\omega t+\phi)$
At $t=0, v=A \omega \cos \phi$
Now, $\cos \dfrac{\pi}{6}=\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$ and $\cos \dfrac{5 \pi}{6}=-\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$
As $v$ is negative at $t=0, \phi$ must be $5 \pi / 6$.
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A damped harmonic oscillator consists of a block $(m = 2.00\,kg)$, a spring $(k = 10.0\,N/m)\,(k = 10.0\,N/m)$, and a damping force $F =  - bv$, it oscillates with an amplitude of $25.0\,cm$' because of the damping, the amplitude falls to three-fourth of this initial value at the completion of four seconds. What is the value of $b$ ?
explanation:
$A=A_{0} e^{-b t / 2 m}$
$\therefore \quad \dfrac{3 A_{0}}{4}=A_{0} e^{-\dfrac{b \times 4}{2 \times 2}}=A_{0} e^{-b} \Rightarrow b=\ln \left(\dfrac{4}{3}\right)$
subject: Physics
topic: Damped Simple Harmonic Motion

question: In damped oscillation the amplitude of oscillations is reduced to one-third of its initial value $a_{0}$ at the end of 100 oscillation. When the oscillation completes 200 oscillations, its amplitude must be
explanation:
In damped oscillation amplitude goes on decaying exponentially
$a=a_{0} e^{-b t / 2 m}$
where, $b=$ damping coefficient
$\frac{{{a_0}}}{3} = {a_0}{e^{ - \left( {\frac{{100bT}}{{2m}}} \right)}} = {a_0}{e^{ - 100P}}$
where $P=b T / 2 m, T=$ time of one oscillation
or $\quad \dfrac{1}{3}=e^{-100 P}$ (1)
Finally $a = {a_0}{e^{ - \left( {\frac{{200\,bT}}{{2m}}} \right)}}$
$\begin{aligned}& a=a_{0}\left[e^{-100 P}\right]^{2} \\& a=a_{0} \times\left(\dfrac{1}{3}\right)^{2} \quad[\therefore \text { from Eq. (1) }] \\& a=\dfrac{a_{0}}{9}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Damped Simple Harmonic Motion

question: Consider a liquid which fills a uniform U-tube, as shown in fig up to a height $h$. Find angular frequency of small oscillations of the liquid in the U-tube.
explanation:
Suppose that the liquid is displaced slightly from equilibrium so that its level rises in one arm of the tube, while it is depressed in the second arm by the same amount, $x$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956079/preptracker/questions/q_39398_explanation_1764956078904.jpg If the density of the liquid is $\rho$, then the total mechanical energy of the liquid column is
The difference in the two levels is $2 x$. So the column having length $2 x$ produces restoring force on the entire liquid.
$\begin{aligned}& \rho A 2 x g=\rho A 2 h a \\& a=-\left(\dfrac{g}{h}\right) x \Rightarrow \omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{h}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A particle of mass $m$ is attached to three identical springs $A,B$ and $C$ each of force constant $k$ as shown in figure. If the particle of mass $m$ is pushed slightly against the spring A and released then the time period of oscillations is (The particle is on a frictionless horizontal surface).
explanation:
When the particle of mass $m$ at $O$ is pushed by y in the direction of $A$ the spring $A$ will be compressed by y while spring $B$ and $C$ will be stretched by $y^{\prime}=y \cos 45^{\circ}$. So that the total restoring force on the mass $m$ along $O A$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957939/preptracker/questions/q_39395_explanation_1764957939176.jpg $F_{\text {net }}=F_{A}+F_{B} \cos 45^{\circ}+F_{C} \cos 45^{\circ}$
$=k y+2 k y^{\prime} \cos 45^{\circ}=k y+2 k\left(y \cos 45^{\circ}\right) \cos 45^{\circ}=2 k y$
Also $F_{\text {net }}=k^{\prime} y \Rightarrow k^{\prime} y=2 k y \Rightarrow k^{\prime}=2 k$
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{k^{\prime}}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{2 k}}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Oscillation of a Body Due to Combination of Springs

question: A block of mass $M$ is suspended from a light spring of force constant $k$. Another mass $m$ moving upwards with velocity $v$ shifts the mass $M$ and gets embeded in it. What will be the amplitude of the combined mass?
explanation:
If $v^{\prime}$ are the velocities of the block of mass $M$ and $(M+m)$ while passing from the mean position when executing SHM.
Using law of conservation of linear momentum, we have
$\begin{aligned}m v & =(M+m) v^{\prime} \\\text { or } \quad v^{\prime} & =m v /(M+m)\end{aligned}$
Also, maximum $PE = $ maximum $KE$
$\begin{gathered}\therefore \quad \dfrac{1}{2} k A^{\prime 2}=\dfrac{1}{2}(M+m) v^{\prime 2} \\A^{\prime}=\left(\dfrac{M+m}{k}\right)^{1 / 2} \times \dfrac{m v}{(M+m)}=\dfrac{m v}{\sqrt{(M+m) k}}\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A mass $M$ is attached to a horizontal spring of force constant $k$ fixed on one side to a rigid support as shown figure. The mass oscillates on a frictionless surface with time period $T$ and amplitude A. When the mass is in equilibrium position, another mass $m$ is gently placed on it and both stick together. What will be the new amplitude of oscillation?
explanation:
When a mass $m$ is placed on mass $M$, the new system is of mass $=(M+m)$, attached to the spring. New time period of oscillation,
$\begin{aligned}& T^{\prime}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{M+m}{k}} \\& T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{k}}\end{aligned}$
Let $v=$ velocity of mass $\mathrm{M}$ while passing through the mean position.
$v^{\prime}=$ velocity of mass $(M+m)$, while passing through the mean position.
According to law of conservation of linear momentum
$M v=(M+m) v^{\prime}$
At mean position, $v=A \omega=$ and $v^{\prime}=A^{\prime} \omega^{\prime}$
$\therefore M A \omega=(M+m) A^{\prime} \omega^{\prime}$

$\text { or } \begin{aligned}A^{\prime} & =\left(\dfrac{M}{M+m}\right) \dfrac{\omega}{\omega^{\prime}} A=\dfrac{M}{M+m} \times \dfrac{T^{\prime}}{T} \times A \\& =\left(\dfrac{M}{M+m}\right) \times \sqrt{\dfrac{M+m}{M}} \times A=A \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{M+m}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A spring of mass $m$ is connected to a rigid support as shown in the figure. If the free end of the spring is given velocity $v_{o}$ then kinetic energy of the spring is
explanation:
The velocity of the spring increases linearly from left to right. The velocity of the spring at a distance $x$ from the left end is
$v=\dfrac{v_{o}}{l} x$
consider a small element of length $dx$ as shown in the figure. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957797/preptracker/questions/q_39386_explanation_1764957797006.jpg The kinetic energy of the element is
$\begin{aligned}& d x=\dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{m}{l}\right) d x v^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{m}{l} \dfrac{v_{o}^{2}}{l^{2}} x^{2} d x \\& k=\dfrac{m v_{o}^{2}}{2 l^{3}} \int_{x^{2}}^{l} x^{2} d x=\dfrac{m v_{o}^{2}}{2 l^{3}}\left[\dfrac{l^{3}}{3}\right]=\dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{m}{3}\right) v_{o}^{2}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: In the figure all springs are identical having spring constant $k$and mass $m$ each. The block also has mass $m$. The frequency of oscillation of the block is:
explanation:
If spring has the mass then:
$f=\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m+\dfrac{M_{\text {spring }}}{3}}}$
Spring equivalent $=3 k$ (As all are in parallel $)$
$f=\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{3 k}{m+\dfrac{(3 m)}{3}}} \Rightarrow f=\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{3 k}{2 m}}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Oscillation of a Body Due to Combination of Springs

question: The period of oscillation of a simple pendulum of length $l$ suspended from the roof of the vehicle which moves down without friction on an inclined plane of inclination $\alpha$, is given by:
explanation:
The vehicle goes down with acceleration $g \sin \alpha$. so the pendulum is in noninertial frame and Pseudo force acts on it. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957612/preptracker/questions/q_39380_explanation_1764957612281.jpg So the only resultant force on the bob is $m g \cos \alpha$. The effective acceleration is
$\begin{aligned}& g^{\prime}=g \cos \alpha \\& T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g \cos \alpha}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A pendulum made of a uniform wire of cross sectional area $A$ has time period $T$. When an additional mass $M$ is added to its bob, the time period changes to ${T_M}$. If the Young's modulus of the material of the wire is $Y$ then $\dfrac{1}{Y}$ is equal to: $(g = $ gravitational acceleration$)$
explanation:
Time period, $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}}$
when additional mass $M$ is added to its bob
$\begin{aligned}T_{M} & =2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l+\Delta l}{g}} \\\Delta l & =\dfrac{M g l}{A Y} \\\Rightarrow T_{M} & =2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l+\dfrac{M g l}{A Y}}{g}} \\\left(\dfrac{T_{M}}{T}\right)^{2} & =1+\dfrac{M g}{A Y} \\\dfrac{1}{Y} & =\dfrac{A}{M g}\left[\left(\dfrac{T_{M}}{T}\right)^{2}-1\right]\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: The bob of a simple pendulum is displaced from its equilibrium position $O$ to a position $Q$ which is at height $h$ above $O$ and the bob is then released. Assuming the mass of the bob to be $m$ and time period of oscillations to be $2.0\,\sec $, the tension in the string when the bob passes through $O$ is
explanation:
Tension in the string when bob passes through lowest point
$T=m g+\dfrac{m v^{2}}{r}=m g+m v \omega \quad(\because v=r \omega)$
Putting $v=\sqrt{2 g h}$ and $\omega=\dfrac{2 \pi}{T}=\dfrac{2 \pi}{2}=\pi$ we get $T=m(g+\pi \sqrt{2 g h})$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A particle of mass $m$ is located in a undimensional potential field where potential energy of the particle depends on the coordinate $x$ as $U(x)=\dfrac{A}{x^{2}}-\dfrac{B}{x}$ where $A$ and $B$ are positive contacts. Find the time period of small oscillations that the particle will perform about equilibrium position.
explanation:
The potential energy function for the mass $m$ in a undimensional potential field is given as
$U(x) = \frac{A}{{{x^2}}} - \frac{B}{x}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Since this is the potential energy of mass $m$ in a potential field, so the force acting on the mass is obtained by the relation:
$F = - \frac{{dU}}{{dx}} = \frac{{2A}}{{{x^3}}} - \frac{B}{{{x^2}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Now, the equilibrium position is given by
$\begin{aligned}& F=0 \text { or } \dfrac{2 A}{x^{3}}-\dfrac{B}{x^{2}}=0 \\& (2 A-B x)=0\end{aligned}$
So, $\quad x=\dfrac{2 A}{B}$
Since $x=2 A / B$ gives stable situation
(because energy is minimum here; $d^{2} U / d x^{2}$ is positive), so stable equilibrium position is given as $x=\dfrac{2 A}{B}$
Now if the mass is displaced slightly away from the equilibrium position, the force acting on mass $m$ is given as
$F=\dfrac{2 A}{[2 A / B+\Delta x]^{3}}-\dfrac{B}{[2 A / B+\Delta x]^{2}}$
$\begin{aligned}& =\dfrac{2 A}{\left(\dfrac{2 A}{B}\right)^{3}\left(1+\dfrac{B \Delta x}{2 A}\right)^{3}}-\dfrac{B}{\left(\dfrac{2 A}{B}\right)^{2}\left(1+\dfrac{B \Delta x}{2 A}\right)^{2}} \\& =\dfrac{2 A}{8 A^{3} / B^{3}}\left[1+\dfrac{B \Delta x}{2 A}\right]^{-3}-\dfrac{B}{4 A^{2} / B^{2}}\left[1+\dfrac{B \Delta x}{2 A}\right]^{-2}\end{aligned}$
So, using binomial expansion for small $\Delta x$, we get
$\begin{aligned}F & =\dfrac{B^{3}}{4 A^{2}}\left[1-\dfrac{3 B \Delta x}{2 A}\right]-\dfrac{B^{3}}{4 A^{2}}\left[1-\dfrac{2 B \Delta x}{2 A}\right] \\& =\dfrac{B^{3}}{4 A^{2}}\left[1-\dfrac{3 B \Delta x}{2 A}-1+\dfrac{2 B \Delta x}{2 A}\right]=-\left(\dfrac{B^{4}}{8 A^{3}}\right) \Delta x\end{aligned}$
We can write
$m\frac{{{d^2}x}}{{d{t^2}}} = - \frac{{{B^4}}}{{8{A^3}}}\Delta x$
$\frac{{{d^2}x}}{{d{t^2}}} = - \left( {\frac{{{B^4}}}{{8m{A^3}}}} \right)\Delta x\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(3)$
$\frac{{{d^2}x}}{{d{t^2}}} \propto - \Delta x\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(4)$
So from the above relations, it is clear that particle will perform SHM. But for any SHM,
$\dfrac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}=-\omega^{2} \Delta x$
So, from eqs. (3) and (4), we have $\omega^{2}=\dfrac{B^{4}}{8 m A^{3}}$
$T=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\omega}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{8 A^{3} m}{B^{4}}}=4 \pi A \sqrt{\dfrac{2 m A}{B^{4}}}$
subject: Physics
topic: Force, Energy and their relation in Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Force and Energy in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A particle of mass $10\,gm$ is placed in a potential field given by $V=\left(50 x^{2}+100\right) J / k g$. The frequency of oscillation in cycle/sec is:
explanation:
Potential energy $=U=V m$
$\Rightarrow U=\left(50 x^{2}+100\right) 10^{-2}$

$\begin{gathered}F=-\dfrac{d U}{d x}=-(100 x) 10^{-2} \\\Rightarrow-m \omega^{2} x=-\left(100 \times 10^{-2}\right) x \\10 \times 10^{-3} \omega^{2} x=100 \times 10^{-2} x \\\Rightarrow \omega^{2}=100 \therefore \omega=10 \\\Rightarrow f=\dfrac{\omega}{2 \pi}=\dfrac{10}{2 \pi}=\dfrac{5}{\pi}\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics

question: Two pendulums of different lengths are in phase at the mean position at a certain instant. The minimum time after which they will be again in phase is $5\,T/4$, where $T$ is the time period of shorter pendulum. Find the ratio of lengths of the two pendulums.
explanation:
Let $T_{1}=\mathrm{T}_{\text {and }} \mathrm{T}_{2}=K T ; \dfrac{2 \pi}{T} t-\dfrac{2 \pi}{K T} t=2 \pi$
$t\left(\dfrac{K-1}{K T}\right)=1 ; \dfrac{5 T}{4}\left(\dfrac{K-1}{K T}\right)=1$ which gives $K = 5$
If length of first pendulum $=l, T_{1}=2 \pi \sqrt{l / g}$
$T_{2}=5 \times 2 \pi \sqrt{l / g}=2 \pi \sqrt{25 l / g}$
So, ratio of lengths $=1: 25$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion

question:
Two pendulums have time periods T and $\frac{{5T}}{4}$.
They start S.H.M. at the same time from the mean position. What will be the phase difference between them after the bigger pendulum complete one oscillation?
explanation: $\dfrac{5 T}{4}=T+\dfrac{T}{4}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956078/preptracker/questions/q_39355_explanation_1764956077574.jpg By the time, the bigger pendulum makes one full oscillation, the smaller pendulum will make $\left(1+\dfrac{1}{4}\right)$ oscillation. The bigger pendulum will be in the mean position and the smaller one will be in the positive extreme position. Thus, phase difference $=90^{\circ}$
subject: Physics

question: A point mass oscillates along the $x$-axis according to the law $x=x_{0} \cos (\omega t-\pi / 4)$. If the acceleration of the particle is written as $a=A \cos (\omega t+\delta)$, then
explanation:
Here,
$x=x_{0} \cos (\omega t-\pi / 4)$
$\therefore$ Velocity, $v=\dfrac{d x}{d t}=-x_{0} \omega \sin \left(\omega t-\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right)$
Acceleration, $a=\dfrac{d v}{d t}=-x_{0} \omega^{2} \cos \left(\omega t-\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right)$
$ = {x_0}{\omega ^2}\cos \left[ {\pi + \left( {\omega t - \frac{\pi }{4}} \right)} \right]$
$ = {x_0}{\omega ^2}\cos \left( {\omega t + \frac{{3\pi }}{4}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Acceleration, $a=A \cos (\omega t+\delta)$
Comparing the two equations, we get
$A=x_{0} \omega^{2} \text { and } \delta=\dfrac{3 \pi}{4} .$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Velocity and Acceleration in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A body oscillates with SHM according to the equation $x = 5\cos \,(2\pi t + \pi )$. At time $t = 1.5\,s$, its displacement, speed and acceleration respectively is
explanation:
$x=5.0 \cos (2 \pi t+\pi)$
Velocity $=\dfrac{d x}{d t}=-10 \pi \sin (2 \pi t+\pi)$
Acceleration $=\dfrac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}=-20 \pi^{2} \cot (2 \pi t+\pi)$
$\therefore$ At $t = 1.5\,\sec ,x = 5.0\cos 4\pi = 5$
Velocity $=\dfrac{d x}{d t}=10 \pi($ zero $)=0$
Acceleration $=\dfrac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}=-20 \pi^{2}(1)=-20 \pi^{2}$
subject: Physics

subtopic: Velocity and Acceleration in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A particle executing SHM according to the equation $x=A \cos \omega t$. Average speed of the particle during the interval $0 \leq t \leq \dfrac{\pi}{6 \omega}$.
explanation:
Average velocity
$\begin{aligned}\bar{v} & =\dfrac{\int_{0}^{t} \dfrac{d x}{d t} \cdot d t}{t}=\dfrac{\int_{0}^{t} d x}{t}=\dfrac{x(t)-x(0)}{t} \\& =\dfrac{A(\cos \pi / 6-1)}{\pi / 6 \omega}=\dfrac{3 A \omega}{\pi}(\sqrt{3}-2)\end{aligned}$
since particle does not change its direction in the given interval, average speed
$|\bar{v}|=\dfrac{3 A \omega}{\pi}(2-\sqrt{3})$
subject: Physics

subtopic: Velocity and Acceleration in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: The displacement of particle varies with time as $x = 12\sin \omega t - 16{\sin ^3}\omega t\,(in{\rm{ }}cm)$. If its motion is S.H.M., then its maximum acceleration is
explanation:

$=4[\sin 3 \omega t]\left(\right.$ By using $\left.\sin 3 \theta=3 \sin \theta-4 \sin ^{3} \theta\right)$
$\therefore$ Maximum acceleration
$A_{\max }=(3 \omega)^{2} \times 4=36 \omega^{2}$

question: A block of mass $M$ is hanged from the ceiling by a spring with a spring constant $k$. It executes SHM with a period $P$. If the mass of the ball is increased by four times, the new period will be:
explanation:

question: A mass of $250\,g$ hangs on a spring and oscillates vertically with a period of $1.1\,s$. To double the period, what mass must be added to the $250\,g$ ? (Ignore the mass of the spring)

question: One end of an ideal spring of force constant $k$ is fixed at ceiling and other end is loaded with a block of mass $m$. If equilibrium elongation of the spring is $x$, then time period of vibration of the spring mass system is ( $g = $ acceleration due to gravity)
explanation:

Now, $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{k}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{x}{g}}$

question: A simple pendulum has a time period $T$ in vacuum. Its time period when it is completely immersed in a liquid of density one-eighth of the density of material of the bob is :
explanation:
Time period in liquid is given as
$\begin{aligned}& T^{\prime}=\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_{s}}{\rho_{s}-\rho_{\ell}}} T \\& T^{\prime}=\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_{s}}{\rho_{s}-\dfrac{\rho_{s}}{8}}} T \\& T^{\prime}=\sqrt{\dfrac{8}{7} T}\end{aligned}$

question: If the length of a pendulum is made 9 times and mass of the bob is made 4 times then the value of time period becomes:
explanation: $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}} \Rightarrow T \alpha \sqrt{l}$, hence if $l$ made 9 times $T$ becomes 3 times. Also time period of simple pendulum does not depend on the mass of the bob.

question: A pendulum has time period $T$ in air. When it is made to oscillate in water, it acquired a time period $T^{\prime}=\sqrt{2} T$. The density of the pendulum bob is equal to (density of water $=1$ )
explanation:
The effective acceleration of a bob in water
$=g^{\prime}=g\left(1-\dfrac{\sigma}{\rho}\right)$ where $\sigma$ and $\rho$ are the
density of water and the bob respectively. Since the period of oscillation of the bob in air and water are given as
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}} \text { and } T^{\prime}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g^{\prime}}}$
$\therefore \dfrac{T}{T^{\prime}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{g^{\prime}}{g}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{g(1-\sigma / \rho)}{g}}=\sqrt{1-\dfrac{\sigma}{\rho}}=\sqrt{1-\dfrac{1}{\rho}}$
Putting $\dfrac{T}{T^{\prime}}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$. We obtain, $\dfrac{1}{2}=1-\dfrac{1}{\rho} \Rightarrow \rho=2$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: How does the time period of pendulum vary with length
explanation:
Time period $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{g}}$
$ \Rightarrow T \propto \sqrt L $

question: A simple pendulum is suspended from the ceiling of a lift. When the lift is at rest its time period is $T$. With what acceleration should the lift be accelerated upwards in order to reduce its period to $T / 2$ ( $g$ acceleration due to gravity)

$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{g}}$.
$T^{\prime}=\dfrac{2 \pi}{2} \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}} \Rightarrow T^{\prime}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{4 g}}$
the lift should be accelerated by $3 g$ upwards because the total acceleartion $g+3 g$ pseudo acceleration.

question: The total energy of the body executing simple harmonic motion is $E$ then, the kinetic energy when the displacement is at the mid point of mean and extreme position is
explanation:
Total energy of SHM,
$E=\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2} a^{2},(\text { where }, a=\text { amplitude })$
Kinetic energy,
$K=\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2}\left(a^{2}-y^{2}\right) \Rightarrow K=E-\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2} y^{2}$
When $y=\dfrac{a}{2} \Rightarrow K=E-\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2}\left(\dfrac{a^{2}}{4}\right)=E-\dfrac{E}{4}$
$K=\dfrac{3 E}{4}$
subject: Physics

question: A particle is executing SHM with an amplitude of $4\,cm$. At what distance from equilibrium position its potential energy will be $25 \%$ of its total energy?

$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} x = \frac{a}{2} = \frac{4}{2}\,cm = 2\,cm$

question: In SHM restoring force is $F=-k x$, where $k$ is force constant, $x$ is displacement and $A$ is amplitude of motion then total energy depends upon:
explanation:
In SHM restoring force is
$F=-m \omega^{2} x$
Given that $F=-k x$
Hence $\quad k=m \omega^{2}$
Total energy is $\mathrm{TE}=\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2} A^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} k A^{2}$.
Hence total energy depends upon $k$ and $A$.

question: A particle executes SHM along a straight line. It starts its motion at extreme position at $t = 0$. The time interval after which its kinetic energy will be equal to its potential energy for the first time is $(T = $ Time period)
explanation:
Let $x=a \cos \omega t$
$\begin{aligned}& \therefore \quad K E=\dfrac{1}{2} m a^{2} \omega^{2} \sin ^{2} \omega t \\& P E=\dfrac{1}{2} m a^{2} \omega^{2} \cos ^{2} \omega t\end{aligned}$
when $K E=P E, \quad \sin ^{2} \omega t=\cos ^{2} \omega t$
$\begin{aligned}& \therefore \quad \tan \omega t= \pm 1 \\& \therefore \quad \omega t=\dfrac{\pi}{4} \Rightarrow t_{\min }=\dfrac{\pi}{4 \omega}=\dfrac{T}{8} .\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: The K. E and P. E of a particle executing SHM with amplitude $A$ will be equal when its displacement is
explanation:

subject: Physics

question: The kinetic energy of SHM is $1 / n$ time its potential energy. If the amplitude of the SHM be $A$, then what is the displacement of the particle?
explanation:
$\begin{aligned}& K E=\dfrac{1}{n} P E \\& \dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2}\left(A^{2}-x^{2}\right)=\dfrac{1}{n}\left(\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2} x^{2}\right)\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow x=\sqrt{\dfrac{n}{n+1}} A$

question: A body executes simple harmonic motion. The potential energy (PE), kinetic energy (KE) and total energy (TE) are measured as a function of displacement $x$. Which of the following statement is true?
explanation:
$P E=\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2} x^{2}$
$KE = \frac{1}{2}m{\omega ^2}\left( {{a^2} - {x^2}} \right)$
$TE = PE + KE = \frac{1}{2}m{\omega ^2}{a^2}$
Since $PE$ is maximum at $x=a$ and $KE$ is maximum at $x=0$, therefore $TE$ remains constant throughout the motion.

question: The phase difference between two SHM $y_{1}=10 \sin \left(10 \pi t+\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)$ and $y_{2}=12 \sin \left(8 \pi t+\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right)$ at $t = 0.5\,s$ is
explanation:
Phase for $1^{s t}$ SHM: $\phi_{1}=10 \pi t+\dfrac{\pi}{3}$
Phase for $2^{\text {nd }}$ SHM: $\phi_{2}=8 \pi t+\dfrac{\pi}{4}$
Phase difference: $\Delta \phi=\phi_{1}-\phi_{2}=2 \pi t+\dfrac{\pi}{12}$
Put $t = 0.5{\rm{ }}s$
$\Delta \phi=2 \pi(0.5)+\dfrac{\pi}{12}=\dfrac{13 \pi}{12}$

question:
Two simple harmonic motions are represented by equations
$y_{1}=4 \sin (10 t+\phi)$ and $y_{2}=5 \cos 10 t$. What is the phase difference between their velocities?
explanation:
$y_{1}=4 \sin (10 t+\phi)$;
${y_2} = 5\cos 10t$

${v_2} = \frac{{d{y_2}}}{{dt}} = - 50\sin 10t = 50\cos (10t + \pi /2)$
Phase difference between $v_{1}$ and $v_{2}=\left(\phi-\dfrac{\pi}{2}\right)$

question:
Match the Column I (quantity) with Column II (value) for an object executing simple harmonic motion in a horizontal plane with displacement given as \(x=A \cos \omega t\) and select the correct answer from the codes given below
A. $\dfrac{v_{\max }}{A}$
B. $\dfrac{a_{\max }}{A}$
C. $\begin{array}{l}\text { If object starts from } \\x=+A, \text { then time to } \\\text { reach at } \dfrac{A}{\sqrt{2}}\end{array}$
D. $\begin{array}{l}\text { If object starts from } \\x=0 \text { and move towards } \\\text { right, then the time to } \\\text { reach at }+A / 2\end{array}$
P. $T / 8$
Q. $T / 12$
R. $\omega$
S. $\omega^{2}$
explanation:
For an object executing SHM,
A. ${v_{{\rm{max }}}} = A\omega $
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{v_{{\rm{max }}}}}}{A} = \frac{{A\omega }}{A} = \omega $
B. Similarly, $a_{\max }=A \omega^{2}$
So, $\dfrac{a_{\max }}{A}=\dfrac{A \omega^{2}}{A}=\omega^{2}$
C. If object starts from $x=+A$, its equation is
$x=A \cos \omega t$
For $x=+A / \sqrt{2}$
$\dfrac{A}{\sqrt{2}}=A \cos \omega t$
$\cos \omega t=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=\cos \left(\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{2 \pi}{T} \times t=\dfrac{\pi}{4} \Rightarrow t=T / 8$
D. If object starts from $x=0$, its equation is $x=A \sin \omega t$
For $x=+A / 2 \Rightarrow \dfrac{A}{2}=A \sin \omega t$
$\therefore \dfrac{1}{2}=\sin \omega t \Rightarrow \sin \dfrac{\pi}{6}=\sin \omega t$
$\Rightarrow \omega t=\dfrac{\pi}{6} \Rightarrow t=\dfrac{T}{12}$
Option (2) is correct.

options.text: A - R, B - P, C - S, D - Q
A - R, B - S, C - P, D - Q
A - S, B - R, C - Q, D - P
A - R, B - S, C - Q, D - P

question: Which of the following quantities are always negative in a simple harmonic motion?
explanation: Force and acceleration will be always towards the origin. Position vector defined from the origin is always away from the mean position. $\bar{v}$ can be towards or away from the mean position. So $\vec F.\vec r\,\,\& \,\,\vec a \cdot \vec r$ are (-) ve.

question: A particle executes linear simple harmonic motion with an amplitude of $2\,cm$. When the particle is at $1\,cm$ from the mean position the magnitude of its velocity is equal to that of its acceleration. Then its time period in seconds is
explanation:

Now given,
$\begin{aligned}& \omega^{2} x=\omega \sqrt{A^{2}-x^{2}} \Rightarrow \omega^{2} \cdot 1=\omega \sqrt{2^{2}-1^{2}} \\& \Rightarrow \omega=\sqrt{3} \quad \therefore T=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\omega}=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\sqrt{3}}\end{aligned}$

Differentiating with respect to $t$ on both sides,

Motion is simple harmonic:
So, $\omega^{2}=\dfrac{1}{4}$ and $T=2 \pi \sqrt{4}=4 \pi$

question: A particle executes simple harmonic oscillation with an amplitude $a$. The period of oscillation is $T$. The minimum time taken by the particle to travel half of the amplitude from the equilibrium position is
explanation:

As particle travels half of the amplitude from the equilibrium position, so
$y=\dfrac{a}{2}$
Therefore, $\dfrac{a}{2}=a \sin \omega t$ or $\sin \omega t=\dfrac{1}{2}=\sin \dfrac{\pi}{6}$
$\Rightarrow t=\dfrac{T}{12}$

question: A stone is swinging in a horizontal circle $0.8\,m$ in diameter, at $30\,rev/\min $. A distant light causes a shadow of the stone to be formed on a nearby wall. What is the amplitude of the motion of the shadow? what is the frequency?
explanation:
The amplitude is the radius of the circle
$R = \frac{{0.8}}{2}\,m = 0.4\,m.$
The frequency of the shadow is the same as that of angular velocity of the circular motion, so $\omega = 30rev/\min = 0.5rev/s = \pi rad/s$
$\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{l}}{\because 2\pi = 1\,rev} \\ { \Rightarrow \pi = 0.5\,rev} \end{array}} \right]$
and $f = \omega /2\pi = \frac{\pi }{{2\pi }} = 0.5\,Hz$
subject: Physics

question: Match the following:
explanation:
In figure $A$, three springs are connected in parallel. The effective spring constant is,
$k_{\text {eff }}=k_{1}+k_{2}+k_{3} \text {. }$
$\therefore T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{k_{1}+k_{2}+k_{3}}}$
In figure $B$, three identical springs are connected in parallel. The effective spring constant is
$k_{\mathrm{eff}}=k+k+k=3 k \therefore T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{3 k}}$
In figure $C$, two springs are connected in series. The effective spring constant is
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{1}{k_{e f f}}=\dfrac{1}{k_{1}}+\dfrac{1}{k_{2}}=\dfrac{k_{2}+k_{1}}{k_{1} k_{2}} \Rightarrow k_{e f f}=\dfrac{k_{1} k_{2}}{k_{1}+k_{2}} \\& \therefore T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m\left(k_{1}+k_{2}\right)}{k_{1} k_{2}}}\end{aligned}$
In figure $D$, two identical springs are connected in series. The effective spring constant is
$\begin{aligned}& k_{\text {eff }}=\dfrac{(k)(k)}{k+k}=\dfrac{k}{2} \\& \therefore T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{2 m}{k}}\end{aligned}$
So option (2) is correct.

options.text: A - P, B - Q, C - S, D - R
A - S, B - R, C - P, D - Q
A - R, B - P,  C - S, D - Q

question: A block of mass $M$ is suspended from a light spring of force constant $k$. Another mass $m$ moving downwards hits the mass $M$ and gets embeded in it. What will be the shift in mean position of the system.

question: A mass $m$ is suspended separately by two different springs in successive order, then time periods is $t_{1}$ and $t_{2}$ respectively. If $m$ is connected by both springs as shown in figure, then time period is $t_{0}$, the correct relation is
explanation:

$k_{1}=\dfrac{4 \pi^{2} m}{t_{1}^{2}}$
Similarly, $k_{2}=\dfrac{4 \pi^{2} m}{t_{2}^{2}}$
$\begin{aligned}& \text { and } \quad\left(k_{1}+k_{2}\right)=\dfrac{4 \pi^{2} m}{t_{0}^{2}} \\& \therefore \quad \dfrac{4 \pi^{2} m}{t_{0}^{2}}=\dfrac{4 \pi^{2} m}{t_{1}^{2}}+\dfrac{4 \pi^{2} m}{t_{2}^{2}} \\& \text { or } \quad \dfrac{1}{t_{o}^{2}}=\dfrac{1}{t_{1}^{2}}+\dfrac{1}{t_{2}^{2}}\end{aligned}$

question: In figures $S_{1}$ and $S_{2}$ are identical springs. the oscillation frequency of the mass $m$ is $f$. If one spring is removed, the frequency will become
explanation:
Effective spring constant ${K_{eff{\rm{ }}}} = 2K$.
$f=\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{2 K}{m}}$
When one spring is removed, $K_{\text {eff }}$ becomes $K$ new frequency
$\begin{aligned}& f^{\prime}=\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{m}} \\& \dfrac{f^{\prime}}{f}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \Rightarrow f^{\prime}=\dfrac{f}{\sqrt{2}}\end{aligned}$

question: An object is attached to the bottom of a light vertical spring and set vibrating. The maximum speed of the object is $15\,cm/\sec $ and the time $\frac{\pi }{5}\,\sec $, find its amplitude
explanation:
Maximum velocity, $v_{\max }=a \omega$
${\rm{or }}\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {v_{\max }} = a\left( {\frac{{2\pi }}{T}} \right)$
$a = \frac{{{v_{\max }}T}}{{2\pi }} = \frac{{15 \times 3.14}}{{2 \times 3.14 \times 5}} = 1.5\,cm$

question: Simple pendulum is executing simple harmonic motion with time period $T$. If the length of the pendulum is increased by $21 \%$, then percentage increase in the time period of the pendulum is:
explanation:
The time period of a simple pendulum of a length $l$ is given by
$T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{l}{g}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Where $g$ is acceleration due to gravity.
Where length of pendulum increases by $21 \%$
We have $l^{\prime}=1.21 l$
$\therefore {T^\prime } = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{{1.21l}}{g}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Dividing Eq. (1) by Eq. (2), we get
$\dfrac{T}{T^{\prime}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{1.21}} \Rightarrow T^{\prime}=1.1 T$
Hence, increase in time period
$1.1 T-T=0.1 T$
Percent increase in time period
$=\dfrac{0.1 T}{T} \times 100=10 \%$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A pendulum suspended from the ceiling of an elevator at rest has a time period $T_{1}$, when the elevator moves up with an acceleration $a$ its time period becomes $T_{2}$, when the elevator moves down with an acceleration $a$, its time period becomes $T_{3}$, then:-

question: The time period of a simple pendulum is $T$. The pendulum is oscillated with its bob immersed in a liquid of density $\sigma$. If the density of the bob is $\rho$ and viscous effect is neglected, the time period of the pendulum will be
explanation:
${T^\prime } = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{1}{{\,{g_{eff{\text{ }}}}}}} = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{{\rho l}}{{(\rho - \sigma )g}}} = {\left( {\frac{\rho }{{\rho - \sigma }}} \right)^{1/2}}\,T$
$\left( {\because T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{l}{{\,g}}} } \right)$

question: A simple pendulum of length $\ell$ is making oscillations with its bob immersed in a liquid of density $n$ times less than the density of the bob. What is its period?
explanation:
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g^{\prime}}}$
$g^{\prime}$ is the effective acceleration due to gravity Inside the liquid the effective weight is
$\begin{aligned}& m g^{\prime}=m g-F_{b}=\rho_{s} V g-\rho_{l} V g \\& \rho_{s} V g^{\prime}=\rho_{s} V g-\dfrac{\rho_{s}}{n} V g \\& g^{\prime}=g\left(1-\dfrac{1}{n}\right) \\& \Rightarrow T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g\left(1-\dfrac{1}{n}\right)}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

explanation:

question: The displacement of a particle executing SHM is given by $y=5 \sin \left(4 t+\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)$. If $T$ is the time period and mass of the particle is $2\,g$, the kinetic energy of the particle when $t=\dfrac{T}{4}$ is given by
explanation:
The displacement of particle, executing SHM,
$\begin{aligned}& y=5 \sin \left(4 t+\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right) \\& v=20 \cos \left(4 t+\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)\end{aligned}$
Velocity at $t=\left(\dfrac{T}{4}\right)$
$\begin{aligned}& v=20 \cos \left(4 \times \dfrac{T}{4}+\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right) \\& \Rightarrow v=20 \cos \left(T+\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)\end{aligned}$
Comparing the given equation with standard equation of SHM
$y=a \sin (\omega t+\phi)$,
we get $\omega=4$.
$\text { As } T=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\omega} \Rightarrow T=\dfrac{2 \pi}{4} \Rightarrow T=\dfrac{\pi}{2}$
Now, putting value of $T$ in Eq. (2), we get
$\begin{aligned}v & =20 \cos \left(\dfrac{\pi}{2}+\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)=-20 \sin \dfrac{\pi}{3} \\& =-20 \times \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=-10 \times \sqrt{3}\end{aligned}$
The kinetic energy of particle,
$KE = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2}$
$\because m = 2g = 2 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,kg$
$ = \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times {10^{ - 3}} \times {( - 10\sqrt 3 )^2}$
$ = {10^{ - 3}} \times 100 \times 3 = 3 \times {10^{ - 1}} \Rightarrow K.E = 0.3\,J.$
subject: Physics
topic: Force, Energy and their relation in Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Force and Energy in Simple Harmonic Motion

question: The angular velocity and the amplitude of a simple pendulum is $\omega$ and A respectively. At a displacement $x$ from the mean position if its kinetic energy is $T$ and potential energy is $V$, then the ratio of $T\,\,{\rm{to}}\,\,V$ is
explanation:

and kinetic energy $T=\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2}\left(A^{2}-x^{2}\right)$
$\therefore \dfrac{T}{V}=\dfrac{\mathrm{A}^{2}-x^{2}}{x^{2}}$

question: A particle of mass $m$ is executing SHM about the origin on $x$-axis with frequency $\sqrt{\dfrac{k a}{\pi m}}$, where $k$ is a constant and $a$ is the amplitude. Find its potential energy, if $x$ is the displacement at time $t$.

question: A particle performs simple harmonic motion with amplitude $A$. Its speed is tripled at the instant when it is at a distance $\dfrac{2 A}{3}$ from equilibrium position. The new amplitude of the motion is :
explanation:
We know that $v=\omega \sqrt{A^{2}-x^{2}}$
Initially $v=\omega \sqrt{A^{2}-\left(\dfrac{2 A}{3}\right)^{2}}$
After increasing the velocity
$3v = \omega \sqrt {{{A'}^2} - {{\left( {\frac{{2A}}{3}} \right)}^2}} $
Where $A^{\prime}$ is the final amplitude.
$\frac{1}{3} = \sqrt {\frac{{{A^\prime } - {{\left( {\frac{{2A}}{3}} \right)}^2}}}{{{A^{\prime 2}} - {{\left( {\frac{{2A}}{3}} \right)}^2}}}} $
$9\left[ {{A^2} - \frac{{4{A^2}}}{9}} \right] = {A^{\prime 2}} - \frac{{4{A^2}}}{9}$
$ \Rightarrow {A^\prime } = \frac{{7A}}{3}$
subject: Physics

question: The maximum velocity of a particle, executing simple harmonic motion with an amplitude $7\,mm$, is $4.4\,m/s$. The period of oscillation is
explanation:
Maximum velocity,
${v_{\max {\rm{ }}}} = a\omega \Rightarrow {v_{\max {\rm{ }}}} = a \times \frac{{2\pi }}{T}$
$ \Rightarrow T = \frac{{2\pi a}}{{{v_{\max {\rm{ }}}}}} = \frac{{2 \times 3.14 \times 7 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{4.4}} \approx 0.01\,s$

question:
Out of the following functions representing motion of a particle which represents SHM
A. $y=\sin ^{3} \omega t$
B. $y=\sin \omega t-\cos \omega t$
C. $y=1+\omega t+\omega^{2} t^{2}$
D. $y=5 \cos \left(\dfrac{3 \pi}{4}-3 \omega t\right)$
explanation:
The function $y=5 \cos \left(\dfrac{3 \pi}{4}-3 \omega t\right)$ represents equation of SHM with phase constant $\dfrac{3 \pi}{4}$
$\begin{aligned}& y=\sin \omega t-\cos \omega t \\& \dfrac{d y}{d t}=\omega \cos \omega t+\omega \sin \omega t \\& \dfrac{d^{2} y}{d t^{2}}=-\omega^{2} \sin \omega t+\omega^{2} \cos \omega t \\& =-\omega^{2}(\sin \omega t-\cos \omega t) \\& \dfrac{d^{2} y}{d t^{2}}=-\omega^{2} y\end{aligned}$
The other two functions do not obey
$\dfrac{d^{2} y}{d t^{2}}=-\omega^{2} y$
subject: Physics
topic: Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A particle is performing simple haromonic motion along $x$-axis with amplitude $4\,cm$ and time period $1.2\,\sec $. The minimum time taken by the particle to move from $x = 2\,cm$ to $x =  + 4\,cm$ and back again is given by [Mean position is at $x=0$ ]
explanation:
Time taken by particle to move from $x=$ 0 (mean position) to $x=4$ (extreme position)
$ = \frac{T}{4} = \frac{{1.2}}{4} = 0.3\,s$
Let $t$ be the time taken by the particle to move from
$x = 0{\rm{ }}to{\rm{ }}x = 2\,cm$
$y = a\sin \omega t \Rightarrow 2 = 4\sin \frac{{2\pi }}{T}t \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} = \sin \frac{{2\pi }}{{1.2}}t$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\pi}{6}=\dfrac{2 \pi}{1.2} t \Rightarrow t=0.1 s$.
Hence time to move from
$x=2$ to $x=4$ will be equal to $0.3-0.1=0.2 s$
Hence total time to move from $x=2$ to $x=4$ and back again $=2 \times 0.2=0.4 \mathrm{sec}$
subject: Physics

question: The measured value of length of a simple pendulum is  $20\,cm$ with $2\,mm$ accuracy. The time for 50 oscillations was measured to be 40 seconds with 1 second resolution. From these measurements, the accuracy in the measurement of acceleration due to gravity is ${N \%}$. The value of ${N}$ is
explanation:
As
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{g}} \quad \therefore T^{2}=\dfrac{4 \pi^{2} L}{g} \Rightarrow g=\dfrac{4 \pi^{2} L}{T^{2}}$
The percentage error in ' ${g}$ ' is given by
$\dfrac{\Delta g}{g} \times 100=\dfrac{\Delta L}{L} \times 100+\dfrac{2 \Delta T}{T} \times 100$
Here,
${\left(\dfrac{\Delta g}{g} \times 100\right)=\left(\dfrac{0.2}{20}+\dfrac{2 \times 1}{40}\right) \times 100=6 \%}$
So, ${N=6}$
So, correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question: If ${R}$ is the radius of the earth and the acceleration due to gravity on the surface of earth is ${g=\pi^{2} {~m} / {s}^{2}}$, then the length of the second's pendulum at a height ${h=2 R}$ from the surface of earth will be
explanation:
${R_{e}=R, g=\pi^{2}, h=2 R, l=1 m}$
Let the length of second's pendulum be ' ${l}$ '
$g^{\prime}=\dfrac{g R^{2}}{(h+R)^{2}}=\dfrac{g R^{2}}{(3 R)^{2}}=\dfrac{g}{9}$
Where ${h=}$ Height from surface of earth
$\begin{aligned}& T \propto \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}} \\& \Rightarrow T^{\prime} \propto \sqrt{\dfrac{l^{\prime}}{g^{\prime}}}\end{aligned}$
So, as ${T}$ is same as ${2 s}$
$\begin{aligned}& \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{l^{\prime}}{g^{\prime}}} \Rightarrow \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{l^{\prime} \times 9}{g}} \\& \Rightarrow l^{\prime}=\dfrac{l}{9} ; l^{\prime}=\dfrac{1}{9} m\end{aligned}$
So, correct option is (4).

question:
A block of mass $m$ is tied to one end of a spring which passes over a smooth fixed pulley $A$ and under a light smooth movable pulley $B$. The other end of the string is attached to the lower end of a spring of spring constant $K_{2}$. Find the period of small oscillation of mass $m$ about its equilibrium position.
(Take $m=\pi^{2} {~kg}$, $K_{2}=4 K_{1}$ and${K_1} = 17{\rm{ }}N/m$.
explanation:
Net increment in the tension in string connecting the block will provide acceleration to the block. We can write
$\Delta T = ma\quad \quad \quad (1)$
When the block $m$ is displaced by a distance $x$ beyond equilibrium position, the addition stretch of springs 1 and 2 are $x_{1}$ and $x_{2}$, respectively.We can write
${x_1} = \frac{{x - {x_2}}}{2} \Rightarrow x = 2{x_1} + {x_2}\quad \quad (2)$
As $\Delta T=K_{2} x_{2}$ and $2\,\Delta T = {K_1}{x_1}$
$2\left( {{K_2}{x_2}} \right) = {K_1}{x_1} \Rightarrow \quad {x_1} = \frac{{2{K_2}{x_2}}}{{{K_1}}}{\mkern 1mu} \quad \quad \quad (3)$
From Eqs. (2) and (3),
$x = 2\left[ {\frac{{2{K_2}{x_2}}}{{{K_1}}}} \right] + {x_2} = \frac{{\left( {4{K_2} + {K_1}} \right)}}{{{K_1}}}{x_2}$
$ \Rightarrow \quad {x_2} = \frac{{{K_1}x}}{{\left( {{K_1} + 4{K_2}} \right)}}{\rm{ }}$
${\rm{and }}\quad \vec a = \frac{{ - {K_1}{K_2}}}{{\left( {{K_1} + 4{K_2}} \right){\rm{m}}}} \cdot x\quad $
$ \Rightarrow \vec a = \frac{{ - {K_1}{K_2}}}{{\left( {{K_1} + 4{K_2}} \right){\rm{m}}}} \cdot x$
${\omega ^2} = \frac{{{K_1}{K_2}}}{{\left( {{K_1} + 4{K_2}} \right){\rm{m}}}}$
Hence $R=\dfrac{80}{23 \pi^{2}} {~m}$
After substituting the values, we get $T=1 {~s}$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950157/preptracker/questions/q_41107_explanation_1764950157255.png
subject: Physics

question: A uniform cylinder of mass $m=2 {~kg}$ and radius $R=0.50$ $m$ is in equilibrium on an inclined plane by the action of a light spring of stiffness $K=300 {~N} / {m}$, gravity and reaction force acting on it. If the angle of inclination of the plane is $\phi=30^{\circ}$, find the angular frequency of small oscillation of the cylinder.
explanation:
Here point of contact $P$, will be instantaneous centre of rotation. Let the cylinder rotates a small angle $\theta$ beyond its equilibrium position about an axis passing through $P$. Here the increase in length of spring will be $2 R \theta$. Thus the increased spring force provides a restoring torque about $P$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950154/preptracker/questions/q_41083_explanation_1764950153537.png Now writing torque equation about $P$ as:
$\Delta \tau_{p}=I_{p} \alpha$
$(K \cdot 2R\theta ) \cdot 2R = \left[ {\frac{{m{R^2}}}{2} + m{R^2}} \right]\alpha $
$ \Rightarrow 4K{R^2}\alpha = \frac{3}{2}m{R^2}\alpha $
$or\quad \alpha = \left( {\frac{{8K}}{{3m}}} \right)\theta $
$ \Rightarrow \vec \alpha = - \left( {\frac{{8K}}{{3m}}} \right)\vec \theta $
$Hence\,\,{\omega ^2} = \frac{{8K}}{{3m}}$
$or\,\,\,\,\,\omega = 2\sqrt {\frac{{2K}}{{3m}}} $
$ \Rightarrow \omega = 2\sqrt {\frac{{2 \times 300}}{{3 \times 2}}} = 20\,\,rad/s$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion
parent_topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: Figure shows a block $P$ of mass $m$ resting on a horizontal smooth floor at a distance $l$ from a rigid wall. Block is pushed toward right by a distance $3{\rm{ }}l/d$ and released, when block passes from its mean position another block of mass $m_{1}$ is placed on it which sticks to it due to friction. Find the ratio of $\dfrac{m_{1}}{m}$ so that the combined block just collides with the left wall.
explanation:
When block $P$ is released from rest from a distance $3 l / 2$ toward right from mean position, this will be the amplitude of oscillation, so velocity of block when passing from its mean position is given as
$v=A \omega=\dfrac{3 l}{2} \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}} \quad\left[\text { As } \omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}\right]$
If mass $m_{1}$ is added to it and just after if velocity of combined block becomes $v_{1}$, from momentum conservation we have
$mv = \left( {m + {m_1}} \right){v_1}$
$or\,\,{v_1} = \frac{m}{{\left( {m + {m_1}} \right)}}\left( {\frac{{3l}}{2}\sqrt {\frac{k}{m}} } \right)$
If this is the velocity of combined block at mean position, it must be given as$v_{1}=A \omega_{1} \quad\left[\text { Now } \omega_{1}=\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m+m_{1}}}\right]$
where $A_{1}$ and $\omega_{1}$ are the new amplitude and angular frequency of SHM of the block. It is given that combined block just reaches the left wall thus the new amplitude of oscillation must be $l$ so we have
$\dfrac{m}{\left(m+m_{1}\right)} \cdot \dfrac{3 l}{2} \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}=l_{1} \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m+m_{1}}}$ or $\dfrac{3 \sqrt{m}}{2 \sqrt{m+m}}=1$ or $\quad 9m = 4m + 4{m_1}$
or $m_{1}=\dfrac{5}{4} m \Rightarrow \dfrac{m_{1}}{m}=1.25$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion
parent_topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: Find the period  of small oscillations in a horizontal plane performed by a ball of mass $m=\dfrac{4}{10 \pi^{2}} {~kg}$, fixed at the middle of a horizontally stretched string $l=1.0 {~m}$ in length. The tension of the string is assumed to be constant and equal to $T=10 {~N}$.
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950146/preptracker/questions/q_41051_explanation_1764950145676.png The situation is shown in figure. The ball is in equilibrium at point $O$ as shown in Fig. (a). Now it is displaced from $O$ by a distance $x$ in horizontal plane to a position $P$ as shown in Fig. (b). The components of tensions on ball toward $O$ are $T \sin \theta$ and $T \sin \theta$, hence the restoring force on ball toward mean position is
$F_{R}=-2 T \sin \theta=-\dfrac{2 T x}{\sqrt{x^{2}+l^{2} / 4}}
[-$ ve sign for restoring tendency $]$
As $x$ is very small compared to $l$, we can neglect its square compared to $l^{2} / 4$
Hence, $F_{R}=-\dfrac{4 T}{l} x$
If $a$ is the acceleration of ball toward mean position, $a=-\left(\dfrac{4 T}{m l}\right) x$
Here acceleration is directly proportional to $x$ thus motion of ball is simple harmonic with angular frequency $\omega$,
Here, $\omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{4 T}{m l}}$
Thus, time period of its $SHM,$
$T = \frac{{2\pi }}{\omega } = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{{ml}}{{4T}}} = \pi \sqrt {\frac{{\frac{4}{{10{\pi ^2}}} \times 1}}{{10}}} = 0.2\,s$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion
parent_topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A simple pendulum $50\,cm$ long is suspended from the roof of a cart accelerating in the horizontal direction with constant acceleration $\sqrt 3 \,gm/{s^2}.$ The period of small oscillations of the pendulum about its equilibrium position  ${\left(g=\pi^{2} {~m} / {s}^{2}\right)}$ is
explanation:
With respect to the cart, equilibrium position of the pendulum is shown. If displaced by small angle ${\theta}$ from this position, then it will execute SHM about this equilibrium position, time period of which is given by https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950137/preptracker/questions/q_41023_explanation_1764950137117.png ${T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{g_{\text {etj }}}}}$ where ${g_{eff{\text{ }}}} = \sqrt {{g^2} + {{(\sqrt {3g} )}^2}} = 2g$
${\Rightarrow \quad T=1.0 {~s}}$

question: A small ball is suspended by a thread of length ${l=1 {~m}}$ at the point ${O}$ on the wall, forming a small angle ${\alpha=2^{\circ}}$ with the vertical (as shown in figure). Then the thread with ball was deviated through a small angle ${\beta=4^{\circ}}$ and set free. Assuming the collision of the ball against the wall to be perfectly elastic, the oscillation period of such a pendulum  is  (Take $g = {\pi ^2}$).
explanation:
The time period of free oscillation of pendulum
${T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}}=2 s}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956182/preptracker/questions/q_41019_explanation_1764956181625.png Time taken by bob to go from extreme position ${A}$ to mean position ${B}$ is ${=\dfrac{T}{4}}$
Time taken by bob to move from mean position ${B}$ to position ${C}$ (where its angular displacement ${\alpha}$ is half the angular amplitude ${\beta}$) is found from equation ${\alpha=\beta \sin \left(\dfrac{2 \pi}{T} t\right)}$
Solving we get ${t=\dfrac{T}{12}}$
${\Rightarrow}$ Total time period of oscillation
$=2\left[\dfrac{T}{4}+\dfrac{T}{12}\right]=\dfrac{2}{3} T=\dfrac{4}{3} {~s}$

question: The period of oscillations of mass ${m=200 {~g}}$ poured into a bent tube (see figure), whose right arm makes an angle ${30^{\circ}}$ with the vertical and whose left arm is vertical, is ${T}$ seconds. The cross-sectional area of the tube is ${A=0.5 {~cm}^{2}}$. The value of ${10 T}$ is   Neglect viscosity.
explanation:
${T=\sqrt{\dfrac{m}{A \rho g\left(\sin \theta_{1}+\sin \theta_{2}\right)}}}$
${=\sqrt{\dfrac{0.2}{0.5 \times 10^{-4} 9.8\left(\sin 90^{\circ}+\sin 60^{\circ}\right) \times 13.6 \times 10^{3}}}}$
$T = \sqrt {\frac{{0.2}}{{0.5 \times 9.8\left( {1 + \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}} \right) \times 13.6 \times {{10}^{ - 1}}}}} $
$ = 0.8\,s$
${\therefore \quad 10 T=8}$

question: Three identical ideal springs, each of force constant ${k}$ are joined to three identical balls (each of mass ${m}$ ) as shown in the figure. ${O}$ is centroid of the triangle. Initially, each of the springs is in its natural length. Now all the three balls are simultaneously given small displacements of equal magnitude along the directions shown in the figure. The oscillation frequency of balls is ${\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{\beta k}{2 m}}}$.  Find  the value of  ${\beta}$ is
explanation:
In the figure, ${\theta=30^{\circ}}$.
When displacement is each ball ${x}$, the extension in each spring is ${x^{\prime}=x \cos \theta=x \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}$
Each spring is connected to two balls.
So, net extension of each spring ${=2 x^{\prime}=\sqrt{3} x}$
Force along the direction of displacement on each ball is
${F=k \sqrt{3} x \cos 30^{\circ}+k \sqrt{3} x \cos 30^{\circ}=3 k x}$
${F=-3 k x}$ [negative because restoring in nature]
$\begin{aligned}& a=\dfrac{F}{m}=-\dfrac{3 k}{m} x \Rightarrow \omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{3 k}{m}} \\& f=\dfrac{2}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{3 k}{m}}=\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{6 k}{2 m}} \Rightarrow \beta=6\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question:
A sphere of mass ${M}$ and radius ${R}$ is on a smooth fixed inclined plane in equilibrium as shown in the figure. If now the sphere is displaced through a small distance along the plane, what will be the angular frequency of the resulting $SHM$ ?
(Given, ${k=\dfrac{4 M}{3}}$)
explanation:
If the sphere moves down along the plane by a distance ${x}$, extension in the spring ${=x \cos 30^{\circ}}$
${\therefore \quad}$ Restoring force ${=F \cos 30^{\circ}}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950131/preptracker/questions/q_40975_explanation_1764950130840.png $=\left(k x \cos 30^{\circ}\right) \cos 30^{\circ}=k x\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{3}{4} k x$
As it is restoring in nature, ${F_{R}=-\dfrac{3}{4} k x}$
${a=-\dfrac{3}{4} \cdot \dfrac{k}{M} x}$
$\omega = \sqrt {\frac{3}{4} \cdot \frac{k}{M}} = \sqrt {\frac{3}{{4M}} \cdot \frac{{4M}}{3}} = 1\,rad\,{s^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion
parent_topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A uniform rod of length $l$ and mass $m = 1\,kg$ is hinged at its lowest point $\mathrm{O}$ and is connected at its highest point $A$ by means of a spring of spring constant $k$ (in fig.). When it is pushed slightly, what is the frequency of oscillation? (Take $l = 2\,m,k = 10 \,units,{\rm{ }}\sqrt {10}  = 3.14$ )
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950128/preptracker/questions/q_40951_explanation_1764950128031.png Total $E = {E_{rotational{\rm{ }}}} + {E_{transitional{\rm{ }}}}$
$E = \frac{1}{3}\,m{L^2}{\left( {\frac{{d\theta }}{{dt}}} \right)^2} - mg\frac{L}{2}(1 - \cos \theta ) + \frac{1}{2}k\,{(L\theta )^2}$
$ = \frac{1}{3}\,m{L^2}{\left( {\frac{{d\theta }}{{dt}}} \right)^2} - mg\frac{L}{2}\left[ {1 - \left( {1 - \frac{{{\theta ^2}}}{2}} \right)} \right] + $
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\frac{1}{2}\,k{L^2}{\theta ^2}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left[ {\because {\text{As}}\theta \to {0^c} \Rightarrow \cos \theta \approx 1 - \frac{{{\theta ^2}}}{2}} \right]$
$ = \frac{1}{3}\,m{L^2}{\left( {\frac{{d\theta }}{{dt}}} \right)^2} + \frac{1}{2}\left( {\,k{L^2} - \frac{{mgL}}{2}} \right){\theta ^2}$
Angular frequency,
$\omega = \sqrt {\frac{{{\rm{Spring}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{factor}}}}{{{\rm{Inertial}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{factor}}}}} $
$ = \sqrt {\frac{{k{L^2} - \frac{{mgL}}{2}}}{{\frac{1}{3}\,m{L^2}}}} $
$ = \sqrt {\frac{{3k}}{m} - \frac{{3\,g}}{{2\,L}}} $
$ = \sqrt {\frac{{3 \times 10}}{1} - \frac{{3 \times 10}}{{2 \times 2}}} $
$ = 3\sqrt {\frac{{10}}{4}} $
$ = \frac{3}{2} \times 3.14$
$\therefore \omega = 4.71\,rad/s$
subject: Physics

parent_topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: Two blocks each of mass $m = 1\,kg$ are connected to the spring of spring constant $k = 2$ units (as in fig.) If the blocks are displaced slightly in opposite directions and released, they will execute SHM. What is the time period of oscillation  ?
explanation:
Reduced mass of the system,
$\mu = \frac{{m(m)}}{{m + m}} = \frac{m}{2}$
$\therefore $ Time period $T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{\mu }{k}} $
$ = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{1}{{2 \times 2}}} $
$ = \pi $
$\therefore T = 3.14\,s$

question:
A body of mass $'m'$ is attached to one end of a massless spring which is suspended vertically from a fixed point. The mass is held in hand, so that the spring is neither stretched nor compressed. Suddenly, the support of the hand is removed. The lowest position attained by the mass during oscillation is $8\,cm$ below the point, where it was held in hand. The frequency of oscillation is
(Take $\sqrt {9.8} = \pi $ )
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950117/preptracker/questions/q_40923_explanation_1764950117537.png Loss in $GPE = $ Gain in elastic $P.E.$.
$ \Rightarrow mgx = \frac{1}{2}k{x^2}$
$ \Rightarrow x = \frac{{2mg}}{k}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{2mg}}{k} = 8 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,m$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{m}{k} = \frac{{8 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}{{2\,g}}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{k}{m} = \frac{g}{{4 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}$
$\therefore v = \frac{1}{{2\pi }}\sqrt {\frac{k}{m}} $
$ = \frac{1}{{2 \times \pi }}\sqrt {\frac{{9.8}}{{4 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}} $
$ = \frac{{10 \times \pi }}{{2 \times \pi \times 2}}$
$ = \frac{{10}}{4} = 2.5\,Hz$

question:
Two bodies of masses $1\,kg$ and $5\,kg$ are connected to a vertical spring (as shown in figure). The smaller mass executes SHM of angular frequency $16\,rad/s$, and amplitude $2\,cm$ while the bigger mass remains stationary on the ground. Find the maximum force exerted by the system on the floor.
(Take $g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$ )
explanation:
$ \Rightarrow \omega = \sqrt {\frac{k}{m}} \Rightarrow 16 = \sqrt {\frac{k}{1}} $
$ \Rightarrow k = 256\,N{m^{ - 1}}$
$F = Mg + kx + mg$
$ = 5 \times 10 + 256 \times 2 \times {10^{ - 2}} + 1 \times 10$
$\therefore F = 65.12\,N$

question:
A particle of mass $m$ connected with a spring of force constant $k$ is executing SHM with amplitude$A = 1\,m$. When it is at $ + \frac{A}{2}$ from equilibrium position moving towards right, it receives an impulse which doubles its velocity without changing direction. Its amplitude after the impulse is
(Take $\sqrt {13} = 3.61$ )
explanation:
$T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{m}{k}} $
$v = \omega \sqrt {{A^2} - {y^2}} $
$ = \frac{{2\pi }}{T}\sqrt {{A^2} - {y^2}} $
Here, $y = A/2$
$ \Rightarrow v = \frac{{2\pi }}{T}\sqrt {{A^2} - {{(A/2)}^2}} = \frac{{\pi \sqrt 3 \,A}}{{\,T}}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956166/preptracker/questions/q_40883_explanation_1764956166480.png After impulse, $v' = 2v = \frac{{2\pi \sqrt 3 \,A}}{{\,T}}$
After impulse, $T,\omega ,m,k$ remains constant but amplitude will change.
${v^\prime } = \frac{{2\pi \sqrt 3 \,A}}{{\,T}} = \frac{{2\pi }}{T}\sqrt {{A_1}^2 - {y^2}} $
$ \Rightarrow \sqrt 3 \,A = \sqrt {{A_1}^2 - {y^2}} $
$ \Rightarrow 3\,{A^2} = {A_1}^2 - {\left( {\frac{A}{2}} \right)^2}$
$ \Rightarrow {A_1} = \frac{{\sqrt {13} }}{2}\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} A = \frac{{\sqrt {13} }}{2} \times 1$
$\therefore \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {A_1} = \frac{{3.61}}{2}\,m = 1.81\,m$
subject: Physics

parent_topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question:
A uniform disc of mass $m = 2\,kg$ and radius $R = 5\,cm$ is pivoted smoothly at its centre of mass. A light spring of stiffness $k$ is attached with the disc tangentially (as shown in figure). Find the angular frequency of torsional oscillation of the disc is
(Take $k = 5N/s,\sqrt 5 = 2.23)$
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950095/preptracker/questions/q_40871_explanation_1764950095504.png If disc is twisted clockwise for a small angle $\theta$, deformation in spring $(x) = R\theta $
$ \Rightarrow {F_s} = kx = k(R\theta )$
${\tau _{res{\rm{ }}}} = - {F_s}R$
$ = - k{R^2}\theta \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Also $\tau = {I_c}\alpha \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From (1) & (2)
$ \Rightarrow \alpha = \frac{{{k^2}\theta }}{{{I_c}}}$
$ = - \frac{{2k}}{m}\theta \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left[ {\therefore \,{I_c} = \frac{{m{R^2}}}{2}} \right]$
Comparing with standard equation,
${\omega ^2}\theta = \frac{{ - 2k}}{m}\theta $
$\therefore \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} \omega = \sqrt {\frac{{2k}}{m}} $
$ = \sqrt {\frac{{2 \times 5}}{2}} $
$\therefore \omega = 2.23\,rad/s$
subject: Physics

parent_topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question:
For the arrangement shown in figure, the spring is initially compressed by $3cm$. When the spring is released the block collides with the wall and rebounds to compress the spring again. The maximum compression in the spring after collision (coefficient of restitution is 0.7) is
(Take $\sqrt 5 = 2.24,{\mkern 1mu} \,k = {10^4}N/m$)
explanation:
From Energy conservation, $\frac{1}{2}m{v_0}^2 + \frac{1}{2}k{x^2} = \frac{1}{2}k{x_0}^2$
$ \Rightarrow {v_0} = \sqrt {\frac{k}{m}\left( {{x_0}^2 - {x^2}} \right)} .$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,[{\rm{velocity}}\,{\rm{of}}\,{\rm{block}}\,{\rm{just}}\,{\rm{before}}\,{\rm{collision}}]$
Here ${x_0} = 0.03\,m,\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} x = 0.01\,m,$
$k = {10^4}\,N/m,m = 1\,kg$
${v_0} = \sqrt {\frac{{{{10}^4}}}{1}\left[ {{{(0.03)}^2} - {{(0.01)}^2}} \right]} $
$ = \sqrt {{{10}^4} \times {{10}^{ - 4}}[9 - 1]} $
$ = \sqrt {{{10}^4} \times {{10}^{ - 4}}[9 - 1]} $
$ = 2\sqrt 2 \,m/s$
After collision, $v = e{v_0}$
$ = (0.7)2\sqrt 2 $
$ = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }} \times 2\sqrt 2 $
$ = 2\,m/s$
$\,\,\,\,\,\left[ \because \right.{\text{Take}}\left. {0.7 \approx \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}} \right]$
maximum compression in the spring;
$\frac{1}{2}kx_m^2 = \frac{1}{2}k{x^2} + \frac{1}{2}m{v^2}$
$ \Rightarrow {x_m} = \sqrt {{x^2} + \frac{m}{k}{v^2}} = \sqrt {{{(0.01)}^2} + \frac{{1{{(2)}^2}}}{{{{10}^4}}}} $
$ = \frac{{\sqrt {1 + 4} }}{{{{10}^2}}} = \frac{{2.24}}{{{{10}^2}}}\,m$
$\therefore \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {x_m} = 2.24\,cm$

question: Two bodies ${M}$ and ${N}$ of equal masses are suspended from two separate springs of constants ${k_{1}}$ and ${k_{2}}$ respectively. If ${M}$ and ${N}$ oscillate vertically so that their maximum velocities are equal, the ratio of amplitude of vibration of ${M}$ to that of ${N}$ is
explanation:

${\because v_{\max _{1}}=v_{\max _{2}}}$
${A_{1} \sqrt{\dfrac{k_{1}}{m}}=A_{2} \sqrt{\dfrac{k_{2}}{m}}}$
${\Rightarrow \dfrac{A_{1}}{A_{2}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}}}$.
So correct option is (3)
