question: A pendulum of length $1 m$ is released from $\theta=60^{\circ}$. The rate of change of speed of the bob at $\theta=30^{\circ}$ is (Take, $g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$ )
explanation:
For a simple pendulum, time period,
$\begin{align*}& T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{2 \pi}{T}=\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{l}} \\& \therefore \quad \omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{l}}, \quad \omega^{2}=\dfrac{g}{l} \tag{1}\end{align*}$
Amplitude when angular displacement is $60^{\circ}$
$=\dfrac{2 \pi l}{360^{\circ}} \times 60^{\circ}=\dfrac{2 \pi l}{6}$
Therefore, displacement when angular displacement is $30^{\circ}$
$\begin{equation*}=\dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{2 \pi l}{6}\right), \quad y=\dfrac{\pi l}{6} \tag{2}\end{equation*}$
Acceleration, $a=-\omega^{2} y$
Using Eqs. (1) and (2), we get
$a = - \frac{g}{l} \times \frac{{\pi l}}{6} = - \frac{{10 \times 3.14}}{6}$
$ = - 5.2\,m{s^{ - 2}} \simeq 5\,m{s^{ - 2}}$
subject: Physics

question: The time period of a simple pendulum in a stationary van is $T$. The time period of a mass attached to a spring is also $T$. The van accelerates at the rate $5\,m{s^{ - 2}}.$ If the new time periods of the pendulum and spring be $T_{p}$ and   $T_{s}$ respectively, then
explanation:
Time period of simple pendulum placed in a van accelerating at the rate of a $m{s^{ - 2}}$ is given by https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956154/preptracker/questions/q_40765_explanation_1764956154095.png $T=2 \pi\left[\dfrac{l}{\sqrt{g^{2}+a^{2}}}\right]^{\frac{1}{2}}$
However, the time period of mass attached to the spring is
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{m}{k}\right)}$
It is independent of $g$ as well as $a$. Hence, when the van accelerates, the time period of the simple pendulum decreases and that of spring remains unchanged.
Hence, $T_{p} < T$ and $T_{s}=T$
i.e. $\quad T_{p} < T_{s}$

question: A pendulum is made to hang from the ceiling of an elevator. It has period of $T s$ (for small angles). The elevator is made to accelerate upwards with $10\,m/{s^2}.$ The period of the pendulum now will be (Take, $g = 10\,m/{s^2}$ )
explanation:
When the elevator is at rest, its time period is given by
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{10}}$
When the elevator accelerates upwards, its time period becomes
$T^{\prime}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g+a}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{10+10}}$
$[\because T-m g=m a \Rightarrow T=m(g+a)]$
$=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{20}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{10}} \times \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=\dfrac{T}{\sqrt{2}}$

question: A clock $S$ is based on oscillation of a spring and a clock $P$ is based on pendulum motion. Both clocks run at the same rate on the earth. On a planet having the same density as earth but twice the radius

$g=\dfrac{G M}{R^{2}}=\dfrac{G \times \dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^{3} \rho}{R^{2}}=\dfrac{4}{3} \pi G \rho R$
$\Rightarrow g \propto R$
For pendulum clock, $g$ will increase on the planet so time period will decrease. But for spring clock, it will not change. Hence, $P$ will run faster than $S$

question: The length of the second pendulum is decreased by $0.3\,cm$ when it is shifted to Chennai from London. If the acceleration due to gravity at London is $981\,\,cm/{s^2},$ the acceleration due to gravity at Chennai is (assume $\pi^{2}=10$ )
explanation:
As, $L_{1}=\dfrac{g_{1} T^{2}}{4 \pi^{2}}=\dfrac{g_{1}}{\pi^{2}}\left[\because T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}}\right]$
and $\quad L_{2}=\dfrac{g_{2} T^{2}}{4 \pi^{2}}=\dfrac{g_{2}}{\pi^{2}}$
Since, length is decreased, $g_{2}$ is less than $g_{1}$.
$\therefore \quad L_{1}-L_{2}=\dfrac{g_{1}-g_{2}}{\pi^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad\left(L_{1}-L_{2}\right) \pi^{2}=g_{1}-g_{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 0.3 \times 10=g_{1}-g_{2}$
$ \Rightarrow \quad {g_2} = 981 - 3 = 978\,cm/{s^2}$

question: Four massless springs whose force constants are ${2 k, 2 k, k}$, and ${2 k}$, respectively are attached to a mass ${M}$, kept on a frictionless plane (as shown in figure). If the mass ${M}$ is displaced in the horizontal direction, then find the frequency of the system.
explanation:
Spring on the left of the block are in series, hence there equivalent is
$k_{1}=\dfrac{(2 k)(2 k)}{2 k+2 k}=k$
Springs on the right of the block are in parallel, hence there equivalent is
$k_{2}=k+2 k=3 k$
Now again both ${K_{1}}$ and ${K_{2}}$ are in parallel
$\therefore \quad k_{\text {eq. }}=k_{1}+k_{2}=k+3 k=4 k$
Hence, frequency is
$f=\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{k_{e q}}{M}}=\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{4 k}{M}}$

question: For a simple harmonic motion in a mass spring system shown, the surface is frictionless. When the mass of the block is $1\,kg$, the angular frequency is ${\omega _1}$. When the mass is $2\,kg$ the angular frequency is ${\omega _2}$. The ratio ${\omega _2}/{\omega _1}$ is
explanation:

$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{k}}$ and $T=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\omega}$
$\dfrac{2 \pi}{\omega}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{k}} ; \quad \omega^{2}=\dfrac{k}{m}$
or $\omega _1^2 = \frac{k}{m} = \frac{k}{1}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
and $\omega _2^2 = \frac{k}{{{m_2}}} = \frac{k}{2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Dividing equations (1) and (2)

question: Assume that the earth is a solid sphere of uniform density and a tunnel is dug along its diameter throughout the earth. It is found that when a particle is released in this tunnel, it executes a simple harmonic motion. The mass of the particle is $100\,g$. The time period of the motion of the particle will be (approximately) (Take $g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$, radius of earth $ = 6400\,km$)
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956145/preptracker/questions/q_40573_explanation_1764956145360.png At point $\mathrm{P}$ inside the tunnel $g_{p}=g \dfrac{x}{R}$, where $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity on the surface of earth and $R$ is radius.
$\therefore$ For the particle,
$m \dfrac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}=-m g_{p}=-m g \dfrac{x}{R}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}=-\left(\dfrac{g}{R}\right) x=-\omega^{2} x$, where $\omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}}$
Therefore the motion is simple harmonic.
$\therefore T=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\omega}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}}$
$=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{64 \times 10^{5}}{10}} \sec =\dfrac{2 \pi \times 800}{60 \times 60} h r$
$ \Rightarrow T = 1\,hr \cdot 24\,\min $.

question:
Assertion: For simple pendulum of vary large $(\rightarrow \infty)$ length, period is $84.6 \mathrm{~min}$.

Reason: The time period of a simple pendulum is proportional to directly the square root of length.
explanation:
When length of pendulum is too high, the direction of $g$ will not be vertically down.(rather, it will be towards centre of earth).
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{g\left(\dfrac{1}{l}+\dfrac{1}{R_{E}}\right)}}$
where $R_{E}=$ radius of earth.
As $l \rightarrow \infty,\left(\dfrac{1}{l}\right) \rightarrow 0 \Rightarrow T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{R_{E}}{g}}=84.6 \mathrm{~min}$
(Using values of $R_{E}, g$ )
Reason is about general formula $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}}$
$\Rightarrow T \propto \sqrt{l}$
So correct option is (2).

$\Rightarrow k^{\prime}=n k$

question:
Assertion: The frequency of a second pendulum in an elevator moving up with an acceleration half the acceleration due to gravity is $0.612\,{s^{ - 1}}$.

Reason: The frequency of a second pendulum does not depend upon acceleration due to gravity.
explanation:
$T=2 s, l \approx 1 m$ (without elevator)
$f=\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{l}}$
$f=\dfrac{1}{T}=\dfrac{1}{2}=0.5 \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$
When elevator accelerates up with acceleration $\left(\dfrac{g}{2}\right)$ we have $g^{\prime}=g-\left(-\dfrac{g}{2}\right)=\dfrac{3 g}{2}$
$f' = \frac{1}{{2\pi }}\sqrt {\frac{{g'}}{l}} $
$\Rightarrow f^{\prime}=\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{3}{2}}\right) \times 0.5=0.612 s^{-1}$
The frequency of a second pendulum depends on the acceleration due to gravity. If the elevator is moving with an acceleration, it will affect the apparent gravity experienced by the pendulum, and thus, the frequency. The Reason is false. So correct option is (3).

(Since $l=$ length from point of suspension to centre of gravity)
So correct option is (4).

question: A particle under the action of a SHM has a period of $3\,{\rm{sec}}$ and under the effect of another it has a period $4\,{\rm{sec}}$. What will be its period under the combined action of both the SHM's in the same direction?
explanation:

When both are combined
$\begin{aligned}K_{n e t} & =K+K_{1} \\& =\dfrac{25}{16} K\end{aligned}$
New time period will become $\dfrac{4}{3}$ times of $3\,{\rm{sec}}$ or $2.4\,{\rm{sec}}$

question: The angular amplitude of a simple pendulum is $\theta_{0}$. The maximum tension in its string will be
explanation:
The simple pendulum at angular amplitude $\theta_{0}$ is shown in figure. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951060/preptracker/questions/q_40445_explanation_1764951060176.png ${T_{\max }} = mg + \frac{{m{v^2}}}{l}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
When bob of the pendulum comes form $A$ to $B$, it covers a vertical distance $h$.
$\therefore \cos \theta_{0}=\dfrac{l-h}{l}$
$ \Rightarrow h = l\left( {1 - \cos {\theta _0}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Also, during $B$ to $A$, potential energy of bob converts into kinetic energy, i.e., $m g h=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$.
$\therefore v = \sqrt {2gh} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 3 \right)$
Thus, using Eqs. (1), (2) and (3), we obtain
$T_{\max }=m g+\dfrac{2 m g}{l}$
$\left(1-\cos \theta_{0}\right)$
$=m g+2 m g\left[1-1+\dfrac{\theta_{0}^{2}}{2}\right]=m g\left(1+\theta_{0}^{2}\right)$

question: A rectangular block of mass ' $M$ ' and cross sectional area ' $A$ ' floats on a liquid of density ' $\rho$ '. It is given a small vertical displacement from equilibrium, it starts oscillating with frequency ' $n$ ' then

Weight of block = weight of the displaced liquid

question:
Assertion: The percentage change in time period is $1.5 \%$. If the length of simple pendulum increases by $3 \%$.


Reason: The period is directly proportional to length of pendulum.
explanation:
The percentage change in the time period of a simple pendulum is $1.5 \%$ if the length of the pendulum increases by $3 \%$. This is a valid assertion and can be explained using the formula for the period of a simple pendulum, which is $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}}$. Here, "l" is the length of the pendulum, and " $\mathrm{g}$ " is the acceleration due to gravity.
The period of a simple pendulum is not directly proportional to the length of the pendulum; it is inversely proportional to the square root of the length of the pendulum.
So, option (3) is the correct choice. Assertion is correct, but Reason is incorrect.

question: The mass and diameter of a planet are twice those of the earth. The period of oscillation of pendulum on this planet will be (if it is a second's pendulum on earth)
explanation:
Gravity, $g = \frac{{GM}}{{{R^2}}}$
( $G$ is constant)
$\therefore \dfrac{g_{\text {earth }}}{g_{\text {planet }}}=\dfrac{M_{e}}{M_{p}} \times \dfrac{R_{p}^{2}}{R_{e}^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{g_{e}}{g_{p}}=\dfrac{2}{1}$
Also, $T \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{g}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{T_{e}}{T_{p}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{g_{p}}{g_{e}}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{2}{T_{p}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{2}} \Rightarrow T_{p}=2 \sqrt{2} s$
So, correct option is (2)

question: Two simple pendulums first of bob mass $M_{1}$ ${\rm{(bob }}A{\rm{)}}$ and length $L_{1}$, second of bob mass $M_{2}$(bob $B$ ) and length $L_{2} \cdot M_{2}=M_{1}$ and $L_{1}=2 L_{2}$. If the vibrational energies of both are same. Then, which is correct?
explanation:
Frequency, $n=\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{l}}$
$\Rightarrow n \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{l}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{n_{1}}{n_{2}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{l_{2}}{l_{1}}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{L_{2}}{2 L_{1}}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{n_{1}}{n_{2}}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$
$\Rightarrow n_{2}=\sqrt{2} n_{1}$
$\Rightarrow n_{2}>n_{1}$
Energy, $E=\dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^{2} A^{2}=2 \pi^{2} m n^{2} A^{2}$ and $A^{2} \propto \dfrac{1}{m n^{2}}(\because E$ is same $)$
$\therefore \dfrac{A_{1}^{2}}{A_{2}^{2}}=\dfrac{m_{2} n_{2}^{2}}{m_{1} n_{1}^{2}}$
Given, $n_{2}>n_{1}$ and $m_{1}=m_{2}$
$\Rightarrow A_{1}>A_{2}$
So, amplitude of bob $B$ is smaller than bob $A$.
So, correct option is (2)

question: A simple harmonic oscillator consists of a particle of mass $m$ and an ideal spring with spring constant $k$. The particle oscillates with a time period $T$. The spring is cut into two equal parts. If one part oscillates with the same particle, the time period will be
explanation:
Mass of the particle $=m$
Spring constant $=k$
Time period of oscillator, $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{k}}$
As $k \propto \dfrac{1}{l}$
(where, $l$ is the length of spring)
$\because k^{\prime}=2 k$
$\therefore T^{\prime}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{2 k}}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} T$
So, correct option is (3)

question: A block of rectangular size of mass $m$ and area of cross - section $A$, floats in a liquid of density $\rho$. If we give a small vertical displacement from equilibrium, it undergoes SHM with time period $T$, then
explanation:
According to the law of floatation, weight of the rectangular block $\downarrow=$ buoyant force $\uparrow$
$\Rightarrow m g=v \times \rho \times g$
Now, volume of rectangular block
$=$ length $\times$ breadth $\times$ height
$=l \times b \times h=l \times A$
(because $A=b \times h$ )
At equilibrium, $m g=A l \rho g$
$\Rightarrow m=A \rho l$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951578/preptracker/questions/q_40276_explanation_1764951578329.png where, $l=$ length of part immersed in liquid.
When it is given in downward displacement $y$, restoring force (upward direction) on block is
$\begin{aligned}& F=-[A(l+y) \rho g-m g] \\& =-[A(l+y) \rho g-A l \rho g] \\& =-A \rho g y\end{aligned}$
$i.e.{\rm{ }}F \propto - y\,{\rm{or }}a \propto - y,$ so it executes SHM
Mass of block $=m$
Spring factor $=A \rho g$
Time period $=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{\text { Inertia factor }}{\text { Spring factor }}}$
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{A \rho g}} \Rightarrow T^{2} \propto \dfrac{m}{A \rho}$
So, correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: Two massless springs with spring constant $2\,K$ and $2\,K$, carry $20\,g$ and $90\,g$ masses at their free ends. These two masses oscillate vertically such that their maximum velocities are equal. Then, the ratio of their respective amplitudes will be:

$\Rightarrow \omega_{1} A_{1}=\omega_{2} A_{2}$
$\dfrac{A_{1}}{A_{2}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{m_{2} K_{1}}{m_{1} K_{2}}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{90 \times K}{20 \times 2 K}}=3: 2 \text {. }$

question: A simple pendulum attached to ceiling of lift has time period $T$ when lift is at rest. Find its time period of lift if it starts accelerating upwards with acceleration $g/2$ ?

${T^\prime } = T\sqrt {\frac{2}{3}} $

question: A uniform cylinder of length $L$ and mass $M$ having cross-sectional area $A$ is suspended, with its length vertical, from a fixed point by a massless spring, such that it is half submerged in a liquid of density $\sigma$ at equilibrium position. When the cylinder is given a downward push and released, it starts oscillating vertically with a small amplitude. The time period $T$ of the oscillations of the cylinder will be
explanation:
Let $x_{0}=$ Submerged length in equilibrium
$x=$ Additional submerged length at time $t$ At equilibrium
$\therefore \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} Mg = A{x_0}\sigma g + k{x_0}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
$ - M\frac{{{d^2}x}}{{d{t^2}}} = A\left( {{x_0} + x} \right)\sigma g - Mg + k\left( {x + {x_0}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From (1) and (2) $\therefore \quad M \dfrac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}=-(A \sigma g+k) x$
$\begin{aligned}& \therefore \quad \omega^{2}=\dfrac{A \sigma g+k}{M} \\& \therefore \quad T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{(A \sigma g+k)}}\end{aligned}$

question: A wire is bent at an angle $\theta$. A rod of mass $m$ can slide along the bent wire without friction as shown in Fig. Soap film is maintained in the frame kept in a vertical position and the rod is in equilibrium as shown in the figure. If rod is displaced slightly in vertical direction, then the time period of small oscillation of the rod is
explanation:
Let $S$ be the surface tension of the soap film. For equilibrium of rod https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951058/preptracker/questions/q_40073_explanation_1764951058122.jpg $2(l+y) \tan \dfrac{\theta}{2}=$ Length of the boundary of soap film in contact with the rod.
$\begin{gathered}m g=\left(F_{\text {surface }}\right)_{1} \\m g=\left(2 l \tan \dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) S \times 2 ; m g=4 S l \tan \dfrac{\theta}{2}\end{gathered}$
If the rod is displaced from its mean position by small displacement $y$, then restoring force on the rod is
$\begin{gathered}F=-\left[4 S(l+y) \tan \dfrac{\theta}{2}-m g\right]=-\left[4 S \tan \dfrac{\theta}{2}\right] y \\a=-\dfrac{4 S \tan \dfrac{\theta}{2}}{m} y=\dfrac{-4 S \tan \left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) y}{\left(\dfrac{4 S l \tan \dfrac{\theta}{2}}{g}\right)} \\\dfrac{d^{2} y}{d t^{2}}=-\left(\dfrac{g}{l}\right) y \quad \therefore T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}}\end{gathered}$

question: An air chamber of volume $V$, has a long neck of cross-sectional area A. A ball of mass $m$ is fitted smoothly in the neck. The bulk modulus of air is $B$. If the ball is pressed down slightly and released, the time period of its oscillation is
explanation:
Let the ball is pressed down by a small amount $\mathrm{y}$, then the volume of air decreases by Ay.
Then, excess pressure is
$d p=-B\left(\dfrac{d V}{V}\right)=-B\left(\dfrac{y A}{V}\right)$
A restoring force $F(A d P)$ acts in the upward direction.
$\therefore \quad F=A\left[-B\left(\dfrac{y A}{V}\right)\right]=-\dfrac{B A^{2}}{V} \cdot y$
$\therefore \quad a=-\dfrac{B A^{2}}{m V} y$ comparing this with $a=-\omega^{2} y$
$\begin{aligned}\omega & =\sqrt{\dfrac{B A^{2}}{m V}} \\\Rightarrow \quad T & =\dfrac{2 \pi}{\omega}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m V}{B A^{2}}}\end{aligned}$

question: A block $A$ of mass $m$ is connected to spring and performs simple harmonic motion with a time period of $2 s$. Another block $B$ of mass $m$ rests on $A$. The coefficient of static friction betwwen $A$ and $B$ is $\mu_{s}=0.6$. The maximum amplitude of oscillation which the system can have so that there is no relative motion between $A$ and $B$ is (take $\pi^{2}=g=10$ )
explanation:
Let $A$ be the maximum amplitude of oscillation for the required situation.
Maximum force on block $B$ is
$F=M a_{\text {max }}=m \omega^{2} A$
For no slipping $F_{\max } < f_{L}$
The maximum frictional force between the blocks is
$f_{L}=\mu_{s} m g$
i.e., $\quad m \omega^{2} A < \mu_{s} m g$
$ = A < \frac{{0.6 \times g}}{{\frac{{4{\pi ^2}}}{4}}} = 0.6 \Rightarrow \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {A_{\max }} = 0.6\,m$

question: A block $P$ of mass $m$ is placed on a smooth horizontal surface. A block $Q$ of same mass is placed over the block $P$ and the coefficient of static friction between them is $\mu_{s}$. A spring of spring constant $K$ is attached to block $Q$. The blocks are displaced together to a distance A and released. The upper block oscillates without slipping over the lower block. The maximum frictional force between the block is
explanation:
Angular frequency of system,
$\omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{K}{m+m}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{K}{2 m}}$
Maximum acceleration, ${a_{\max }} = {\omega ^2}\,A$
Frictional force between $P$ and $Q$
$=$ force exerted on lower block
$=m \omega^{2} A=m\left(\dfrac{K}{2 m}\right) A=\dfrac{K A}{2}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion
parent_topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A horizontal rod of mass $m$ and length $L$ is pivoted at one end. The rod's other end is supported by a spring of force constant $k$. The rod is displaced by a small angle $\theta$ from its horizontal equilibrium position and released. The angular frequency of the subsequent simple harmonic motion is
explanation:
Restoring torque
$\tau = KyL = \frac{{m{L^2}}}{3}\alpha {\rm{ (Since }}y \cong L\theta {\rm{ from figure)}}$
$ \Rightarrow K{L^2}\theta = \frac{{m{L^2}}}{3}\alpha \Rightarrow \frac{{{d^2}\theta }}{{d{t^2}}} = - \frac{{3k}}{m}\theta $
$ \Rightarrow T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{\theta }{\alpha }} = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{{m\theta }}{{3k\theta }}} = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{m}{{3k}}} $ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951054/preptracker/questions/q_40057_explanation_1764951054072.jpg $\omega = \sqrt {\frac{{3k}}{m}} $

question: A uniform disc of mass $m$ and radius $R$ is connected with two light springs 1 and 2 . The springs are connected at the highest point $M$ and the $CM$ ' $N$ ' of the disc. The other ends of the springs are rigidly attached with vertical walls. If we shift the CM in horizontal by a small distance, the disc oscillates simple harmonically. Assuming a perfect rolling of the disc on the horizontal surface, find the angular frequency of oscillation.
explanation:
Let $x$ be the displacement of CM from mean position. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956969/preptracker/questions/q_40053_explanation_1764956968817.jpg The torque about $\mathrm{O}$ is
$\begin{aligned}& \tau_{o}=K_{2} x R+K_{1}(2 x) 2 R=I_{o} \alpha \\& \tan \theta=\theta=\dfrac{x}{R}=\dfrac{2 x}{2 R} \\& K_{2} R^{2} \theta+4 K_{1} R^{2} \theta=I_{o} \dfrac{d^{2} \theta}{d t^{2}} \\& \dfrac{d^{2} \theta}{d t^{2}}=-\dfrac{R^{2} \theta\left[K_{2}+4 K_{1}\right]}{\left(\dfrac{m R^{2}}{2}+m R^{2}\right)} \\& \omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{2\left(4 K_{1}+K_{2}\right)}{3 m} .}\end{aligned}$

question: A simple pendulum has a length $l$. The inertia and gravitational masses of the bob are $m_{i}$ and $m_{g}$ respectively. Then the time period $T$ is given by
explanation:
Torque acting on the bob
$=l \alpha=-(m g) l \sin \theta$
or $\quad\left(m_{i} l^{2}\right) \alpha=-\left(m_{g} g\right) l \theta$
or $\quad \alpha=-\left(\dfrac{m_{g} g}{m_{i} l}\right) \theta=-\omega^{2} \theta$
where, $\omega^{2}=\dfrac{m_{g} g}{m_{i} l} \quad \therefore T=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\omega}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m_{i} l}{m_{g} g}}$
subject: Physics

question: $A$ and $B$ are fixed points and the mass $M$ is tied by strings at $A$ and $B$. If the mass $M$ is displaced slightly out of this plane and released, it will execute oscillations with period. (Given, $AM = BM = L,AB = 2\,d$ )
explanation:
The motion of $M$ is SHM, with length
$CM = \sqrt {{L^2} - {d^2}} $
$T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{{{{\left( {{L^2} - {d^2}} \right)}^{1/2}}}}{g}} $ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956961/preptracker/questions/q_40045_explanation_1764956961341.jpg

question: A pendulum has time period $T$ for small oscillations. An obstacle is placed directly beneath the pivot, so that only the lowest one quarter of the string can follow the pendulum bob when it swings in the left of its resting position as shown in figure. The pendulum is released from rest at a certain point $A$. The time taken by it to return to the point $A$ is
explanation:
The total time for oscillation is $\dfrac{T_{1}}{2}+\dfrac{T_{2}}{2}$
$T_{1}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{g}}, T_{2}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{4 g}}$
Given that $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{g}}$
$\begin{aligned}& T_{1}=\dfrac{T}{2}, T_{2}=\dfrac{T}{4} \\& T_{N e t}=\dfrac{3 T}{4}\end{aligned}$

question: An ideal gas enclosed in a vertical cylinderical container supports a freely moving piston of mass $M$. The piston and the cylinder have equal cross sectional area A. When the piston is in equilibrium, the volume of the gas is $V_{0}$ and its pressure is $P_{0}$. The piston is slightly displaced from the equilibrium position and released. Assuming that the system is completely isolated from its surrounding, the piston executes a simple harmonic motion with frequency
explanation:
Initially at equilibrium $\dfrac{M g}{A}=P_{0}$
$\begin{aligned}& P_{0} V_{0}^{\gamma}=P V^{\gamma} \\& M g=P_{0} A\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned}& P_{0} A^{\gamma} x_{0}^{\gamma}=P A^{\gamma}\left(x_{0}-x\right)^{\gamma} \\& P=\dfrac{P_{0} x_{0}^{\gamma}}{\left(x_{0}-x\right)^{\gamma}}\end{aligned}$
Piston https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951015/preptracker/questions/q_40009_explanation_1764951015417.jpg Let piston is displaced by distance $x$
$\begin{aligned}& M g-\left(\dfrac{P_{0} x_{0}^{\gamma}}{\left(x_{0}-x\right)^{\gamma}}\right) A=F_{\text {restoring }} \\& P_{0} A\left(1-\dfrac{x_{0}^{\gamma}}{\left(x_{0}-x\right)^{\gamma}}\right)=F_{\text {restoring }}\end{aligned}$
$F=P_{o} A\left[1-\dfrac{1}{\left(1-\dfrac{x}{x_{o}}\right)^{\gamma}}\right]=P_{o} A\left[1-\left(1-\dfrac{x}{x_{o}}\right)^{-\gamma}\right]$$=P_{o} A\left[1-\left(1+\dfrac{\gamma x}{x_{o}}\right)\right]$
$F=-\dfrac{\gamma P_{0} A x}{x_{0}}$
$\therefore$ Frequency with which piston executes SHM.
$f=\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma P_{0} A}{x_{0} M}}=\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma P_{0} A^{2}}{M V_{0}}}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion
parent_topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question:
Two uniform ropes having linear mass densities $m$ and $4\,m$ are joined to form a closed loop. The loop is hanging over a fixed frictionless small pulley with the lighter rope above as shown in the figure. (in the figure equilibrium position is shown). Now if point A (joint) is slightly displaced in downward direction and released. It is found that the loop performs SHM with the period of oscillation equal to T. Find the value of
$T\left( {} \right.$ take $\left. {l = \frac{{150m}}{{4{\pi ^2}}},g = 10\,m/{s^2}} \right)$.
explanation:
Let $x$ be the instantaneous downward displacement of joint $A$.
The initial and final position are https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956975/preptracker/questions/q_40005_explanation_1764956974606.jpg $\therefore \quad(2 l \times 4 m+2 l \times m) \ddot{x}=-(4 m \times 2 x-m \times 2 x) g$
$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow \quad \ddot{x}=-\dfrac{3 g}{5 l} x=-\dfrac{3 \times 10}{5 \times \dfrac{150}{4 \pi^{2}}} \times x=-\left(\dfrac{2 \pi}{5}\right)^{2} x \\& \therefore \quad T=\dfrac{2 \pi}{2 \pi / 5} \sec =5 \mathrm{sec}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A bead of mass $m$ can slide on a frictionless wire as shown in figure. Because of the given shape of the wire near $P$, the bottom point, it can be approximated as a parabola. Near $P$, the potential energy of the bead is given $U=c x^{2}$ where $c$ is a constant and $x$ is measured from $P$. The bead, if displaced slightly from point $P$ will oscillate about $P$. The period of oscillation is
explanation:
$U=C x^{2}$
$F=-\dfrac{d U}{d x} \Rightarrow m a=-2 c x \Rightarrow a=-\dfrac{2 c}{m} x$

$\Rightarrow \omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 c}{m}} \Rightarrow T=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\omega}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{2 c}}$
subject: Physics

question: A sphere of mass $m$ and radius $r$ rolls without slipping on a rough concave surface of large radius $\mathrm{R}$. It makes small oscillations about the lowest point. Find the time period.
explanation:
Let at any instant, the body is at angular position $\theta$ with respect to the vertical line drawn from the centre $Q$. If $\phi$ is the angular displacement of the ball about its centre, then
$(R-r) \theta=r \phi \quad \therefore \theta=\left(\dfrac{r}{R-r}\right) \phi$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956112/preptracker/questions/q_39997_explanation_1764956112455.jpg (b)Restoring torque acting on the ball
$\tau=-m g \sin \theta \times r$
For small $\theta, \quad \sin \theta=\theta$
$\begin{gathered}\therefore \tau=-m g \theta \times r \\\text { or } \tau=-m g\left[\dfrac{r}{R-r}\right] \phi r=m g\left(\dfrac{r^{2}}{R-r}\right)(-\phi)\end{gathered}$
Angular acceleration $\alpha=\dfrac{m g}{I}\left(\dfrac{r^{2}}{R-r}\right)(-\phi)$
Now comparing above equation with standard equation of SHM,
$\alpha=-\omega^{2} \phi, \text { we get } \omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{m g}{I}\left(\dfrac{r^{2}}{R-r}\right)}$
Here $I$ is the moment of inertia of the rolling ball about the point of contact which is $I=(7 / 5) m r^{2}$
$\omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{m g}{\dfrac{7}{5} m r^{2}}\left(\dfrac{r^{2}}{R-r}\right)}=\sqrt{\dfrac{5}{7} \dfrac{g}{(R-r)}}$
and $\quad T=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\omega}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{7(R-r)}{5 g}}$
Alternative method:
We can imagine that when the $CM$ of the body is shifted slightly by an angle $\theta$ relative to the centre of curvature of the surface, the magnitude of acceleration of the $CM$ of the body can be given as
$a=\dfrac{g \sin \theta}{1+\dfrac{k^{2}}{r^{2}}}$
(because the body is accelerating down the inclined plane of instantaneous angle $\theta$ )
Substituting $\sin \theta=\theta=\dfrac{x}{R-r}$, we have
$a = \frac{{ - gx}}{{(R - r)\left( {1 + \frac{{{k^2}}}{{{r^2}}}} \right)}}$https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956113/preptracker/questions/q_39997_explanation_1764956113265.jpg (negative sign is given because $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{x}$ are oppositely directed)
Comparing the above equation with $a=-\omega^{2} x$, we have
$\omega^{2}=\dfrac{g}{(R-r)\left(1+\dfrac{k^{2}}{r^{2}}\right)}$
For solid sphere $k^{2}=\dfrac{2 r^{2}}{5}$.
Hence $\omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{5}{7} \dfrac{g}{(R-r)}}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion
parent_topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A body of mass $m$ hangs from a smooth light fixed pulley $P_{1}$ by the inextensible string fitted with the springs of stiffness $k_{1}$ and $k_{2}$. the string passes over the smooth light pulley $P_{2}$ which is connected with another ideal spring of stiffness $k_{2}$. Find the period of oscillation of the body.
explanation:
Suppose static deflection of the body of mass $m$ be $x$, that of spring (1) be $x_{1}$ and spring (2) be $x_{2}$.
$\therefore \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {x_1}{K_1} = {x_3}{K_3} = mg\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
${K_2}{x_2} = 2{K_1}{x_1}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
The given arrangement can be obtained by superimposing the following arrangements. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956976/preptracker/questions/q_39993_explanation_1764956976269.png $K_{3} x_{3}=m g \quad \dfrac{K_{2} x_{2}}{2}=m g \quad k_{1} x_{1}=m g$
Now, $x=x_{3}+2 x_{2}+x_{1}=\dfrac{m g}{K_{3}}+\dfrac{4 m g}{K_{2}}+\dfrac{m g}{K_{1}}$
The given arrangement can be replaced by an equivalent spring-mass system shown in fig -IV in the which static deflection of the mass is $x$.
$\begin{aligned}\therefore \quad m g & =K_{e} x \\\Rightarrow m g & =K_{e}\left(\dfrac{m g}{K_{3}}+\dfrac{4 m g}{K_{2}}+\dfrac{m g}{K_{1}}\right) \\\Rightarrow & \dfrac{1}{K_{e}}=\left(\dfrac{1}{K_{3}}+\dfrac{4}{K_{2}}+\dfrac{1}{K_{1}}\right) \\\therefore \quad T & =2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{K_{e}}}=2 \pi \sqrt{m\left(\dfrac{1}{K_{3}}+\dfrac{4}{K_{2}}+\dfrac{1}{K_{1}}\right)}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion
subtopic: Oscillation of a Body Due to Combination of Springs

question: A mass $m$ is hung on an ideal massless spring. Another equal mass is connected to the other end of the spring. the whole system is at rest. At $t = 0,\,m$ is released and the system falls freely under gravity. Assume that natural length of the spring is $L_{0}$, its initial stretched length is $L$ and the acceleration due to gravity is $g$. What is distance between masses as function of time?
explanation:
In $CM$ frame both the masses execute SHM with
$\omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{\mu}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 k}{m}}$
Initially particles are at extreme Initial extension in the spring is $\left(L-L_{o}\right)$
Distance between them $=L_{0}+\left(L-L_{0}\right) \cos \sqrt{\dfrac{2 k}{m}} t$
subject: Physics

question: The coefficient of friction between block of mass $m$ and $2\,m$ is $\mu=2 \tan \theta$. There is no friction between block of mass $2\,m$ and inclined plane. The maximum amplitude of the two block system for which there is no relative motion between both the blocks is
explanation:
Here $\omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{3 m}}$. The maximum static frictional force is
$f_{\max }=\mu m g \cos \theta=2 \tan \theta m g \cos \theta=2 m g \sin \theta$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951008/preptracker/questions/q_39985_explanation_1764951007835.jpg Applying Newton's second law on the upper block at lower extreme position,
$f-m g \sin \theta=m \omega^{2} A \Rightarrow f=A m \omega^{2}+m g \sin \theta$
As $f \leq f_{\max }, \quad \omega^{2} A \leq g \sin \theta$ or $A \leq \dfrac{3 m g \sin \theta}{k}$
subject: Physics

question: The pulley shown in the figure. has a radius $r$, moment of inertia $I$ about its axis and mass $m$. Find the time period of vertical oscillations of its centre of mass. the spring constant of spring is $K$ and the spring does not slip over the pulley.
explanation:
Let $x_{0}$ be the static deflection of the pulley. It is slightly pulled downward and released at $t=0$. At time $=t$, its displacement from equilibrium position is $x$ and linear acceleration is ' $a$ '. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956964/preptracker/questions/q_39981_explanation_1764956964094.jpg $\therefore \,\,2K{x_0} = mg\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Let $T_{1} \& T_{2}$ are tensions in the strings
$\left( {{T_1} + {T_2}} \right) - mg = ma\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
For rotation, $\left(T_{1}-T_{2}\right) R=I\left(\dfrac{a}{R}\right)$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} \left( {{T_1} - {T_1}} \right) = \frac{{Ia}}{{{R^2}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(3)$
From (2) and (3)
$\begin{gathered}2 T_{1}=m a+\dfrac{I a}{R^{2}}+m g \\2\left(k x_{o}+k x\right)=m a+\dfrac{I a}{R^{2}}+m g \\\Rightarrow a=\dfrac{2 K}{\left(m+\dfrac{I}{R^{2}}\right)} \times x\end{gathered}$

$\therefore \quad T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m+\dfrac{I}{R^{2}}}{2 K}}$
subject: Physics

subtopic: Oscillation of a Body Due to Combination of Springs
parent_topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A uniform stick of mass $M$ and length $L$ is pivoted at its centre. Its ends are tied to two springs each of force constant $K$. In the position shown figure, the strings are in their natural length. When the string is displaced through a small angle $\theta$ and released. The stick:
explanation:
If stick is rotated through a small angle $\theta$, the spring is stretched by a distance $\dfrac{L}{2} \theta$. Therefore restoring force is spring $=K\left(\dfrac{L}{2} \theta\right)$. Each spring causes a torque $\tau(=F d)=\left(\dfrac{K L \theta}{2}\right) \dfrac{L}{2}$ in the same direction.
Therefore equation of rotational motion is
$\begin{aligned}& \tau=I \alpha \\& -2\left(\dfrac{K L \theta}{2}\right) \dfrac{L}{2}=I \alpha \text { with } I=\dfrac{M L^{2}}{12}\end{aligned}$
$\alpha=-\left(\dfrac{6 K}{M}\right) \theta$
Standard equation of angular S.H.M. is
$\begin{aligned}& \alpha=-\omega^{2} \theta \\& \omega^{2}=\dfrac{6 K}{M}\end{aligned}$
frequency $f=\dfrac{\omega}{2 \pi}=\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{6 K}{M}}$

options.text: Executes periodic motion which is not simple harmonic

question: A smooth ring of mass $m_{1}$ is lying on a rigid horizontal string. A bob of mass $m_{2}$ hangs from the ring by an inextensible light string of length $l$. Find the angular frequency of oscillation of the system.
explanation:
Both will oscillate about their centre of mass. the oscillation of $\mathrm{M}$ is same as a simple pendulum of mass $\mathrm{M}$ and length $l_{1}$. The time period of both particles are same. Let it be $T$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951002/preptracker/questions/q_39974_explanation_1764951001716.jpg $T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{{{l_1}}}{g}} $
where $l_{1}$ can be given as $M l_{1}=m l_{2}$ and $l_{1}+l_{2}=l$
$l_{1}=\dfrac{m l}{M+m}$
Then, $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m l}{(M+m) g}}$

question: Two light strings, each of length $l$, are fixed at points $A$ and $B$ on a fixed horizontal rod $xy$. If the bob is slightly displaced normal to the plane of the strings and released then period of the resulting small oscillation will be:
explanation:
The effective length of the bob is $\dfrac{l}{\sqrt{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{\sqrt{2} g}{l}} \Rightarrow T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{\sqrt{2} g}}$

question: A simple pendulum of length $L$ is suspended from the top of a flat beam of thickness $L / 2$. The bob is pulled away from the beam so that it makes an angle $\theta=30^{\circ}$ with the vertical, as shown in the figure. It is then released from rest If $\phi$ is the maximum angular deflection to the right, then
explanation:
Take the lowest point of the pendulum as the reference line to measure $P.E$
$P . E_{i}=P . E_{f}$ (when the bob is at extreme positions)
$\begin{gathered}m g(L-L \cos \theta)=m g\left(\dfrac{L}{2}-\dfrac{L}{2} \cos \phi\right) \\1-\cos \theta=\dfrac{1}{2}-\dfrac{\cos \phi}{2}\end{gathered}$
$\dfrac{\cos \phi}{2}=\cos \theta-\dfrac{1}{2}$
As $\theta=30^{\circ} \quad \cos \theta=\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$
$\begin{aligned}& \cos \phi=\sqrt{3}-1 \\& \phi=\cos ^{-1}(0.732) \approx 42^{0} \\& \theta < \phi < 2 \theta\end{aligned}$

question: Two identical springs of spring constant $k$ each are connected in series and parallel as shown in figure. A mass $M$ is suspended from them. The ratio of their frequencies of vertical oscillation will be
explanation:
${k_s} = \frac{{k \times k}}{{k + k}} = \frac{k}{2}$
${k_p} = k + k = 2k$
$\therefore \frac{{{n_s}}}{{{n_p}}} = \frac{{\frac{1}{{2\pi }}\sqrt {\frac{{{k_s}}}{M}} }}{{\frac{1}{{2\pi }}\sqrt {\frac{{{k_p}}}{M}} }}$
$ = \sqrt {\frac{{{k_s}}}{{{k_p}}}} $
$ = \sqrt {\frac{{k/2}}{{2k}}} = \frac{1}{2}$

question: Three masses $0.1\,kg,0.3\,kg$ and $0.4\,kg$ are suspended at end of a spring. When the $0.4\,kg$ mass is removed, the system oscillates with a period $2\,s$. When the $0.3\,kg$ mass is also removed, the system will oscillate with a period
explanation:

Mass has reduced to $\dfrac{1}{4}$ th the initial mass.
Hence time period will remain half i.e., $\frac{2}{2} = 1$

question: A tray of mass $12\,kg$ is supported by two identical springs as shown in figure. When the tray is pressed down slightly and then released, it executes SHM with a time period of $1.5\,s$. The spring constant of each spring is
explanation:
Given $m = 12\,kg,\,T = 1.5\,s$
Since, both the springs are connected in parallel then
Spring constant, $K = {k_1} + {k_2}$
Time period, $T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{m}{K}} = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{{12}}{{{k_1} + {k_2}}}} $
$ = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{{12}}{{2k}}} \left( {\because {k_1} = {k_2} = k} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow 1.5 = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{6}{k}} {\rm{ }}or{\rm{ }}k = 105.16\,N{m^{ - 1}}$

question: A $10\,kg$ metal block is attached to a spring of spring constant $1000\,N\,{m^{ - 1}}$. A block is displaced from equilibrium position by $10\,cm$ and released. The maximum acceleration of the block is
explanation:
Given that
$A = 10\,cm = 0.1\,m$
$k = 1000N{m^{ - 1}}$
$m = 10\,kg$
Maximum acceleration of the block is
${a_{\max }} = {\omega ^2}\,A = \frac{{kA}}{m}\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} \left( {\because \omega = \sqrt {\frac{k}{m}} } \right)$
$ = \frac{{1000N{m^{ - 1}} \times 0.1\,m}}{{10\,kg}} = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$

question: A man measures the period of a simple pendulum inside a stationary lift and finds it to be $T$ second. If the accelerates upwards with an accelerating $\frac{g}{4}$, then the period of the pendulum will be
explanation:
In stationary lift $T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{l}{{\,g}}} $
In upward moving lift
${T^\prime } = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{l}{{(\,g + a)}}} $
$({\rm{a}}\,\,{\rm{ = }}\,\,{\rm{acceleration of lift}})$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{T^\prime }}}{T}\sqrt {\frac{g}{{(g + a)}}} $
$\sqrt {\frac{g}{{\left( {g + \frac{g}{4}} \right)}}} = \sqrt {\frac{4}{5}} $
$ \Rightarrow T' = \frac{{2T}}{{\sqrt 5 }}$
subject: Physics

question: A person measures a time period of a simple pendulum inside a stationary lift and finds it to be $T$. If the lift starts accelerating upwards with an acceleration $\left(\dfrac{g}{3}\right)$, the time period of the pendulum will be
explanation:
Time period of simple pendulum inside a stationary lift,
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}}$
where $l=$ length of string and $g = $ gravitational acceleration Acceleration of lift in upward direction,
$a=\dfrac{g}{3}$
Therefore, time period of pendulum
$\begin{aligned}& T^{\prime}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g+a}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g+\dfrac{g}{3}}} \\& =2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{3 l}{4 g}}=2 \pi \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}} \\& =\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} 2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}}=\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} T \quad \text { [from eq.(1)] }\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: The path length of oscillation of simple pendulum of length $1\,m$ is $16\,cm$. Its maximum velocity is ( take, $g = {\pi ^2}m/{s^2}$)
explanation:
Given,
Length of the pendulum $1\,m$
amplitude $(a) = \frac{{{\rm{path}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{length}}}}{2} = \frac{{16}}{2} = 8\,cm$
Acceleration due to gravity $(g) = {\pi ^2}m/{s^2}$
we know that, time period
$(T) = $ $2\pi \sqrt {\frac{l}{g}} = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{1}{{{\pi ^2}}}} $
$T = \frac{{2\pi }}{\pi }$
$T = 2\,s$
Therefore, Maximum velocity $\left(v_{\max }\right)=a \omega$
$ = a \times \frac{{2\pi }}{T}\left( {\because \omega = \frac{{2\pi }}{T}} \right)$
$ = \frac{{8 \times 2 \times \pi }}{2} = 8\pi cm/s$
subject: Physics

question: The bob of a simple pendulum performs SHM with period $T$ in air and with period $T_{1}$ in water. Relation between $T$ and $T_{1}$ is ( neglect friction due to water, density of the material of the bob is $ = \frac{9}{8} \times {10^3}kg{m^3}$, density of water $ = 1\,gc{c^{ - 1}}$)
explanation:
Time period of simple pendulum in water is given by
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g_{\text {eff }}}}$
[ symbols have their usual meaningd and $g_{\text {eff }}=$ acceleration due to gravity in water]
As we know, $g_{\text {eff }}=g\left(\dfrac{\sigma-\rho}{\rho}\right)$
[ where, $\sigma=$ density of bob, $\rho=$ density of water] $=9.8\left(\dfrac{\dfrac{9}{8} \times 10^{3}-10^{3}}{\dfrac{9}{8} \times 10^{3}}\right)$
$=9.8\left(\dfrac{\dfrac{9}{8}-1}{\dfrac{9}{8}}\right)=9.8\left(\dfrac{1}{8} \times \dfrac{8}{9}\right)$
$=\dfrac{9.8}{9}$
So, $T_{1}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l \times 9}{9.8}} \Rightarrow T_{1}=3 T$
[ therefore, time period of simple pendulum in air, $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{9.8}}$ ]
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion
parent_topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A simple pendulum is oscillating with amplitude A and angular frequency $\omega$. At displacement $x$ from mean position, the ratio of kinetic energy to potential energy is
explanation:
Kinetic energy of pendulum oscillating with amplitude $A$ and angular frequency $\omega$ at displacement $x$ from mean position is
$K E=\dfrac{1}{2} k\left(A^{2}-x^{2}\right)$
Potential energy of pendulum at displacement $x$, from mean position is
$\begin{aligned}P E & =\dfrac{1}{2} k x^{2} \\\therefore \dfrac{K E}{P E} & =\dfrac{\dfrac{1}{2} k\left(A^{2}-x^{2}\right)}{\dfrac{1}{2} k x^{2}}=\dfrac{A^{2}-x^{2}}{x^{2}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: Two simple pendulums $A$ and $B$ are made to oscillate simultaneously and it is found that $A$ completes 10 oscillations in $20\,s$ and $B$ completes 8 oscillations in $10\,s$. The ratio of the lengths of $A$ and $B$ is
explanation:
Frequency of oscillations made by a simple pendulum of length $l$ is
$f=\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{l}} \Rightarrow f \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{l}}$
Since, $A$ completes 10 oscillations in $20\,s$
$ \Rightarrow {f_A} = \frac{{10}}{{20}} = 0.5\,Hz$
Similarly, ${f_B} = \frac{8}{{10}} = 0.8\,Hz$
$\therefore \dfrac{f_{A}}{f_{B}}=\dfrac{5}{8}=\sqrt{\dfrac{l_{B}}{l_{A}}}$ or $\quad \dfrac{l_{A}}{l_{B}}=\dfrac{64}{25}$

question: Two bodies $A$ and $B$ of equal mass are suspended from two separate massles springs of force constant $k_{1}$ and $k_{2}$ respectively. The bodies oscillate vertically such that their maximum velocities are equal. The ratio of the amplitudes of body $A$ to that of body $B$ is
explanation:
The maximum velocity of body in SHM
$v=A \omega$
Where, A is amplitude of body and
$\omega=$ angular frequency
It is given that, the maximum velocities of bodies are equal
$\begin{aligned}v_{A} & =v_{B} \\A_{A} \omega_{A} & =A_{B} \omega_{B} \\A_{A} \sqrt{\dfrac{k_{1}}{m}} & =A_{B} \sqrt{\dfrac{k_{2}}{m}}\left(\because \omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}\right) \\\dfrac{A_{A}}{A_{B}} & =\sqrt{\dfrac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}}\end{aligned}$

question: A block of mass $m$ attached to one end of the vertical spring produces extension $x$. If the block is pulled and released, the periodic time of oscillation is
explanation:
For vertical spring, the periodic time of oscillation is
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{k}}$
As, restoring force, $|F|=k x$
$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow k=\dfrac{F}{x}=\dfrac{m g}{x} \\& \therefore T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{\dfrac{m}{m g}}{x}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{x}{g}}\end{aligned}$

question: A mass $m_{1}$ connected to a horizontal spring perform SHM with amplitude $A$. While mass $m_{1}$ is passing through mean position, another mass $m_{2}$ is placed on it so that both the masses move together with amplitude $A_{1}$. The ratio of $\dfrac{A_{1}}{A}$ is $\left(m_{2} < m_{1}\right)$
explanation:
Potential energy of oscillating mass is given by
$P E=\dfrac{1}{2} k x^{2}$
When only one block is oscillating at $x = A$
$E_{\max }=\dfrac{1}{2} k A^{2}$ and at mean position $x = 0$. So,
$E = 0$
$\therefore A \propto \frac{1}{{\sqrt m }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
When mass $m_{2}$ is placed on top of the mass $m$. Then, total mass is $\left(m_{1}+m_{2}\right)$ and $E = 0$ at this point, as $x = 0$
When they reaches at $x = A$,
then ${A_1} \propto \frac{1}{{\sqrt {{m_1} + {m_2}} }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Dividing eq.(2) by (1)
$\dfrac{A_{1}}{A}=\left(\dfrac{m_{1}}{m_{1}+m_{2}}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}$
subject: Physics

question: A clock pendulum having coefficient of linear expansion, $\alpha=9 \times 10^{7} /{ }^{\circ} C^{-1}$ has a period of 0.5 $s$ at ${20^{\rm{o}}}C$. If the clock is used in a climate, where the temperature is ${30^{\rm{o}}}C$, how much time does the clock lose in each oscillation ? $g = $ constant)
explanation:
Given, the coefficient of linear expansion
$\alpha=9 \times 10^{-7 o} C^{-1}$
Initial time period ${T_0} = 0.5\,s$,
initial temperature ${T_i} = {\rm{ }}{20^{\rm{o}}}C$
final temperature ${T_i} = {\rm{ }}{30^{\rm{o}}}C$
Expansion in length $\Delta l=l \alpha\left(T_{f}-T_{i}\right)$
$\Delta l=l \times 9 \times 10^{-7}(30-20)$
Now, the time period of pendulum
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}}$
Error in time period
$\dfrac{\Delta t}{T}=\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{\Delta l}{l}+\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{\Delta g}{g}$
Since $\Delta g=0$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\Delta T}{T}=\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{\Delta l}{l}$
Now, substituting values in the above equation, we get,
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{\Delta T}}{{0.5}} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {\frac{{l \times 9 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}}{l}(30 - 20)} \right]$
$ \Rightarrow \Delta T = \frac{{0.5 \times 9 \times {{10}^{ - 7}} \times 10}}{2}$
$ \Rightarrow \Delta T = 2.25 \times {10^{ - 6}}\,s$
subject: Physics

parent_topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: Two pendulum begin to swing simultaneously. The first pendulum makes nine full oscillations when the other makes seven. The ratio of the lengths of the two pendulums is
explanation:
As two pendulums being to swing simultaneously, then
${n_1}{T_1} = {n_2}{T_2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Where $n_{1}$ and $n_{2}$ are the number of oscillations of first and second pendulums respectively and $T_{1}$ and $T_{2}$ be their respective time periods
The time period of simple pendulum is given by
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}}$
where $l = $ length of pendulum
and $g = $ acceleration due to gravity
$ \Rightarrow {T^2} \propto l\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
So, from eq.(1) and (2), we get
$\dfrac{l_{1}}{l_{2}}=\dfrac{T_{1}^{2}}{T_{2}^{2}}=\dfrac{n_{2}^{2}}{n_{1}^{2}}$
Here $n_{1}=9, n_{2}=7$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{l_{1}}{l_{2}}=\dfrac{(7)^{2}}{(9)^{2}}=\dfrac{49}{81}$
Hence the ratio of pendulums length
$l_{1}: l_{2}=49: 81$
subject: Physics

question: A bob of a simple pendulum has mass $m$ and is oscillating with an amplitude $a$. If the length of the pendulum is $L$, then the maximum tension in the string is [$\cos \,{0^{\rm{o}}} = 1,\,g$ acceleration due to gravity]
explanation:
Consider the figure shown below https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956100/preptracker/questions/q_39750_explanation_1764956099891.jpg The string posses maximum tension when bob is at mean position of oscillation i.e., at position $R$
From geometry, $O P=\sqrt{L^{2}-a^{2}}$
Also, $RP = OR - OP$
$=L-\sqrt{L^{2}-a^{2}}$
The whole kinetic energy of bob at position $R$ is converted into its potential energy at position $B$
$\begin{gathered}\therefore \dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}=m g\left(L-\sqrt{L^{2}-a^{2}}\right) \\v^{2}=2 g\left(L-\sqrt{L^{2}-a^{2}}\right)\end{gathered}$
Balancing forces at $R$,
$\begin{aligned}& T-m g=\dfrac{m v^{2}}{l}=\dfrac{2 m g\left(L-\sqrt{L^{2}-a^{2}}\right)}{L} \\& \therefore T=m g+2 m g\left(1-\sqrt{1-\dfrac{a^{2}}{L^{2}}}\right)\end{aligned}$
Using approximation,
$\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} = 1 - \frac{{{x^2}}}{2}{\rm{ for }}x < < 1,$
we get
$\begin{aligned}T & =m g+2 m g\left[1-\left(1-\dfrac{a^{2}}{2 L^{2}}\right)\right] \\& =m g+m g\left(\dfrac{a}{L}\right)^{2}=m g\left[1+\left(\dfrac{a}{L}\right)^{2}\right]\end{aligned}$

question:
Assertion: The bob of simple pendulum is a ball full of water. If a fine hole is made at the bottom of the ball, then the time period will no more remain constant.


Reason: The time period of simple pendulum does not depend upon mass.
explanation: When water flows out of the ball the centre of mass comes down from the centre of the ball. So, the effective length of the ball increase, hence the time period of pendulum also increase. After the water is completely drained, centre of mass of ball is at its geometric centre. So, effective length of pendulum decreases and the time period of pendulum also decreases hence, time period does not remain constant. Time period of a simple pendulum does not depend upon the mass of bob as $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}}$. So option (2) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: For an oscillating simple pendulum, the tension in the string is maximum at the mean position and minimum at the extreme position.


Reason: The velocity of oscillating bob in simple harmonic motion is maximum at the mean position.
explanation:
In simple pendulum, when bob is in deflection position, the tension in the string is
$T=m g \cos \theta+\dfrac{m v^{2}}{l}$. Since the value of $\theta$ is
different at different positions, hence tension in the string is not constant throughout the oscillation. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956094/preptracker/questions/q_39624_explanation_1764956094547.jpg At end points $\theta$ is maximum; the value of $\cos \theta$ is least, hence the value of tension in the string is least. At the mean position, the value of $\theta=0^{\circ}$ and $\cos 0^{\circ}=1$, so the value of tension is largest.
Also velocity is given by $v=\omega \sqrt{a^{2}-x^{2}}$ which is maximum when $x=0$, at mean position.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question: A tunnel is made across the earth of radius $R$, passing through its centre. A ball is dropped from a height h in the tunnel. the motion will be periodic with time period.
explanation:
When the ball $m$ falls from a height $h$, it reaches the surface of earth in time, $t=\sqrt{2 h / g}$. Its velocity is $v=\sqrt{2 g h}$. It then moves into the tunnel and reaches on the other side of earth and goes again upto a height $h$ on the other side of earth. The ball again returns back and thus executes periodic motion. Outside the earth ball crosses distance $h$ four times. When the ball is in the tunnel at distance $x$ from the centre of the earth, then gravitational force acting on ball is
$\begin{aligned}& \therefore \quad F=\dfrac{G M m x}{R^{3}} \\& \text { i.e., } F \propto x\end{aligned}$
As, this force, $F$ is directed towards the centre of earth i.e., the mean position. So, the ball will execute periodic motion about the centre of earth.
Here, inertia factor $=$ mass of ball $=m$
$\text { Spring factor }=\dfrac{G M m}{R^{3}}=\dfrac{g m}{R}$
$\therefore$ Time period of oscillation of ball in the tunnel is
$\begin{aligned}T^{\prime} & =2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{\text { inertia factor }}{\text { spring factor }}} \\& =2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{g m / R}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{R}{g}}\end{aligned}$
Time spent by ball outside the tunnel on both the sides will be $=4 \sqrt{2 h / g}$
Therefore, total time period of oscillation of ball is
$=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{R}{g}}+4 \sqrt{\dfrac{2 h}{g}}$
subject: Physics
topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion
parent_topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: A wooden cube (density of wood '$d$') of side ' $l$ ' floats in a liquid of density '$\rho$' with its upper and lower surface horizontal. If the cube is pushed slightly down and relased, it performs simple harmonic motion of period '$T$' to
explanation:
At equilibrium $F_{b}=m g$
$\rho A l_{0} g=d l g$
Restoring force,
$\begin{aligned}& F_{r}=m g-F_{b}^{\prime} \\& F=m g-\rho A\left(l_{0}+x\right) g \\& d A l a=d A l g-\rho A l_{0} g-\rho g A x \\& a=-\dfrac{\rho g}{d l} x\end{aligned}$
Therefore, wooden cube performs S.H.M.
$\therefore \omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho g}{d l}} \Rightarrow T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l d}{\rho g}}$
subject: Physics

parent_topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: Figure shows a system consisting of massless pulley, a spring of force constant $k$ and a block of mass $m$. If the block is slightly displaced vertically down from its equilibrium position and then released, the period of its vertical oscillation is
explanation:
Let us assume that in equilibrium condition spring is $x_{0}$ elongate from its natural length https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949681/preptracker/questions/q_39479_explanation_1764949680914.jpg In equilibrium $T_{0}=m g$
and $k x_{0}=2 T_{0}$
$ \Rightarrow k{x_0} = 2mg\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
If the mass $m$ moves down a distance $\mathrm{x}$ from its equilibrium position then pulley will move down by $\dfrac{x}{2}$. So the extra force in spring will be $\dfrac{k x}{2}$.
From figure
$\begin{aligned}& F_{\text {net }}=m g-T=m g-\dfrac{k}{2}\left(x_{0}+\dfrac{x}{2}\right) \\& \left(T=\dfrac{k\left(x_{o}+\dfrac{x}{2}\right)}{2}\right) \\& F_{\text {net }}=m g-\dfrac{k x_{0}}{2}-\dfrac{k x}{4}\end{aligned}$
from eq. (1), we get
${F_{{\rm{net }}}} = \frac{{ - kx}}{4}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Comparing eq. (2) with $F=-K_{\text {Sнм }} x$
then $\quad K_{\text {SHM }}=\dfrac{K}{4}$
$\Rightarrow T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{K_{\mathrm{SHM}}}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{4 m}{K}}$
subject: Physics

parent_topic: Some Systems Executing Simple Harmonic Motion

question: Two identical particles each of mass $m$ are interconnected by a light spring of stiffness $k$, the time period for small oscillation is equal to:
explanation:
The reduced mass
$\mu=\dfrac{(m)(m)}{m+m}=\dfrac{m}{2}$
The given system is equivalent to a system of a particle of mass $m/2$ connected to a spring of stiffness $k$ ragidly.
The required period of oscillation.
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu}{k}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m / 2}{k}}=\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{2 m}{k}}$

question: The masses $m_{1}$ and $m_{2}$ are suspended together by a massless spring of constant $k$. When the masses are in equilibrium, $m_{1}$ is removed without disturbing the system; the amplitude of vibration is -
explanation:
In equilibrium $F=\left(m_{1}+m_{2}\right) g$
By removing mass $m_{1}$
$\begin{aligned}& F^{\prime}=m_{2} g \\& F_{r}=F^{\prime}-F=m_{2} g-\left(m_{1}+m_{2}\right) g \\& k A=m_{1} g \Rightarrow A=\dfrac{m_{1} g}{k}\end{aligned}$
since the lower spring exerts force on the block only in the downward motion.

question:
A mass $M$, attached to a horizontal spring, executes S.H.M. with amplitude $A_{1}$. When the mass $M$ passes through its mean position then a smaller mass $m$ is placed over it and both of them move together with amplitude $A_{2}$. The ratio
of $\left(\dfrac{A_{1}}{A_{2}}\right)$ is:
explanation:
The net force exerted by the spring is zero at the mean point. Therefore, linear momentum must be conserved. $\therefore M v_{1}=(M+m) v_{2}$
$\begin{aligned}\text { As } \quad & =A \omega=A \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}} \\\Rightarrow & M A_{1} \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{M}}=(M+m) A_{2} \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m+M}} \\& \therefore\left(V=A \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{M}}\right) \\& \because A_{1} \sqrt{M}=A_{2} \sqrt{M+m} \\& \therefore \dfrac{A_{1}}{A_{2}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{m+M}{M}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A block of mass $m$ rests on a platform. The platform is given up and down SHM with an amplitude $\mathrm{d}$. What can be the maximum frequency so that the block never leaves the platform?
explanation:
For the gievn amplitude $d$ the block looses contact with the platform when maximum acceleration is equal to $g$ at the highest position.
$\begin{aligned}& k d=m g \Rightarrow m \omega^{2} d=m g \\& \Rightarrow \omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{d}} \Rightarrow n=\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{d}}\end{aligned}$

question: Two springs, of force constants $k_{1}$ and $k_{2}$ are connected to a mass $m$ as shown. The frequency of oscillation of the mass is $f$. If both $k_{1}$ and $k_{2}$ are made four times their original values, the frequency of oscillation becomes.
explanation:
The two springs are in parallel.
$\therefore$ Effective spring constant,
$k=k_{1}+k_{2}$
Now, frequency of oscillation is given by
$f = \frac{1}{{2\pi }}\sqrt {\frac{{{k_1} + {k_2}}}{m}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
When both $k_{1}$ and $k_{2}$ are made four times their original values, the new frequency is given by
$\begin{aligned}& f^{\prime}=\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{4 k_{1}+4 k_{2}}{m}} \\& =\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{4\left(k_{1}+k_{2}\right)}{m}}=2\left(\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{k_{1}+k_{2}}{m}}\right)=2 f\end{aligned}$

question: A simple pendulum of length $l$ and having a bob of mass $M$ is suspended in a car. The car is moving on a circular track of radius $R$ with a uniform speed $v$. If the pendulum makes small oscillations about its equilibrium position, what will be its time period?
explanation:
The bob is subjected to two simultaneous, accelerations perpendicular to each other viz acceleration due to gravity $g$ and radial acceleration $a_{R}=\dfrac{v^{2}}{R}$ towards the centre of the circular path. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956084/preptracker/questions/q_39455_explanation_1764956083776.jpg $\therefore$ Effective acceleration $a_{e f f}=\sqrt{g^{2}+\left(\dfrac{v^{2}}{R}\right)^{2}}$
$\therefore$ Time period of the simple pendulum
$ = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{l}{{{a_{{\text{eff }}}}}}} $
$ = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{l}{{\sqrt {{g^2} + {{\left( {\frac{{{v^2}}}{R}} \right)}^2}} }}} = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{l}{{\sqrt {{g^2} + \frac{{{v^4}}}{{{R^2}}}} }}} $
subject: Physics

question: If $T_{1}$ and $T_{2}$ are the time-periods of oscillation of a simple pendulum on the surface of earth (of radius $R$) and at a depth $d$, then $d$ is equal to
explanation: $\begin{aligned}& T_{1}=\dfrac{k}{\sqrt{g}} \text { and } T_{2}=\dfrac{k}{\sqrt{g\left(1-\dfrac{d}{R}\right)}} \\& \dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}=\sqrt{1-\dfrac{d}{R}} \Rightarrow\left(\dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}\right)^{2}=1-\dfrac{d}{R} \\& d=\left[1-\left(\dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}\right)^{2}\right] R\end{aligned}$

question: The period of a simple pendulum, whose bob is a hollow metallic sphere, is $T_{1}$. The period is $T_{2}$ when the bob is filled with sand, $T_{3}$ when it is filled with mercury and $T_{4}$ when it is half filled with mercury. Which of the following is true?
explanation: Time period of pendulum doesn't depends upon mass but it depends upon length (distance between point of suspension and centre of mass). In first three cases length are same so $T_{1}=T_{2}=T_{3}$ but in last case centre of mass lowers which in turn increases the length. So option (3) is correct

question: Consider a liquid which fills a uniform U-tube, as shown in fig up to a height $h$. Find angular frequency of small oscillations of the liquid in the U-tube.
explanation:
Suppose that the liquid is displaced slightly from equilibrium so that its level rises in one arm of the tube, while it is depressed in the second arm by the same amount, $x$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956079/preptracker/questions/q_39398_explanation_1764956078904.jpg If the density of the liquid is $\rho$, then the total mechanical energy of the liquid column is
The difference in the two levels is $2 x$. So the column having length $2 x$ produces restoring force on the entire liquid.
$\begin{aligned}& \rho A 2 x g=\rho A 2 h a \\& a=-\left(\dfrac{g}{h}\right) x \Rightarrow \omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{h}}\end{aligned}$

question: A particle of mass $m$ is attached to three identical springs $A,B$ and $C$ each of force constant $k$ as shown in figure. If the particle of mass $m$ is pushed slightly against the spring A and released then the time period of oscillations is (The particle is on a frictionless horizontal surface).
explanation:
When the particle of mass $m$ at $O$ is pushed by y in the direction of $A$ the spring $A$ will be compressed by y while spring $B$ and $C$ will be stretched by $y^{\prime}=y \cos 45^{\circ}$. So that the total restoring force on the mass $m$ along $O A$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957939/preptracker/questions/q_39395_explanation_1764957939176.jpg $F_{\text {net }}=F_{A}+F_{B} \cos 45^{\circ}+F_{C} \cos 45^{\circ}$
$=k y+2 k y^{\prime} \cos 45^{\circ}=k y+2 k\left(y \cos 45^{\circ}\right) \cos 45^{\circ}=2 k y$
Also $F_{\text {net }}=k^{\prime} y \Rightarrow k^{\prime} y=2 k y \Rightarrow k^{\prime}=2 k$
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{k^{\prime}}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{2 k}}$
subject: Physics

question: A block of mass $M$ is suspended from a light spring of force constant $k$. Another mass $m$ moving upwards with velocity $v$ shifts the mass $M$ and gets embeded in it. What will be the amplitude of the combined mass?
explanation:
If $v^{\prime}$ are the velocities of the block of mass $M$ and $(M+m)$ while passing from the mean position when executing SHM.
Using law of conservation of linear momentum, we have
$\begin{aligned}m v & =(M+m) v^{\prime} \\\text { or } \quad v^{\prime} & =m v /(M+m)\end{aligned}$
Also, maximum $PE = $ maximum $KE$
$\begin{gathered}\therefore \quad \dfrac{1}{2} k A^{\prime 2}=\dfrac{1}{2}(M+m) v^{\prime 2} \\A^{\prime}=\left(\dfrac{M+m}{k}\right)^{1 / 2} \times \dfrac{m v}{(M+m)}=\dfrac{m v}{\sqrt{(M+m) k}}\end{gathered}$

question: A mass $M$ is attached to a horizontal spring of force constant $k$ fixed on one side to a rigid support as shown figure. The mass oscillates on a frictionless surface with time period $T$ and amplitude A. When the mass is in equilibrium position, another mass $m$ is gently placed on it and both stick together. What will be the new amplitude of oscillation?
explanation:
When a mass $m$ is placed on mass $M$, the new system is of mass $=(M+m)$, attached to the spring. New time period of oscillation,
$\begin{aligned}& T^{\prime}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{M+m}{k}} \\& T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{k}}\end{aligned}$
Let $v=$ velocity of mass $\mathrm{M}$ while passing through the mean position.
$v^{\prime}=$ velocity of mass $(M+m)$, while passing through the mean position.
According to law of conservation of linear momentum
$M v=(M+m) v^{\prime}$
At mean position, $v=A \omega=$ and $v^{\prime}=A^{\prime} \omega^{\prime}$
$\therefore M A \omega=(M+m) A^{\prime} \omega^{\prime}$

$\text { or } \begin{aligned}A^{\prime} & =\left(\dfrac{M}{M+m}\right) \dfrac{\omega}{\omega^{\prime}} A=\dfrac{M}{M+m} \times \dfrac{T^{\prime}}{T} \times A \\& =\left(\dfrac{M}{M+m}\right) \times \sqrt{\dfrac{M+m}{M}} \times A=A \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{M+m}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A spring of mass $m$ is connected to a rigid support as shown in the figure. If the free end of the spring is given velocity $v_{o}$ then kinetic energy of the spring is
explanation:
The velocity of the spring increases linearly from left to right. The velocity of the spring at a distance $x$ from the left end is
$v=\dfrac{v_{o}}{l} x$
consider a small element of length $dx$ as shown in the figure. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957797/preptracker/questions/q_39386_explanation_1764957797006.jpg The kinetic energy of the element is
$\begin{aligned}& d x=\dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{m}{l}\right) d x v^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{m}{l} \dfrac{v_{o}^{2}}{l^{2}} x^{2} d x \\& k=\dfrac{m v_{o}^{2}}{2 l^{3}} \int_{x^{2}}^{l} x^{2} d x=\dfrac{m v_{o}^{2}}{2 l^{3}}\left[\dfrac{l^{3}}{3}\right]=\dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{m}{3}\right) v_{o}^{2}\end{aligned}$

question: The period of oscillation of a simple pendulum of length $l$ suspended from the roof of the vehicle which moves down without friction on an inclined plane of inclination $\alpha$, is given by:
explanation:

question: A pendulum made of a uniform wire of cross sectional area $A$ has time period $T$. When an additional mass $M$ is added to its bob, the time period changes to ${T_M}$. If the Young's modulus of the material of the wire is $Y$ then $\dfrac{1}{Y}$ is equal to: $(g = $ gravitational acceleration$)$
explanation:
Time period, $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}}$
when additional mass $M$ is added to its bob
$\begin{aligned}T_{M} & =2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l+\Delta l}{g}} \\\Delta l & =\dfrac{M g l}{A Y} \\\Rightarrow T_{M} & =2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l+\dfrac{M g l}{A Y}}{g}} \\\left(\dfrac{T_{M}}{T}\right)^{2} & =1+\dfrac{M g}{A Y} \\\dfrac{1}{Y} & =\dfrac{A}{M g}\left[\left(\dfrac{T_{M}}{T}\right)^{2}-1\right]\end{aligned}$

question: The bob of a simple pendulum is displaced from its equilibrium position $O$ to a position $Q$ which is at height $h$ above $O$ and the bob is then released. Assuming the mass of the bob to be $m$ and time period of oscillations to be $2.0\,\sec $, the tension in the string when the bob passes through $O$ is
explanation:
Tension in the string when bob passes through lowest point
$T=m g+\dfrac{m v^{2}}{r}=m g+m v \omega \quad(\because v=r \omega)$
Putting $v=\sqrt{2 g h}$ and $\omega=\dfrac{2 \pi}{T}=\dfrac{2 \pi}{2}=\pi$ we get $T=m(g+\pi \sqrt{2 g h})$

question: A pendulum has time period $T$ in air. When it is made to oscillate in water, it acquired a time period $T^{\prime}=\sqrt{2} T$. The density of the pendulum bob is equal to (density of water $=1$ )
explanation:
The effective acceleration of a bob in water
$=g^{\prime}=g\left(1-\dfrac{\sigma}{\rho}\right)$ where $\sigma$ and $\rho$ are the
density of water and the bob respectively. Since the period of oscillation of the bob in air and water are given as
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}} \text { and } T^{\prime}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g^{\prime}}}$
$\therefore \dfrac{T}{T^{\prime}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{g^{\prime}}{g}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{g(1-\sigma / \rho)}{g}}=\sqrt{1-\dfrac{\sigma}{\rho}}=\sqrt{1-\dfrac{1}{\rho}}$
Putting $\dfrac{T}{T^{\prime}}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$. We obtain, $\dfrac{1}{2}=1-\dfrac{1}{\rho} \Rightarrow \rho=2$

question: The period of a simple pendulum suspended from the ceiling of a car is $T$ when the car is at rest. If the car moves with a constant acceleration the period of the pendulum
explanation:
$\begin{aligned}& T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g^{\prime}}} \\& g^{\prime}=\sqrt{g^{2}+a^{2}}\end{aligned}$
Where $a$ is the acceleration of the car
As $g^{\prime}>g \Rightarrow T$ decreases

question: Match the following:
explanation:
In figure $A$, three springs are connected in parallel. The effective spring constant is,
$k_{\text {eff }}=k_{1}+k_{2}+k_{3} \text {. }$
$\therefore T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{k_{1}+k_{2}+k_{3}}}$
In figure $B$, three identical springs are connected in parallel. The effective spring constant is
$k_{\mathrm{eff}}=k+k+k=3 k \therefore T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{3 k}}$
In figure $C$, two springs are connected in series. The effective spring constant is
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{1}{k_{e f f}}=\dfrac{1}{k_{1}}+\dfrac{1}{k_{2}}=\dfrac{k_{2}+k_{1}}{k_{1} k_{2}} \Rightarrow k_{e f f}=\dfrac{k_{1} k_{2}}{k_{1}+k_{2}} \\& \therefore T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m\left(k_{1}+k_{2}\right)}{k_{1} k_{2}}}\end{aligned}$
In figure $D$, two identical springs are connected in series. The effective spring constant is
$\begin{aligned}& k_{\text {eff }}=\dfrac{(k)(k)}{k+k}=\dfrac{k}{2} \\& \therefore T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{2 m}{k}}\end{aligned}$
So option (2) is correct.

options.text: A - P, B - Q, C - S, D - R
A - S, B - R, C - P, D - Q
A - R, B - P,  C - S, D - Q
A - P, B - R, C - Q, D - S

question: A block of mass $M$ is suspended from a light spring of force constant $k$. Another mass $m$ moving downwards hits the mass $M$ and gets embeded in it. What will be the shift in mean position of the system.
explanation:
The initial shift in mean position of the block of mass $\mathrm{M}$ is $x_{1}=\dfrac{M g}{K}$ (from natural length)
The new mean poistion of the combined mass
$\text { is } \quad \begin{gathered}x_{2}=\dfrac{M g+m g}{K} \\\text { shift }=\dfrac{m g}{K}\end{gathered}$

question: A mass $m$ is suspended separately by two different springs in successive order, then time periods is $t_{1}$ and $t_{2}$ respectively. If $m$ is connected by both springs as shown in figure, then time period is $t_{0}$, the correct relation is
explanation:

$k_{1}=\dfrac{4 \pi^{2} m}{t_{1}^{2}}$
Similarly, $k_{2}=\dfrac{4 \pi^{2} m}{t_{2}^{2}}$
$\begin{aligned}& \text { and } \quad\left(k_{1}+k_{2}\right)=\dfrac{4 \pi^{2} m}{t_{0}^{2}} \\& \therefore \quad \dfrac{4 \pi^{2} m}{t_{0}^{2}}=\dfrac{4 \pi^{2} m}{t_{1}^{2}}+\dfrac{4 \pi^{2} m}{t_{2}^{2}} \\& \text { or } \quad \dfrac{1}{t_{o}^{2}}=\dfrac{1}{t_{1}^{2}}+\dfrac{1}{t_{2}^{2}}\end{aligned}$

question: In figures $S_{1}$ and $S_{2}$ are identical springs. the oscillation frequency of the mass $m$ is $f$. If one spring is removed, the frequency will become
explanation:
Effective spring constant ${K_{eff{\rm{ }}}} = 2K$.
$f=\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{2 K}{m}}$
When one spring is removed, $K_{\text {eff }}$ becomes $K$ new frequency
$\begin{aligned}& f^{\prime}=\dfrac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{m}} \\& \dfrac{f^{\prime}}{f}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \Rightarrow f^{\prime}=\dfrac{f}{\sqrt{2}}\end{aligned}$

question: An object is attached to the bottom of a light vertical spring and set vibrating. The maximum speed of the object is $15\,cm/\sec $ and the time $\frac{\pi }{5}\,\sec $, find its amplitude

question: Simple pendulum is executing simple harmonic motion with time period $T$. If the length of the pendulum is increased by $21 \%$, then percentage increase in the time period of the pendulum is:
explanation:
The time period of a simple pendulum of a length $l$ is given by
$T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{l}{g}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Where $g$ is acceleration due to gravity.
Where length of pendulum increases by $21 \%$
We have $l^{\prime}=1.21 l$
$\therefore {T^\prime } = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{{1.21l}}{g}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Dividing Eq. (1) by Eq. (2), we get
$\dfrac{T}{T^{\prime}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{1.21}} \Rightarrow T^{\prime}=1.1 T$
Hence, increase in time period
$1.1 T-T=0.1 T$
Percent increase in time period
$=\dfrac{0.1 T}{T} \times 100=10 \%$

question: A simple pendulum of length $\ell$ is making oscillations with its bob immersed in a liquid of density $n$ times less than the density of the bob. What is its period?
explanation:
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g^{\prime}}}$
$g^{\prime}$ is the effective acceleration due to gravity Inside the liquid the effective weight is
$\begin{aligned}& m g^{\prime}=m g-F_{b}=\rho_{s} V g-\rho_{l} V g \\& \rho_{s} V g^{\prime}=\rho_{s} V g-\dfrac{\rho_{s}}{n} V g \\& g^{\prime}=g\left(1-\dfrac{1}{n}\right) \\& \Rightarrow T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g\left(1-\dfrac{1}{n}\right)}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A particle is executing two different simple harmonic motions, mutually perpendicular, of different amplitudes and having phase difference of $\pi / 2$. The path of the particle will be
explanation:
If the equations of two mutually perpendicular $S H M$ of same frequency be
$x=a_{1} \sin \omega t$ and $y=a_{2} \sin (\omega t+\phi)$
General equation of Lissajous' figure is
$\dfrac{x^{2}}{a_{1}^{2}}+\dfrac{y^{2}}{a_{2}^{2}}-\dfrac{2 x y}{a_{1} a_{2}} \cos \phi=\sin ^{2} \phi$.
When $\phi=\dfrac{\pi}{2}$, then $\dfrac{x^{2}}{a_{1}^{2}}+\dfrac{y^{2}}{a_{2}^{2}}=1$
which represents the path of ellipse.

question: For a periodic motion represented by the equation $y=\sin \omega t+\cos \omega t$, the amplitude of the motion is
explanation:

$=\sqrt{2}\left(\sin \omega t \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+\cos \omega t \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$
$\Rightarrow y=\sqrt{2} \sin (\omega t+\pi / 4)$.
The above equation represents on SHM with initial phase $\pi / 4$ and amplitude $\sqrt{2}$.

question:
Consider two SHMs along the same straight line \(x_{1}=A_{1} \sin \left(\omega t+\phi_{1}\right), x_{2}=A_{2} \sin \left(\omega t+\phi_{2}\right)\), where \(A_{1}\) and \(A_{2}\) are their amplitudes and \(\phi_{1}\) and \(\phi_{2}\) are their initial phase angle. If the two SHMs meet simultaneously and ' \(R\) ' is the resultant amplitude, match column I with column II.
A. The two $SHMs$ are in phase, ${A_1} = {A_2} = A$
B. The two $SHMs$ are in phase, ${A_1} \ne {A_2}$
C. The two $SHMs$ are $90^\circ $ out of phase, ${A_1} = {A_2} = A$
D. The two $SHMs$ are $180^\circ $ out of phase, ${A_1} = {A_2}$
P. $R = {A_1} + {A_2}$
Q. $R = 0$
R. $R = 2A$
S. $R = \sqrt 2 A$
explanation:
$x_{1}=A_{1} \sin \left(\omega t+\phi_{1}\right)$
$x_{2}=A_{2} \sin \left(\omega t+\phi_{2}\right)$
where $A_{1}, A_{2}$ are amplitudes of two SHM's and $\phi_{1}, \phi_{2}$ are phase angles.
Resultant amplitude,
$R = \sqrt {A_1^2 + A_2^2 + 2{A_1}{A_2}\cos \left( {{\phi _1} - {\phi _2}} \right)} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
(i) When two SHM's are in phase and, $A_{1}=A_{2}=A$, then from equation (1) $\Rightarrow R=\sqrt{A^{2}+A^{2}+2 A^{2}}=2 A$
(ii) When two SHM's are in phase but $A_{1} \neq A_{2}$, then
$R=\sqrt{A_{1}{ }^{2}+A_{2}{ }^{2}+2 A_{1} A_{2}}=\sqrt{\left(A_{1}+A_{2}\right)^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow R=A_{1}+A_{2}$
(iii) When two $\mathrm{SHMs}$ are $90^{\circ}$ out of phase, then
$\Rightarrow R=\sqrt{A^{2}+A^{2}+0}=\sqrt{2} A$
(iv) When two SHMs are $180^{\circ}$ out of phase and $A_{1}=A_{2}$,
$R=\sqrt{A_{1}^{2}+A_{1}^{2}-2 A_{1}^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow R=0$
Hence option (1) is correct answer.
subject: Physics
topic: Super Position of SHM’s

question: $x$ and $y$ displacements of a particle are given as $x = a\sin \omega t\,y(t) = a\sin 2\omega t$. Its trajectory will look like
explanation:
$x=a \sin \omega t \& y=a \sin 2 \omega t$
$\begin{aligned}& y=2 a \sin \omega t \cos \omega t \\& y=2 x \sqrt{1-\dfrac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}} \\& y=\dfrac{2}{a} x \sqrt{(a-x)(a+x)}\end{aligned}$
When $y=0$
$\begin{aligned}& x=0 \&(a-x)(a+x)=0 \\& \Rightarrow x= \pm a\end{aligned}$
so the correct graph is (2)

question:
A particle which is simultaneously subjected to two perpendicular simple harmonic motions
represented by: \(x=a_{1} \cos \omega t\) and \(y=a_{2} \cos 2 \omega t\) traces a curve given by :
explanation:
$\begin{aligned}y & =a_{2}\left[2 \cos ^{2} \omega t-1\right] \\& =a_{2}\left[2 \cdot \dfrac{x^{2}}{a_{1}^{2}}-1\right] \\y & =\dfrac{2 a_{2}}{a_{1}^{2}} x^{2}-a_{2}\end{aligned}$
at$x = 0,y$ is negative and this is an equation of parabola. Hence answer is (4)

question: Two SHMs $r_{1}=A \sin \omega t$ and $r_{2}=B \sin \omega t$ are superposed on a particle. $r_{1}$ and $r_{2}$ are along the directions which make $37^{0}$ with each other. Choose the correct statement
explanation:
https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2023_09_27_1a60125c58390c5c7f6bg-28.jpg?height=428&width=504&top_left_y=672&top_left_x=1212 Along $X$-axis $x=r_{1}+r_{2} \cos 37^{\circ}$
or $\quad x=A \sin \omega t+B \sin \omega t \cos 37^{\circ}$
$=A \sin \omega t+\dfrac{4}{5} B \sin \omega t$
This is the sum of two SHMs happenings on $X$-axis with phase difference $\phi=0$. So, for this
${A_R} = \sqrt {{{(A)}^2} + {{\left( {\frac{4}{5}B} \right)}^2} + 2 \cdot A \cdot \frac{4}{5}B\cos \,{0^o}} $
$x = \sqrt {{A^2} + \frac{{16}}{{25}}{B^2} + \frac{8}{5}AB} \sin \omega t$
$x = {\left( {A + \frac{4}{5}B} \right)^2}\sin \omega t\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Along Y-axis $y=r_{2} \sin 37=(B \sin \omega t) \dfrac{3}{5}=\dfrac{3}{5} B \sin \omega t$
or $\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} y = \frac{3}{5}B\sin \omega t\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
SHMs given by (1) and (2) are perpendicular with phase difference zero. So options (1) is correct

options.text: The path of the particle is a circle
The particle will perform only periodic motion but not SHM
The path is a closed curve but not a straight line

question: The displacement $x$ of a particle executing a certain periodic motion is given by $y=25 \sin (4 t) \cos ^{2}(5 t)$. This expression may be considered to be the superposition of $n$ independent harmonic motions. What is the value of $n$ ?
explanation:
$y=25 \sin 4 t\left[\dfrac{1+\cos 10 t}{2}\right]$
$=\dfrac{25}{2} \sin 4 t+\dfrac{25}{2} \sin 4 t \cos 10 t$
or $y=\dfrac{25}{2} \sin 4 t+\dfrac{25}{4}[\sin (14 t)-\sin (6 t)]$
or $y=\dfrac{25}{2} \sin 4 t+\dfrac{25}{4} \sin 14 t-\dfrac{25}{4} \sin 6 t$
or $y=\dfrac{25}{2} \sin 4 t+\dfrac{25}{4} \sin 14 t-\dfrac{25}{4} \sin (6 t)$
So it is superposition of 3 SHMs. Thus $n=3$
subject: Physics

question: The displacement of the particle varies with time according to the relation. $y=a \sin \omega t+b \cos \omega t$, then
explanation:
Given, $y=a \sin \omega t+b \cos \omega t$
Let $a = A\cos \theta \,\,{\rm{and}}\,\,b = A\sin \theta \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
then $y=A \cos \theta \sin \omega t+A \sin \theta \cos \omega t$
$y=A \sin (\omega t+\theta)$
Which is in the form of SHM
From Eq. (1)
${a^2} + {b^2} = {A^2}{\cos ^2}\theta + {A^2}{\sin ^2}\theta $
$ \Rightarrow A = \sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2}} $

options.text: The motion is oscillating but not SHM
The motion is SHM with amplitude $a + b$
The motion is SHM with amplitude $a^{2}+b^{2}$
The motion is SHM with amplitude $\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}}$

question: The displacement equation of the SHM obtained by combining the motions $x_{1}=2 \sin \omega t$, $x_{2}=4 \sin \left(\omega t+\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right)$ and $x_{3}=6 \sin \left(\omega t+\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)$ is.
explanation:
$x=x_{1}+x_{2}+x_{3}$
$\begin{aligned}=2 & \sin \omega t+2 \sqrt{3} \sin \omega t+2 \cos \omega t+3 \sin \omega t \\& +3 \sqrt{3} \cos \omega t \\& =(5+2 \sqrt{3}) \sin \omega t+(2+3 \sqrt{3}) \cos \omega t \\\therefore \quad & x=A \sin (\omega t+\phi)\end{aligned}$
Where $A=\sqrt{68+32 \sqrt{3}}$ and $\phi=\tan ^{-1}\left(\dfrac{2+3 \sqrt{3}}{5+2 \sqrt{3}}\right)$
$x = 11.1\sin (\omega t + 31.54)^\circ $

question: The resultant amplitude due to superposition of three simple harmonic motions ${y_1} = 3\sin \omega t{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {y_2} = 5\sin \omega t + {37^{\rm{o}}}$ and ${y_3} =  - 15\cos \omega t$ is ____.
explanation:
$y_{3}=-15 \cos \omega t=-15 \sin \left(\dfrac{\pi}{2}+\omega t\right)$
$=15 \sin \left(\omega t+\dfrac{3 \pi}{2}\right)=15 \sin \left(\omega t+270^{\circ}\right)$
$\therefore$ Vector diagram of amplitudes is: https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956080/preptracker/questions/q_39404_explanation_1764956080397.jpg $\Sigma A_{x}=3+5 \cos 37^{\circ}=7$
$\Sigma A_{y}=-15+5 \sin 37^{\circ}=-12$
$\therefore A=\sqrt{\left(\Sigma A_{x}\right)^{2}+\left(\Sigma A_{y}\right)^{2}}=\sqrt{7^{2}+12^{2}}=\sqrt{193}$ units

question: Three simple harmonic motions, of equal amplitudes $A$ and equal time periods, along the same line combine. The phase of the second motion is $60^{\circ}$ ahead of the first and phase of the third motion is $60^{\circ}$ ahead of the second. The amplitude of resultant motion is ____.
explanation:
vector diagram of amplitudes is given below. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958698/preptracker/questions/q_39401_explanation_1764958697614.jpg By treating the SHMs as vector
$\bar{A}_{1}=A \hat{i} ; \bar{A}_{2}=\dfrac{A}{2} \hat{i}+\dfrac{\sqrt{3} A}{2} \hat{j} ; \bar{A}_{3}=-\dfrac{A}{2} \hat{i}+\dfrac{\sqrt{3} A}{2} \hat{j}$
The resultant amplitude is $\bar{A}_{R}=A \hat{i}+\sqrt{3} A \hat{j}$
$A_{R}=2 A$
